chapters.text:
1               Advent of Europeans in India

The advent of Europeans in India began with Vasco da Gama’s arrival in 1498, establishing a direct sea route that opened India
to European trade and colonial ambitions. This marked the start of a transformative era of economic exchanges, political conflicts,
and cultural interactions that reshaped India’s history and later laid the foundation for British colonial rule.

1. Background and Context
     Aspect                                                         Description
 Motivation for      y European powers sought direct sea routes to India due to disruptions in traditional trade routes (fall
 exploration           of Constantinople in 1453) and the lucrative spice trade.
                     y “The wealth of nations lies in controlling trade routes." → This mercantilist principle was reflected in
                       the Treaty of Tordesillas (1494), and zeal for exploration also motivated Vasco da Gama’s 1498 voyage.
 Technological       y Advances in shipbuilding, navigation, and maritime techniques enabled long-distance voyages.
 advancements        y Portuguese multi-decked ships, designed to withstand Atlantic gales, carried heavier armaments, giving
                       them naval superiority.
 Renaissance         y The Renaissance fuelled a desire for exploration and wealth accumulation, driving European powers
 spirit                to Asia.
                     y The Portuguese Crown, under D. Manuel (1495-1521), aimed to monopolise the spice trade for
                       economic enrichment.

2. Arrival and Establishment of European Powers
  European Power                                              Key Events and Timeline
 Portuguese (1498)        y Arrival: Vasco da Gama arrived at Calicut in 1498, opening the direct sea route to India, marking
                            the first European presence.
                          y Naval Monopoly: Established naval dominance in the Indian Ocean spice trade through the
                            aggressive Cartaz System (mandatory naval trade licences) , Blue Water Policy and the Cafila
                            System (armed merchant convoys).
                          y Consolidation: Governor Alfonso de Albuquerque captured Goa in 1510 from the Sultan of
                            Bijapur, which became the permanent capital of Portuguese India by 1530.
                          y Decline: Their naval hegemony was shattered by the English East India Company in the Battle of
                            Swally (1612) off the coast of Surat.


 Dutch (1602)             y Establishment: The Dutch East India Company was formed in 1602 with a primary focus on the
                            spice-rich Indonesian archipelago.
                          y Established their first factory at Masulipatnam (1605), followed by Pulicat (1610).
                          y Fort Geldria (1613) at Pulicat served as their principal base for Coromandel Coast operations.
                          y Decline: Completely crushed by King Marthanda Varma of the Travancore Kingdom at the Battle
                            of Colachel (1741), ending Dutch political ambitions in India.


English (1600)         y Inception: Formed via a Royal Charter in 1600. Opened an interim post at Masulipatnam (1611)
                         and secured Mughal permission for their first permanent factory at Surat (1613).
                       y Territorial Shifts: Acquired Bombay in 1662 as a royal dowry for King Charles II, shifting their
                         western headquarters from Surat to Bombay in 1687.
                       y Political Ascendancy: Transformed from a commercial enterprise to a political sovereign
                         following historic victories in the Battle of Plassey (1757) and the Battle of Buxar (1764).
French (1664)          y The Compagnie des Indes Orientales was formed in 1664 under state patronage (King Louis
                         XIV).
                       y Established their premier settlement at Pondicherry (1674), which was meticulously developed
                         into a major commercial hub by Francois Martin.
                       y Decline: Locked into a bitter geopolitical duel with the British through the three Carnatic Wars
                         (1746-1763). The crushing French defeat at the Battle of Wandiwash (1760) permanently
                         restricted them to tiny commercial enclaves.
Danes (1616)           y The Danish East India Company entered in 1616, setting up factories at Tranquebar (1620) in
                         Tamil Nadu and trading colony in Serampore (1755) in Bengal (which also became a major center
                         for missionary activities).
                       y Exit: Maintained a policy of strict commercial neutrality. Due to financial unviability, they sold all
                         their Indian assets to the British East India Company in 1845.


3. Objectives and Strategies
   Objective                   Strategies Employed                                           Examples
Trade monopoly       European powers aimed to control the spice,        The Portuguese used the Cartaz system to regulate
                     textile, and other lucrative trades through        Asian shipping, while the Dutch enforced monopoly
                     naval supremacy and monopolistic practices.        rights in the Spice Islands.
Fortifications and   Establishment of fortified trading posts and       “Control the seas, control the trade” - Portuguese
naval control        naval fleets to secure trade routes.               forts at Cochin, Cannanore, and Diu; Dutch Fort
                                                                        Geldria at Pulicat; French Fort Louis at Pondicherry.
Diplomacy and        Collaborating with local rulers to gain trade      The Raja of Cochin allied with the Portuguese
local alliances      privileges or territorial concessions.             against the Zamorin of Calicut; the Dutch aided
                                                                        Cochin to expel the Portuguese in 1663.
Territorial          Transition from commercial to territorial          Dupleix’s attempts to establish a territorial empire in
ambitions            control, especially by the French.                 the Deccan, supporting Muzaffar Jang in 1749.
(French)


4. Administrative and Commercial Structures
 European                                      Administrative and Commercial Structures
  Power
Portuguese           y Centralised system under Estado da India, headed by a Viceroy based in Goa.
                     y Supported by municipal councils and religious authorities like the Archbishop and chief inquisitor.
                     y Commercial operations controlled by Casa da India with use of Cartaz (naval pass) and cafila
                       (convoy) systems.
                     y Royal monopoly over pepper trade until 1564.
                     y Example: Goa became the political and commercial centre; the Portuguese used forced naval control to
                       dominate the spice trade.
Dutch                y Governor-General at Batavia (Java) supervised overall operations; local councils managed Indian
                       factories (like Pulicat and Hooghly).
                     y Focused on intra-Asian trade → exported Indian textiles, silk, and opium in exchange for Southeast
                       Asian spices.
                     y Focused mainly on profitable intra-Asian trade and the Indonesian spice trade, with comparatively
                       limited territorial ambitions in India.
                     y Example: Dutch minted gold pagodas at Nagapattinam (1658); Batavia served as a trade coordination
                       hub.
French               y Centralised administration under governors like Dupleix, with headquarters at Pondicherry.
                     y Relied on Indian brokers and merchant communities for credit and logistics.
                     y Engaged in the export of cotton, muslin, and saltpetre; imported bullion to cover trade deficits.
                     y Example: Fort Louis (1700-1707) was built in Pondicherry; Indian Chettis and Mudaliars financed
                       French operations.
English              y Administrative structure based on presidencies with governors (like Fort William in Calcutta); overall
                       coordination by Court of Directors in London.
                     y Initially focused on textile and spice trade, later expanded into political control and revenue
                       collection.
                     y Developed fortified settlements which became administrative centres.
                     y Example: Fort William (1700) emerged as the eastern headquarters; English trade paved the way for
                       colonial expansion post-Battle of Plassey.

5. Impact on Indian Society and Economy
 Impact Area                                 Details                                            Examples
Economic               European trade brought bullion, boosting India’s        Dutch and English exemptions from transit
growth                 monetary system and market exchanges.                   duties increased trade efficiency; bullion
                                                                               imports facilitated land revenue commutation.
Urbanization           Economic gravity shifted from traditional inland        Pondicherry grew to a population of ~120,000
                       Mughal administrative centers (like Agra, Delhi) to     by 1741; Pulicat was fortified with Fort
                       the coastal presidency towns of Madras, Bombay,         Geldria.
                       and Calcutta → growth of port cities as autonomous
                       power centers.
Agricultural           Introduction of new crops from the Americas,            Portuguese introduced chillies, potatoes, and
transformation         reshaping Indian agriculture and cuisine (such as       pineapples, integrating them into Indian
                       chillies replacing pepper).                             farming practices.
Military               Introduction of disciplined infantry, muskets, and      French commanders like Raymond and De
innovations            fortifications by the French, adopted by Indian         Boigne reorganised armies for Mysore and
                       rulers.                                                 Sindhia, respectively.
Cultural and           European religious policies (like the Portuguese        Portuguese conversion efforts in Goa led to
religious tensions     Inquisition) caused resentment among locals.            Hindu discontent; the British were
                                                                               comparatively tolerant till 1813, post which
                                                                               missionaries entered India.


6. Causes of Decline of European Powers
    European                              Reasons                                                 Examples
     Power
 Portuguese            Loss of local advantages, opposition from            Marathas captured Salsette and Bassein in 1739;
 decline               powerful dynasties (like Marathas), and              Portuguese failed to control Aden, acquired by
                       religious intolerance.                               Ottomans in 1538.
 Dutch decline         Inability to sustain monopolies due to               Indian ships evaded Dutch control via Acheh; the
                       competition and limited territorial                  focus shifted to Java and the Spice Islands.
                       ambitions in India.
 French decline        Financial constraints, European wars, and            Dupleix’s recall in 1754 and the Treaty of Paris (1763)
                       English opposition thwarted territorial              ended French dominance in India.
                       ambitions.
The advent of Europeans in India initiated profound changes in trade, politics, and society, ultimately leading to colonial domination.
This tumultuous period of dominance not only integrated India into the global economy but also systematically re-engineered its
internal socioeconomic and political structures, laying the essential groundwork for India’s modern history.

2             Battles of Plassey and Buxar

2.1 Battle of Plassey (1757)
The Battle of Plassey, fought on June 23, 1757, was a decisive conflict between the British East India Company, led by Robert
Clive, and the Nawab of Bengal, Siraj-ud-Daulah. Marking the beginning of British political dominance in India, this victory
was secured through strategic alliances and the betrayal of Mir Jafar, fundamentally altering the subcontinent’s political and
economic landscape.

1. Causes of the Battle of Plassey
          Cause                                                           Details
 Misuse of trade              The EIC’s duty-free trade privileges (dastaks), granted by the Mughal emperor, were misused
 privileges                   for private trade, causing significant revenue losses for Bengal’s treasury, which fuelled
                              resentment among local rulers.
 Unauthorized                 The EIC fortified Fort William in Calcutta without the Nawab’s permission, escalating tensions
 fortification of Calcutta    as Siraj-ud-Daulah viewed it as a challenge to his authority.
 Black hole tragedy           The alleged imprisonment of ~150 Englishmen in a small room by Siraj, resulting in ~120 deaths
                              due to suffocation, provoked British retaliation, though the incident’s details remain debated.
 Anglo-French rivalry         During the Seven Years' War (1756–63), the English feared that Siraj-ud-Daulah might support
                              the French or restrict British military activities in Bengal.
 Conspiracy during the        Rivalries involving Mir Jafar, Jagat Seth, and Ghasiti Begum led to betrayal during the battle,
 battle                       which ultimately weakened Siraj’s position.

2. Course of the Battle
    Phase                                                           Details
 Pre-battle        Robert Clive secured a secret alliance with Mir Jafar, Jagat Seth, and others, promising Mir Jafar the
 strategy          Nawabship in exchange for betraying Siraj, ensuring the battle’s outcome was predetermined.
 Battle            On June 23, 1757, Clive’s 3,000 troops faced Siraj’s 50,000-strong army. Mir Jafar’s inaction during the
                   battle allowed the EIC to secure a swift victory, highlighting conspiracy over military prowess.
 Post-battle       Siraj was captured and executed under orders from Mir Jafar’s son, Miran. Mir Jafar was installed as
 outcome           Nawab, granting the EIC access to Bengal’s vast resources.

3. Outcomes and Long-Term Consequences of the Battle of Plassey
      Aspect                                                           Details
 British victory       y The English EIC, led by Robert Clive, defeated Nawab Siraj-ud-Daulah’s forces on 23 June 1757.
 and expansion         y The Battle laid the groundwork for British colonial rule, enabling further conquests like the Battle
                         of Buxar (1764), which granted the EIC diwani rights over Bengal, Bihar, and Orissa, solidifying
                         their territorial control.
                       y The EIC were granted the Zamindari of the 24 Parganas, marking their transition into territorial
                         power.
                       y Though indirect, the EIC began asserting control over administration, revenue, and military
                         matters in Bengal.
 Puppet Nawab          y Mir Jafar was made Nawab of Bengal as a reward for betraying Siraj-ud-Daula; he granted
 and Political           commercial privileges and large sums to the Company.
 instability           y Mir Jafar’s puppet rule led to his deposition in 1760 due to failure to meet EIC demands. His
                         successor, Mir Qasim, ceded territories like Burdwan, Midnapur, and Chittagong, further weakening
                         indigenous authority.


 Loot and               y EIC and its officers received massive payments from the Bengal treasury, enriching "nabobs"
 financial gains          (returning officials).
                        y The EIC’s control over Bengal’s resources led to significant wealth transfer to Britain. This wealth
                          fuelled Britain’s Industrial Revolution, impoverishing Bengal’s economy.
 Decline of             y The EIC’s trade monopoly marginalised local merchants and artisans, disrupting Bengal’s
 indigenous trade         commercial landscape and paving the way for British manufactured goods to dominate.
 Precursor to           y The socio-economic disruptions caused by Plassey’s outcomes, such as peasant exploitation and
 revolt                   loss of local autonomy, sowed seeds of discontent, contributing to later uprisings like the Revolt of
                          1857.

4. Significance of the Battle of Plassey
       Aspect                                                            Details
 Political control       The battle established British political supremacy in Bengal, marking the start of colonial rule. The
                         Nawab became a puppet, dependent on the EIC, which began interfering in administrative
                         appointments.
 Economic                The EIC monopolised Bengal’s trade, sidelining French and Dutch competitors. This control over a
 dominance               region contributing 60% of British imports from Asia boosted the EIC’s economic power.
 Administrative          Established the British as the kingmakers, allowing them to install puppet Nawabs (starting with Mir
 shift                   Jafar) and granting them Zamindari rights (not full Diwani).
 Exposure of             The battle revealed deep divisions within Bengal’s administration, as betrayals by Mir Jafar, Jagat
 internal rivalries      Seth, and others weakened indigenous rule, facilitating British dominance.
 Strategic               Plassey set a precedent for British use of diplomacy and alliances to conquer Indian territories, a
 precedent               tactic later refined through policies like the Subsidiary Alliance.
 Social disruption       The battle destabilised Bengal’s social structure by empowering zamindars and intermediaries, who
                         collaborated with the British, often at the expense of peasants.
K.M. Panikkar described the Battle of Plassey not as a battle but as a "transaction in which the rich bankers of Bengal and Mir Jafar
sold out the Nawab to the English. " The Battle of Plassey was a turning point that established British supremacy in India, paving
the way for nearly two centuries of colonial rule. Its outcome reshaped India’s political structure and set the foundation for the
expansion of British power across the subcontinent.

2.2 Battle of Buxar
The Battle of Buxar, fought on October 22, 1764, was a decisive conflict between the British East India Company, led by Major
Hector Munro, and a coalition of Indian rulers, including Mir Qasim (Nawab of Bengal), Shuja-ud-Daula (Nawab of Awadh),
and Mughal Emperor Shah Alam II. This battle marked a crucial turning point, solidifying British dominance in northern India
and reshaping the subcontinent’s political landscape.

1. Causes of the Battle of Buxar
          Cause                                                             Details
 Mir Qasim’s assertion         Mir Qasim, appointed Nawab in 1760, aimed to establish independent authority by reorganising
 of authority                  his administration and army. His demand for revenue accounts from Ram Narayan, Bihar’s
                               deputy governor, who was supported by British officials in Patna, heightened tensions.
 Misuse of trade               The EIC officials misused duty-free trade permits (dastaks), extending them to private trade
 privileges (Dastaks)          and selling them to Indian merchants, causing significant revenue losses for Mir Qasim and
                               unfair competition for local traders.
 Abolition of duties           To counter the misuse of dastaks, Mir Qasim abolished all transit duties and placed Indian
                               merchants on an equal footing with the EIC, but the EIC protested, demanding preferential
                               treatment. This dispute escalated into armed conflicts in 1763, setting the stage for Buxar.
 British interference in       The EIC’s growing interference in the Nawab’s court, including their support for rival
 Bengal politics               factions and pressure for territorial concessions, alienated Mir Qasim, who sought to resist
                               British dominance.
 Formation of anti-            Mir Qasim, after fleeing to Awadh post-1763 defeats, allied with Shuja-ud-Daulah and Shah
 British alliance              Alam II to challenge the EIC, aiming to regain control over Bengal and curb British expansion.


2. Course of the Battle

          Phase                                                            Details
 Pre-battle                 After losses in 1763 at Katwah, Murshidabad, Giria, Sooty, and Munger, Mir Qasim sought
 developments               refuge in Awadh, forming an alliance with Shuja-ud-Daulah and Shah Alam II to counter the
                            EIC’s growing power.
 Battle                     On October 22, 1764, the EIC’s disciplined force of approximately 7,000 troops, led by Hector
                            Munro, faced a larger confederate army of over 40,000. The battle was closely contested, but
                            superior British military tactics and artillery secured a decisive victory.
 Post-battle outcome        Mir Qasim fled, losing his throne permanently. Shah Alam II and Shuja-ud-Daulah
                            surrendered, leading to the Treaty of Allahabad (1765), which formalised British control over
                            eastern India.

3. Significance of the Battle of Buxar
           Aspect                                                             Details
 Establishment of British         The EIC’s victory over the combined forces of Bengal, Awadh, and the Mughal Emperor
 supremacy                        established them as the dominant political power in northern India, paving the way for
                                  further territorial expansion.
 Weakening of Indian              The defeat of Mir Qasim, Shuja-ud-Daulah, and Shah Alam II exposed the military and
 powers                           political vulnerabilities of Indian rulers, reducing their ability to resist British advances.
 Legalization of British          The Treaty of Allahabad (1765) granted the EIC diwani rights (revenue collection) over
 gains                            Bengal, Bihar, and Orissa, legitimising their financial control through a Mughal farman,
                                  while Awadh became a buffer state.
 Introduction of dual             The battle led to the dual system of governance (1765), where the EIC controlled revenue
 government                       (Diwani) while the Nawab handled administration (Nizamat), giving the Company real power
                                  behind nominal native rule.
 Strategic confidence             The victory bolstered British confidence in their military superiority, encouraging further
                                  conquests and reinforcing their belief in the inevitability of British rule across India.
 Economic monopoly                The EIC strengthened its trade monopoly in Bengal, marginalizing local merchants and
                                  European rivals like the French and Dutch, enhancing its economic dominance.

4. Impacts and Long-Term Consequences
     Impact                                                              Details
 Consolidation of       The EIC’s acquisition of diwani rights through the Treaty of Allahabad (1765) formalised their
 British rule           control over Bengal, Bihar, and Orissa, laying the foundation for British colonial rule across India.
 Economic               The EIC’s revenue control led to excessive tax demands, ruining agriculture and trade. The misuse
 exploitation           of dastaks destroyed local industries like silk and cotton, contributing to economic decline and
                        famines (such as the Great Bengal Famine of 1770).
 Peasant distress       High revenue demands under the dual government forced peasants to abandon lands, turning many
                        into bandits. The lack of administrative focus on welfare exacerbated rural impoverishment.
 Political              The EIC reinstated Mir Jafar as Nawab, who ceded Midnapore, Burdwan, and Chittagong. Later,
 realignments           puppet rulers like Najim-ud-daula were appointed, with real power held by British-appointed
                        deputy subahdars.
 Abolition of dual      The dual system’s failure, marked by administrative breakdowns and economic ruin, led Warren
 government             Hastings to abolish it in 1772, with the EIC assuming direct control over diwani and nizamat
                        functions.
 Decline of Mughal      Shah Alam II’s defeat and dependence on the EIC reduced the Mughal Emperor to a figurehead,
 authority              legalising British gains while diminishing Mughal sovereignty.
The Battle of Buxar firmly established British political and economic control over Bengal, Bihar, and Odisha, laying the
foundation for nearly two centuries of colonial rule. This victory exposed the declining power of the Mughal Empire and
regional rulers, enabling the British East India Company to expand its influence and revenue rights, which profoundly
impacted India’s subsequent history.

chapters.t: Expansion and Consolidation of British Power in India
chapters.text:
3               Expansion and Consolidation
                        of British Power in India
Evolution of British Expansionary Policies
The territorial growth of the British Empire was a calculated, evolutionary progression rather than a series of accidents. British
expansion evolved from defensive consolidation to aggressive annexation and finally to indirect imperial control. Historian Sir
William Lee-Warner observed a shift from seeking security as "equals" to the assertion of absolute imperial paramountcy over
the subcontinent.

                                             Policy of Ring Fence (1765-1813)
 Context           Spearheaded by Warren Hastings, during a phase when the EIC faced existential threats from the
                   Marathas and Mysore while still consolidating power.
 Mechanism         Focused on creating buffer states to protect British borders. Awadh acted as a shock-absorber against
                   Maratha and Afghan incursions into Bengal.
 Nature            Maintained a fiction of equality; states paid for British military aid while retaining internal administrative
                   autonomy.

                                            Subsidiary Alliance (1798-1805)
 Context           Driven by Lord Wellesley’s aggressive imperialism and fears of Napoleonic French influence destabilising
                   British interests.
 Mechanism         Rulers surrendered external sovereignty, hosted a British Resident, and maintained a permanent garrison,
                   effectively becoming militarily subservient to the EIC.
 Impact            Served as a financial trap; debt forced territorial cessions in Awadh and Hyderabad, leading to the
                   systematic disarmament of native powers.

                                      Policy of Subordinate Isolation (1813-1857)
 Context           Initiated by Lord Hastings, the Company abandoned all pretence of equality, demanding "subordinate
                   cooperation" from Indian states.
 Mechanism         British residents increasingly interfered in succession, diplomacy and internal affairs, reducing the practical
                   sovereignty of princely states to ensure EIC dominance.
 Doctrine          Lord Dalhousie annexed states like Satara, Jhansi, and Nagpur by denying the right of adoption. Awadh
 of Lapse          was annexed in 1856, citing "maladministration", provoking massive resentment.
 (1848-1856)

                                       Policy of Subordinate Union (1858-1935)
 Context           Post-1857, the Crown shifted strategy; princes were now seen as "breakwaters to the storm" (Lord
                   Canning), and the policy of annexation was permanently halted.
 Mechanism         The Queen’s Proclamation (1858) guaranteed territorial integrity and the right to adoption in exchange for
                   the princes acknowledging the Crown as suzerain.
 Impact            States became integrated props of the colonial regime; their forces were reorganised as Imperial Service
                   Troops for British use, ensuring loyal collaboration until independence. The policy culminated in the
                   establishment of the Chamber of Princes (1921).

3.1 Carnatic Wars
The Carnatic Wars (1746-1763) were a series of Anglo-French conflicts in South India, fuelled by European rivalries
and local power struggles. These wars marked the British East India Company's rise and the decline of French
influence in the subcontinent.


1. First Carnatic War (1746-1748)

   Aspect                                                      Details
Belligerents   French East India Company (led by Dupleix and La Bourdonnais) vs British East India Company
               (EIC, led by Governor Morse), with the Nawab of Carnatic (Anwar-ud-din) as a minor player.
Background     The First Carnatic War (1746-1748) erupted against the backdrop of the European War of the Austrian
               Succession, which set Britain and France at odds. Tensions in India escalated when the British East India
               Company seized French ships off the southeastern coast, provoking retaliation from French Governor
               Dupleix, despite his initial efforts to maintain peace.
Key events     y British Naval Provocation (1746): A British squadron under Commodore Barnett captured French
                 vessels carrying Indian goods, ignoring Dupleix’s neutrality plea to Governor Morse of Madras,
                 sparking hostilities.
               y French Capture of Madras (September 1746): La Bourdonnais arrived with eight warships from
                 Mauritius, outmatching the EIC’s four ships under Peyton, who retreated to Hoogly. Around September
                 15, 1746, French forces captured undefended Madras, compelling Governor Morse to surrender after
                 failing to secure Nawab Anwar-ud-din’s aid.
               y Battle of Adyar/San Thome (October 1746): Nawab Anwar-ud-din sent 10,000 troops under Mahfuz
                 Khan to blockade Fort St. George, demanding the French honor their promise to hand over Madras.
                 French forces, reinforced, repelled the Nawab’s army near the Adyar River, showcasing European
                 military superiority over larger Indian forces.
               y Siege of Fort St. David (1746-1748): Dupleix targeted Fort St. David in Cuddalore, but the EIC,
                 bolstered by naval support, withstood an 18-month siege. La Bourdonnais’ defiance of Dupleix,
                 demanding ransom for Madras, weakened French coordination.
Treaty or      Treaty of Aix-la-Chapelle (1748): Ended hostilities, with France returning Madras to the EIC in
Outcome        exchange for Louisburg in North America. French military superiority was demonstrated at Adyar, but
               internal disputes limited their gains.

2. Second Carnatic War (1749-1754)

   Aspect                                                      Details
Belligerents   French East India Company (led by Dupleix, Bussy) with Chanda Sahib and Muzaffar Jang vs. British
               East India Company (led by Robert Clive, Governor Saunders) with Muhammad Ali and Nasir Jang.
Background     The Second Carnatic War (1749-1754) stemmed from succession crises in the Carnatic and Hyderabad.
               Dupleix backed Chanda Sahib and Muzaffar Jang, while the British supported Muhammad Ali and
               Nasir Jang, turning local conflicts into a proxy war for dominance.
Key events     y Battle of Ambur (August 1749): Dupleix formed a triple alliance with Chanda Sahib and Muzaffar
                 Jang, ambushing and killing Nawab Anwar-ud-din at Ambur, southeast of Vellore. Chanda Sahib
                 became Nawab of the Carnatic, while Muhammad Ali fled to Trichinopoly.
               y French Gains in Hyderabad (1750): After Nasir Jang’s death in 1750, Muzaffar Jang was installed
                 as Nizam, rewarding Dupleix with the governorship of Mughal territories south of the Krishna River and
                 ceding territories near Pondicherry. Bussy’s French troops secured French influence in Hyderabad.
               y Clive’s Siege of Arcot (August-November 1751): Robert Clive, under Governor Saunders, captured
                 Arcot with 200 European and 300 Indian troops to divert Chanda Sahib’s forces from Trichinopoly.
                    ♦ Despite a 53-day siege by Raja Sahib’s 3,000-strong force, supported by French troops, Clive held
                       Arcot, boosting EIC prestige.
                    ♦ Siege of Arcot is often regarded as the turning point of the Second Carnatic War and made Robert
                       Clive a major British military figure.
               y Fall of Trichinopoly (1752): French general Jacques-Francois Law abandoned the Trichinopoly siege,
                 fleeing to Srirangam, where he surrendered on June 9, 1752. Chanda Sahib was captured and
                 beheaded, establishing Muhammad Ali as Nawab of the Carnatic.
Treaty or      Treaty of Pondicherry (1754): Both the EIC and the French agreed to refrain from interfering in Indian
Outcome        princely disputes, defining mutual territorial possessions. Dupleix was recalled in 1754 due to financial
               losses, limiting French influence to Hyderabad under Bussy.


3. Third Carnatic War (1756-1763)
  Aspect                                                            Details
Belligerents    French East India Company (led by Count de Lally and Bussy) vs British East India Company (led by
                Eyre Coote and Robert Clive), with Hyderabad’s Nizam Salabat Jang shifting to EIC support.
Background      The Third Carnatic War (1756-1763) mirrored Europe's Seven Years' War, intensifying Anglo-French
                hostilities in India. While the French sought to maintain influence in Hyderabad and Carnatic, the
                British EIC gained decisive advantage after Clive's capture of Chandernagore (1757) and access to
                Bengal's vast resources, ultimately tipping the balance of power.
Key events      y French Capture of Fort St David (June 1758): Count de Lally swiftly seized Fort St David in
                  Cuddalore, while Bussy captured Vizianagaram, alarming the EIC. However, Lally’s financial
                  constraints and lack of naval support weakened French momentum.
                y Siege of Madras (December 1758-February 1759): Lally besieged Madras, but financial troubles, troop
                  demoralisation, and Admiral d’Ache’s refusal to provide naval support stalled progress. EIC’s
                  General Pocock relieved Madras with a fleet, forcing Lally’s retreat to Kanchipuram.
                y Loss of Northern Circars (1758-1759): Lally’s decision to recall Bussy from Hyderabad left French
                  forces under incompetent commanders.
                     ♦ The EIC occupied Rajahmundry and Masulipatam (Battle of Masulipatam (1759)), securing a
                       treaty with Nizam Salabat Jang in 1759, ending French influence in Hyderabad.
                y Battle of Wandiwash (January 22, 1760): Eyre Coote’s army decisively defeated Lally at Vandavasi
                  (Wandiwash), capturing Bussy. The EIC reduced French possessions to Jinje and Pondicherry,
                  culminating in Pondicherry’s surrender on February 16, 1761, after a six-month siege.
Treaty or       Treaty of Paris (1763): Ended the Seven Years’ War, restoring Pondicherry, Chandernagore, Karaikal,
Outcome         Yanaon, and Mahe to the French, but confining them to trade without political power. The EIC emerged
                as India’s dominant colonial power.

4. Impact of the Carnatic Wars
  Impact                         Description                                                Example
British         The Carnatic Wars solidified the British East         The EIC’s victory in the Third Carnatic War (1760-
dominance       India Company (EIC) as the dominant colonial          1763), particularly the capture of Pondicherry in 1761,
established     power in India, eliminating French competition        ended French political influence, leaving the EIC
                and paving the way for British territorial            unchallenged in South India.
                expansion.
Decline of      The French East India Company was reduced to          The Treaty of Paris (1763) restored French factories
French          a minor trading entity, confined to enclaves like     but restricted them to trade, and the French Company
influence       Pondicherry and Chandernagore, with no                ceased operations in 1769, relying on private traders
                political or military power.                          under English dominance.
Exposure of     The wars revealed the ineffectiveness of large        The Nawab of Carnatic’s 10,000-strong force under
Indian          Indian armies against disciplined European            Mahfuz Khan was routed by a smaller French army at
military        forces, encouraging European intervention in          the Battle of Adyar (1746), highlighting Indian armies’
weaknesses      Indian politics.                                      lack of modern tactics and naval support.
Shift to        The EIC transitioned from a trading company           Post-1763, the EIC consolidated control over Madras
territorial     to a territorial power, gaining control over key      and allied with Muhammad Ali, the Nawab of Carnatic,
power           regions and establishing administrative               laying the groundwork for British governance in the
                frameworks in South India.                            region.
Strengthened    The EIC’s superior naval presence in India,           The French naval defeat in August 1758 and Admiral
British naval   compared to the French, was critical to their         d’Ache’s withdrawal left Lally without support during
supremacy       success, enabling control over coastal trade          the Siege of Madras (1758-1759), underscoring British
                and military logistics.                               naval dominance.
Political       The wars demonstrated how European powers             Dupleix’s support for Chanda Sahib and Muzaffar Jang
instability     manipulated Indian succession disputes to             in the Second Carnatic War (1749-1754) and the EIC’s
exploited       expand influence, weakening regional powers           backing of Muhammad Ali intensified conflicts,
                like Hyderabad and Carnatic.                          reducing Indian rulers to pawns.
Economic        The EIC secured access to Bengal’s resources          After capturing Chandernagore (1757), the EIC used
gains for       and South Indian trade, enhancing its financial       Bengal’s wealth to fund campaigns in the Third Carnatic
EIC             and military capabilities for further conquests.      War, such as the decisive Battle of Wandiwash (1760).


5. Causes of the French Failure in India
 Nature of the        The French EIC was heavily state-controlled, bureaucratic, and reliant on royal subsidies. The English
 Company              EIC was a private, profit-driven enterprise enjoying superior functional autonomy and agile decision-
                      making.
 Resource and         British control over the lucrative revenues of Bengal (post-1757) bankrolled sustained military
 Financial            campaigns. Conversely, French expansion was crippled by the chronic fiscal crisis and bankruptcy of the
 Asymmetry            French monarchy.
 British Naval        Absolute maritime supremacy of the British Royal Navy ensured uninterrupted supply lines, rapid troop
 Hegemony             mobility, and the successful blockade of French strongholds (like the Battle of Wandiwash, 1760).
 Geopolitical         The French state was strategically overstretched, prioritising European continental conflicts and North
 Distractions         American colonies (like the Seven Years' War), thereby treating India as a peripheral theatre.
 Strategic and        The politically motivated recall of Dupleix (1754) abruptly halted French imperial momentum.
 Leadership           The British benefited from pragmatic, continuous, and militarily superior leadership (Robert Clive, Eyre
 Deficit              Coote).
 Commercial           The French neglected core commercial operations to pursue costly political intrigues. The British
 orientation          maintained a symbiotic balance, utilising robust trade surpluses to finance territorial expansion.
 Internal Issues      French operations were consistently undermined by institutional corruption, uncoordinated command
                      structures (naval vs. land forces), and unchecked private trade by officials at the company's expense.
The Carnatic Wars decisively established British supremacy in India, diminishing French influence and exposing Indian military
vulnerabilities. They transformed the EIC into a territorial power, setting the stage for British colonial rule and reshaping India’s
political landscape.

3.2 Anglo-Mysore Wars
The Anglo-Mysore Wars were a series of four conflicts fought between the Kingdom of Mysore and the British East
India Company (1767-1799), marking a crucial phase in British expansion and resistance led by rulers like Hyder Ali
and Tipu Sultan.


 1. Causes of the Anglo-Mysore Wars
          Causes                                                          Explanation

                                                            Strategic
 British-French rivalry          Mysore had strong ties with the French, who helped them with military supplies through
                                 ports like Mahe. Since Britain and France were global rivals, the British saw this as a direct
                                 threat to their presence in India.
 Mysore’s growing                Hyder Ali and Tipu Sultan built a strong army with modern weapons, trained by the
 military power                  French. This made the British anxious about losing control in South India.
                                 Hyder Ali established a modern arsenal at Dindigul after becoming Faujdar (1755) with
                                 French expertise.
 Mysore’s Strategic              Mysore occupied a geographically strategic position connecting the Malabar Coast, Deccan
 Position                        Plateau, and Carnatic region, making it a major obstacle to British expansion.

                                                             Political
 Control over Carnatic           Mysore’s expanding influence near British territories like Madras and the Carnatic region
 and Madras                      worried the British, as it threatened their security and trade interests.


Rejection of subsidiary         Tipu Sultan refused to accept British political control through Lord Wellesley’s Subsidiary
alliance                        Alliance system. This refusal became a key reason for the Fourth War.
Unstable alliances              Allies kept changing; sometimes the Nizam and Marathas supported Mysore, other times they
                                sided with the British. This made the political situation unstable and led to repeated conflicts.

                                                           Economic
Control of Malabar coast        Mysore controlled key spice trade routes along the Malabar coast. The British wanted this
trade                           trade for themselves, so they saw Mysore as an obstacle to their commercial expansion.
Self-sufficient economy of      Hyder and Tipu built arms factories, managed trade, and tried to make Mysore independent
Mysore                          of British goods. This challenged the British plan to dominate the Indian economy.

                                                    Immediate Triggers
British treaty violations       The British broke their promise made in the Treaty of Madras (1769) by not helping Hyder Ali
                                when the Marathas attacked him in 1771. This anger led to the Second War.
Tipu’s attack on                Tipu attacked Travancore (a British ally) in 1790 because it took control of some areas Tipu
Travancore                      claimed. This was the immediate cause of the Third War.
Tipu’s secret foreign           Tipu tried to build alliances with France, Arabia, and Afghanistan. When the British found
diplomacy                       out, they saw this as a conspiracy and used it as a reason to start the Fourth War.

2. First Anglo-Mysore War (1767-1769)
  Aspect                                                              Details
Main actors        Mysore under Hyder Ali vs. British East India Company, Nizam of Hyderabad, and Marathas (initially
                   allied with the British).
Background         The British sought to curb Mysore’s growing influence under Hyder Ali, who had modernised his army
                   with French support and gained control over Malabar trade routes. Mysore’s proximity to Madras
                   Presidency posed a serious strategic threat.
Course of          Hyder Ali used strategic diplomacy to neutralise the Marathas through payments and won over the Nizam.
war                After an indecisive campaign, Hyder marched swiftly to the gates of Madras in 1769, creating panic and
                   forcing the British to negotiate.
Key events         Hyder’s surprise march to Madras, shifting of alliances, display of military mobility, and psychological
                   warfare.
Treaty             Treaty of Madras (1769): Mutual restitution of conquered territories, prisoner exchange, and a clause
                   for mutual defence, later ignored by the British.
Outcome            Marked diplomatic and psychological victory for Mysore. Hyder Ali emerged as a formidable Indian power
                   challenging British supremacy. Exposed the fragility of British military dominance in the early stages.

3. Second Anglo-Mysore War (1780-1784)
  Aspect                                                              Details
Main actors        Mysore under Hyder Ali (later Tipu Sultan) initially formed an initial alliance with the Marathas and
                   Nizam vs the British East India Company. → The alliance was short-lived.
Background         Triggered by the British breach of the Treaty of Madras, they failed to support Hyder during the Maratha
                   incursions in 1771. Hyder turned to the French for arms and began importing war materials through Mahe,
                   alarming the British.
Course of          Hyder launched an offensive in Carnatic, capturing Arcot and defeating Colonel Baillie. Later, British
war                General Eyre Coote detached Mysore’s allies (by offering territorial and financial concessions to both the
                   Marathas and the Nizam). Hyder died in 1782; Tipu Sultan continued with tactical brilliance but no
                   decisive breakthrough.
Key events         Battle of Arcot, Battle of Pollilur (1780) (loss of British), Battle of Sholinghur (1781), Battle of Porto Novo
                   (1781) (British victory), capture of Colonel Braithwaite by Tipu Sultan
Treaty             Treaty of Mangalore (1784): Both sides agreed to the status quo ante bellum, the return of territories and
                   prisoners. One of the few treaties signed on equal footing.
Outcome            Though militarily inconclusive, it strengthened Tipu’s legitimacy and showcased Mysore’s military
                   resilience. The British reputation suffered, highlighting the limitations of the Company forces.


4. Third Anglo-Mysore War (1790-1792)
  Aspect                                                             Details
Main actors     Tipu Sultan vs the British (led by Lord Cornwallis), supported by the Nizam of Hyderabad and the Marathas.
Background      Conflict reignited when Tipu attacked Travancore (a British ally) in 1789. Travancore had acquired forts
                from the Dutch in Cochin, which Tipu claimed as vassal territory. The British viewed this as a violation of
                their strategic sphere.
Course of       Tipu initially defeated Gen. Meadows, but Cornwallis led a large allied force that captured Bangalore and
war             advanced toward Seringapatam. Despite resistance, Tipu was forced into negotiations after successive
                territorial losses.
Key events      Capture of Bangalore (1791), Siege of Seringapatam, loss of Coimbatore, hostage crisis involving Tipu’s
                sons.
Treaty          Treaty of Seringapatam (1792): Tipu ceded almost 50% of Mysore, including Baramahal, Dindigul,
                Malabar; paid ₹3 crore indemnity; gave up his two sons as hostages to British custody.
Outcome         Tipu’s power was drastically reduced; the British achieved territorial consolidation in South India.
                British-Nizam-Maratha cooperation proved successful in countering a powerful native state.

5. Fourth Anglo-Mysore War (1799)
     Aspect                                                             Details
Main actors             Tipu Sultan vs. the British (under Lord Wellesley, with Gen. Harris, Gen. Stuart, and Arthur
                        Wellesley), supported by the Nizam and Marathas.
Background              Tipu’s refusal to join the Subsidiary Alliance and his renewed ties with France (including
                        diplomatic contact with Napoleon, Versailles, and Mauritius) alarmed Wellesley. Tipu was accused of
                        conspiring against British rule.
Course of war           The British launched a swift and well-coordinated assault. Seringapatam was stormed on May 4,
                        1799. Tipu Sultan died fighting bravely. British forces looted his treasures and eliminated the last
                        major threat in the South.
Key battles/events      Battle of Seringapatam; death of Tipu; total collapse of Mysore resistance.
Treaty                  No formal treaty. The British restored the Wodeyar dynasty under Krishnaraja Wodeyar III.
                        Mysore was placed under a Subsidiary Alliance, ending its sovereignty.
Outcome                 Complete British political dominance over Mysore. Elimination of French influence from the
                        Deccan. Tipu’s martyrdom immortalised him as a symbol of early nationalism and resistance.
                        Marked the end of major independent military resistance in South India.

6. Consequences of the Anglo-Mysore Wars
   Consequences                                                        Explanation

                                                           Political
End of Mysore’s               After Tipu Sultan’s death in 1799, the British dismantled independent rule in Mysore. They
sovereignty                   installed a minor Wodeyar prince under the Subsidiary Alliance system, ensuring British
                              control over administration and foreign affairs. This marked a significant territorial and
                              political integration of Mysore into the British Indian Empire.
Rise of British               The elimination of Mysore, the strongest South Indian power resisting the British, paved the
hegemony in south             way for uncontested colonial expansion in the Deccan and southern peninsula. It also helped
India                         secure British strategic positions along the Coromandel and Malabar Coasts.

                                                           Military
Decline of indigenous         The defeat and death of Tipu Sultan, one of the most militarily advanced Indian rulers, marked a
resistance                    decline in organized, state-led resistance against colonial forces. The wars exposed the
                              technological superiority and discipline of the British army.
Demonstration of              Mysore's use of military modernization, including iron-cased rockets, French-style artillery,
modern warfare                and trained infantry, revealed the potential of hybrid warfare. However, its failure also
                              emphasized the limitations of regional powers in matching British coordination and logistics.


                                                                Economic
 British access to             Post-war, the British gained direct access to Mysore’s agricultural resources, forest wealth
 Mysore’s wealth               (sandalwood), and trade routes. These added substantially to the East India Company’s
                               revenues and enabled fiscal consolidation of colonial rule in South India.
 Economic decline of           Decades of war, followed by crippling indemnities (like ₹3 crores after 1792), led to fiscal
 Mysore                        exhaustion, reduced agricultural productivity, and disruption of local artisanal and trade
                               networks. Mysore’s economic autonomy was lost, replaced by British commercial exploitation.

                                                                 Cultural
 Tipu as a symbol of           Tipu Sultan became a lasting symbol of patriotic defiance, valour, and early nationalism in
 Anti-Colonial struggle        Indian memory. His administrative reforms, attempts to modernise, and refusal to surrender
                               made him a revered figure in India’s freedom struggle narrative.
 Psychological impact on       The repeated defeats of a well-organised state like Mysore created a sense of invincibility of
 Indian states                 British power. Many Indian rulers became more inclined to accept British supremacy, seek
                               protection, or join the Subsidiary Alliance system to avoid direct confrontation.

7. Innovations under Hyder Ali

     Dimension                            Key                                                    Impact/Examples
                             Innovations and Interventions
 Military                 Iron-Cased Rockets: Upgraded from                      Example: Crucial in defeating the British at the
 modernization            bamboo to iron chambers for higher thrust              Battle of Pollilur (1780) during the Second Anglo-
                          and explosive capacity. Created a                      Mysore War. Later reverse-engineered by the British
                          specialised 1,200-strong Rocket Corps.                 as Congreve rockets.
                          European Synthesis: Recruited French                   Example: Established a modern arms factory and
                          experts to train infantry and standardise              arsenal at Dindigul (1755) with French technical
                          artillery, uniforms, and regular salaries.             assistance, reducing reliance on European arms imports.
                          Naval Fleet: Established a navy on the                 Strategic Intent: Aimed to protect the lucrative
                          Malabar Coast (1763) utilising dhows and               Malabar pepper trade from European monopolisation
                          ketches.                                               and secure maritime supply lines.

 Administrative           Direct Control & Anti-Corruption:                      Impact: Eliminated feudal fragmentation. His
 centralization           Bypassed traditional intermediaries (Polygars)         meticulous accounting (initially aided by Khande
                          to establish direct state control. Instituted strict   Rao) maximised state revenue to fund continuous
                          financial checks and balances.                         warfare.
                          Intelligence Network: Deployed Harkaras                Impact: Provided an asymmetric information
                          (spies/messengers) across South India.                 advantage over the Marathas, the Nizam, and the
                                                                                 British East India Company.
 Socio-economic           Agrarian Focus: Assessed land revenue                  Impact: Laid the groundwork for Mysore’s ‘proto-
 policy                   based on soil fertility. Encouraged domestic           industrialisation’, later expanded by Tipu Sultan
                          metalwork and arms manufacturing.                      (e.g., sericulture).
                          Cultural and Religious Synthesis:                      Example: Commissioned the Lal Bagh Botanical
                          Maintained religious tolerance, appointed              Gardens in Bengaluru (later Tipu Sultan expanded it),
                          Hindu ministers (like Purnaiya), and                   blending Mughal and French landscaping, which also
                          permitted Christian church construction.               served as a centre for economic botany.
The Anglo-Mysore Wars ended with British victory and the death of Tipu Sultan in 1799, paving the way for British dominance
in southern India and weakening indigenous resistance against colonial expansion.

3.3 Anglo-Maratha Wars
The Anglo-Maratha Wars (1775-1818) were a series of three conflicts between the British East India Company and the Maratha
Empire. The wars fuelled by British expansionism and Maratha internal divisions ended Maratha dominance and cemented British
control over India.


1. Factors responsible for the Rise of the Marathas
The emergence of the Marathas in the 17th century was not merely a political shift but a regional culmination of geographic,
cultural, and strategic factors.

        Factor                                                            Details
 Geographic                The rugged terrain of the Western Ghats (Sahyadris) provided natural fortifications.
 topography                Example: The terrain facilitated the construction of strategic hill forts like Raigad and
                           Pratapgad, making traditional Mughal siege warfare difficult.
 Cultural and social       The Maharashtra Dharma (Bhakti Movement) fostered social equality. Saints like Eknath,
 unification               Tukaram, and Ramdas popularised the Marathi language, uniting fragmented clans under a
                           shared linguistic and cultural pride.
 Military                  Marathas gained extensive military and administrative experience by serving as nobles (bargirs
 apprenticeship            and shiledars) in the Deccan Sultanates (Ahmadnagar and Bijapur) long before establishing
                           their own empire.
 Ashta Pradhan             The Ashta Pradhan Mandal, established by Chhatrapati Shivaji, formed the administrative backbone
 Mandal                    of the Maratha kingdom. Its efficient division of responsibilities in finance, military, diplomacy, and
                           administration enabled Maratha expansion and effective resistance to Mughal invasions.
 Weakening of              Decline of Mughal authority after Aurangzeb created favourable conditions for Maratha
 Mughal Empire             expansion.
 Political vacuum in       The steady decline of the Deccan Sultanates (Adil Shahi and Qutb Shahi) and Aurangzeb’s
 the South                 prolonged, exhausting Deccan campaigns created a power vacuum ripe for Maratha
                           expansion.
 Strategic warfare         Chhatrapati Shivaji’s charismatic leadership united fragmented clans. They masterfully utilised
 and leadership            Guerrilla Warfare (Ganimi Kava), leveraging local terrain to outmanoeuvre larger
                           conventional armies.

2. Causes of the Anglo-Maratha Wars
     Cause                             Description                                                Example
 Maratha             Succession disputes and rivalries among                Narayanrao's assassination in 1773 sparked a
 internal            Maratha chiefs (Peshwas, Sindhia, Holkar,              power struggle, leading to the First War.
 divisions           Bhonsle, Gaekwad) weakened the Confederacy,
                     creating opportunities for British intervention.
 British             The East India Company (EIC) sought to expand          The EIC exploited Maratha disarray to impose a
 imperial            control over western India, aiming to establish        Bengal-like governance model in Bombay,
 ambitions           dominance akin to their Bengal administration.         triggering the First War.
 Treaty disputes     Maratha resentment over treaties like Surat            The Treaty of Bassein (1802), signed by Bajirao II,
 and alliances       (1775) and Bassein (1802) fuelled conflicts, as        alienated Sindhia and Bhonsle, escalating tensions
                     these were seen as undermining Maratha                 for the Second War.
                     sovereignty.
 Pindari raids       The British accused Marathas of sheltering             Lord Hastings used Pindari raids on British-allied
                     Pindaris, nomadic raiders, using this as a pretext     territories as a justification for launching the Third
                     for military action in the Third War.                  War in 1817.
 Economic and        The EIC aimed to control Maratha territories           The British targeted Maratha-controlled regions
 trade motives       for revenue and trade, especially after the 1813       like Gujarat and Deccan for economic gains,
                     Charter Act ended their trade monopoly, pushing        contributing to the Third War.
                     for new markets.


3. First Anglo-Maratha War (1775-1782)
  Aspect                                                           Details
Belligerents   Maratha Confederacy (led by Nana Phadnavis, Mahadji Sindhia) vs East India Company (EIC),
               supported by Raghunathrao.
Background     Triggered by a succession dispute after Peshwa Madhavrao I’s death (1772). Raghunathrao, seeking the
               Peshwaship, allied with the EIC via the Treaty of Surat (1775), ceding territories for military support. The
               Barabhai Council, backing infant Peshwa Sawai Madhavrao, opposed this, escalating tensions.
Key events     y Treaty of Surat (1775): Raghunathrao, a rival claimant to the Peshwaship, signed this treaty with the
                 EIC’s Bombay Presidency, ceding Salsette and Bassein and revenues from Surat and Bharuch in
                 exchange for 2,500 EIC troops to secure his claim → actual transfer occurred later through British military
                 successes.
               y Treaty of Purandar (1776): The EIC’s Calcutta Council, led by Warren Hastings, condemned the Surat
                 treaty as unauthorised and sent Colonel Upton to negotiate with the Barabhai Council, annulling
                 Raghunathrao’s claims and promising him a pension, but the Bombay government defied this, escalating
                 the conflict.
               y Battle of Wadgaon (January 1779): Maratha general Mahadji Sindhia employed a scorched earth
                 policy, burning fields and poisoning wells, luring EIC forces into the ghats near Talegaon. The Marathas
                 surrounded the EIC army at Wadgaon, cutting off supplies, forcing their surrender and the signing of the
                 Treaty of Wadgaon, relinquishing EIC gains since 1775.
               y EIC Counteroffensive (1779-1780): Warren Hastings rejected the Wadgaon treaty, dispatching Colonel
                 Goddard to capture Ahmedabad (February 1779) and Bassein (December 1780), while Captain
                 Popham seized Gwalior (August 1780), pressuring the Marathas to negotiate.
Outcome        Treaty of Salbai (1782): Restored pre-war territories, with the EIC retaining Salsette; Raghunathrao
               denied support; mutual peace agreed, with Mahadji Sindhia as guarantor.

4. Second Anglo-Maratha War (1803-1805)
  Aspect                                                           Details
Belligerents    Maratha Confederacy (Sindhia, Bhonsle, and Holkar) vs EIC, allied with Peshwa Bajirao II.
Background      Sparked by internal Maratha strife after Peshwa Madhavrao Narayan’s death (1795). Bajirao II’s ineffective
                rule and murder of Vithuji Holkar (1801) led to his ousting by Jaswantrao Holkar. Bajirao II’s flight and signing
                of the Treaty of Bassein (1802) with the EIC, accepting vassalage, alienated other Maratha chiefs.
Key events      y Hadapsar Victory (October 1802): Jaswantrao Holkar, seeking revenge for his brother Vithuji’s
                  murder, defeated the combined armies of Bajirao II and Sindhia at Hadapsar near Poona, installing
                  Vinayakrao (son of Amritrao) as a puppet Peshwa, forcing Bajirao II to flee.
                y Treaty of Bassein (December 1802): Bajirao II, seeking EIC protection, signed this treaty, agreeing to
                  station EIC troops in Maratha territories, cede Surat and territories yielding Rs 26 lakh, relinquish claims
                  to Nizam’s Chauth, and bar European employment hostile to the EIC, effectively reducing the Peshwa to
                  a vassal.
                y EIC Military Campaign (1803): Under Arthur Wellesley, the EIC launched a swift offensive, defeating
                  Sindhia and Bhonsle in key battles like Assaye (September 1803) and Laswari (November 1803),
                  showcasing superior British organisation and artillery against fragmented Maratha forces.
                y Holkar’s Failed Coalition (1804): Jaswantrao Holkar attempted to unite Indian rulers against the EIC,
                  but his coalition collapsed due to lack of support, allowing the EIC to consolidate gains through
                  separate subsidiary alliances with Sindhia and Bhonsle.
Outcome         Subsidiary alliances imposed on Sindhia and Bhonsle; EIC gained control over Surat and territories
                yielding Rs 26 lakh; Marathas reduced to vassalage.

5. Third Anglo-Maratha War (1817-1818)
  Aspect                                                           Details
Belligerents   Maratha Confederacy (Peshwa Bajirao II, Bhonsle, and Holkar) vs EIC, allied with Hyderabad and
               Gaekwad.
Background     Driven by British imperial ambitions under Lord Hastings and Maratha resentment over the Treaty of
               Bassein (1802). The EIC accused Marathas of sheltering Pindari raiders, using this as a pretext. Bajirao
               II’s rebellion in 1817 aimed to unify Maratha chiefs and restore power.


 Key events         y Peshwa’s rebellion (November 1817): Bajirao II, resentful of British dominance via the Treaty of
                      Bassein, attacked the British Residency at Poona (Battle of Khirki), aiming to rally Maratha chiefs
                      against the EIC, but faced disunity among allies.
                    y Bhonsle’s Attack (November 1817): Appa Sahib Bhonsle attacked the British Residency at Nagpur
                      (Battle of Sitabuldi), but EIC forces repelled the assault, exploiting Maratha internal weaknesses.
                    y EIC Victories (1817-1818): The EIC, under Lord Hastings, decisively defeated Maratha forces at Khirki
                      (November 1817) against Peshwa, Sitabuldi (November 1817) against Bhonsle, and Mahidpur
                      (December 1817) against Holkar, leveraging superior logistics and alliances with Hyderabad and
                      Gaekwad.
                    y Treaty Signings and Surrender (1817-1818): The EIC imposed the Treaty of Poona (June 1817) on
                      Peshwa, Treaty of Gwalior (November 1817) on Sindhia, and Treaty of Mandasor (January 1818) on
                      Holkar; Bajirao II surrendered in June 1818, marking the end of Maratha resistance.
 Outcome            Treaties of Poona, Gwalior, and Mandasor (1817-1818); dissolution of the Maratha Confederacy;
                    Peshwaship abolished; Bajirao II exiled to Bithur; Satara formed under Pratap Singh. → The war completed
                    British paramountcy over India.

6. Impact of the Anglo-Maratha Wars
     Dimension                                                        Description
 Political impact          y End of Maratha Confederacy: The Third Anglo-Maratha War (1817-18) led to the dissolution
                             of the Maratha Confederacy.
                           y Abolition of Peshwaship: Peshwa Baji Rao II was exiled to Bithoor, and the institution of
                             Peshwa was abolished permanently.
 Rise of British           y Unchallenged power: With the Marathas defeated, the British East India Company became the
 dominance                   paramount power in India, marking the near-complete colonisation of the subcontinent.
                           y Control over key territories: Pune, Nagpur, and others were annexed, and Gwalior signed
                             treaties and accepted British paramountcy.
 Administrative            y Introduction of British Administration: British systems were introduced in former Maratha
 changes                     territories, expanding the Company's administrative reach.
                           y End of Indigenous Governance: Traditional decentralised power structures under Maratha
                             Sardars were dismantled.

 Established British       y Defeat of major Maratha forces (Holkar at Mahidpur, Bhonsle at Sitabuldi) confirmed British
 military supremacy          dominance in Indian warfare after the Anglo-Maratha Wars.
 Socio-economic            y Decline of Maratha Nobility: Sardars and local chieftains lost revenue and power, disrupting
 impact                      traditional rural patronage.
                           y Integration into Colonial Economy: Maratha lands were integrated into the Company’s revenue
                             system, shifting focus to taxation and trade profits.
 Diplomatic shift          y Subsidiary Alliances: Treaties like Bassein (1802) and Mandasor (1818) turned Maratha rulers
                             into British dependents.
                           y Neutralisation of Threats: The British ensured the Marathas were no longer allied with rival
                             European powers (Treaty of Salbai conditions).
 Cultural and              y Fall of Indigenous Pride: The Marathas, successors of Shivaji’s legacy, losing to foreign rule
 symbolic loss               marked a psychological blow to Indian sovereignty.
                           y Disbanding of Maratha Power Centres: Seats of power like Pune lost their political
                             centrality.
The Anglo-Maratha Wars marked the decisive downfall of the Maratha Confederacy and paved the way for British
supremacy in India. With the abolition of the Peshwaship and annexation of key territories, the British established
direct control, reshaping India's political and administrative future.

chapters.t: Impact of British Rule in India
chapters.text:
4               Impact of British
                        Rule in India
The impact of British rule in India was profound and multidimensional. Economic exploitation, political subjugation, and
social transformation reshaped Indian society. As Dadabhai Naoroji said, “India is being bled in a slow and continuous
stream," highlighting the drain of wealth that defined colonial governance.

1. Economic Impact of British Rule in India
       Aspect                          Description                                            Example
 De-industrialisation     British policies reversed India’s textile   As per RC Dutt in Economic History of India- 2/3rd of
                          trade, turning it from an exporter of       cotton textile worn by Indians by 1886 had a British tag.
                          finished goods to an importer of
                          British machine-made products,
                          crippling handicraft industries.
 Commercialisation        Forced cultivation of cash crops like       Indigo cultivation mandated on 3/20th of farmers’ land
 of agriculture           indigo, opium, and cotton for British       (Tinkathia system) left soil infertile, while the opium trade
                          industries reduced food grain               favoured British profits, contributing to food shortages and
                          production, causing famines and             famines.
                          peasant distress.
 Exploitative land        High revenue demands under                  The Permanent Settlement (1793) in Bengal fixed high
 revenue policies         Permanent, Mahalwari, and Ryotwari          revenues (often ranging between 50-60% of produce/
                          settlements impoverished peasants,          value), benefiting zamindars but burdening peasants;
                          increasing landlessness and debt.           failure to pay led to land sales to moneylenders.
 Drain of wealth          The British extracted wealth through        Wealth from Indian rulers, merchants, and peasants was
                          trade profits, high salaries, and           syphoned to Britain, reducing capital available for Indian
                          pensions, draining India’s resources to     development.
                          fund Britain’s economy.
 Transport and            Railways and telegraphs were                The first railway line from Bombay to Thane (1853) and
 communication            developed to facilitate British trade,      the telegraphic line from Calcutta to Agra (1853, final line,
                          connecting raw material sources to          not experimental like from Calcutta to Diamond Harbour
                          ports, primarily benefiting colonial        in 1851) eased the transport of British goods, with a 5%
                          interests.                                  profit guarantee for British investors.
 Rise of new money        A new money-lending class and               Moneylenders seized peasants’ lands due to high-interest
 lending classes          middle class emerged, exploiting            loans, while English-educated Indians became clerks,
                          peasants and serving British                reducing British administrative costs.
                          interests, respectively.

2. Social and Cultural Impact of British Rule in India
                                             Positive Socio-Cultural Impacts

     Aspect                         Description                                                Example
 English             English education fostered nationalism and        The Charter Act (1813) allocated ₹1 lakh annually for
 Education           rationalism among reformers.                      promotion of education, and Wood’s Despatch (1854)
                                                                       recommended the establishment of universities, which
                                                                       were set up in Calcutta, Bombay and Madras in 1857,
                                                                       influencing reformers like Vidyasagar.
 Cultural            Orientalist efforts by British scholars           William Jones’ Asiatic Society (1784) and translations of
 Revival             glorified India’s past, boosting Indian pride     Sanskrit works like Kalidasa’s introduced India’s heritage
                     and cultural awareness.                           globally, fostering self-respect among Indians.


Social Reforms   Legal measures addressed practices like sati     The Sharda Act (1929) set minimum marriage ages (girls
                 and child marriage. The British also tried       14, boys 18), and inter-caste marriage was legalised
                 to address caste discrimination, though          under the Special Marriage Act of 1872, challenging rigid
                 implementation was limited.                      caste norms.
Role of Press    Newspapers, literature, and theatre spread       Vernacular publications (like Kesari in Marathi and
and Arts         nationalist ideas, uniting Indians against       Amrita Bazar Patrika in Bengali) allowed nationalist
                 colonial rule.                                   leaders to communicate directly with the masses,
                                                                  spreading ideas of self-rule and democracy.

                                        Negative Socio-Cultural Impacts
Communal         Colonial policies deepened religious             The divide-and-rule policy was envisioned in the Partition
Divide and       identities and communalism.                      of Bengal; Separate Electorates (1909), etc.
Social           Census and legal codification rigidified caste   The Criminal Tribes Act (1871), The Punjab Land
Stratification   identities, crystallizing castes.                Alienation Act (1900), martial castes after 1857 revolt,
Racial           Europeans considered themselves culturally       ICS exclusion, racial segregation, and the Ilbert Bill
Discrimination   and racially superior.                           controversy.
Tribal           Intrusion of the colonial state,                 Indian Forest Acts (1865, 1878) criminalized shifting
Displacement     commercialization of forests, and influx of      agriculture; cultural disruption triggered uprisings
and Cultural     outsiders (Dikus) disrupted egalitarian tribal   (Santhal Rebellion, 1855; Munda Ulgulan, 1899).
Erosion          societies and customary rights.
Erosion of       The colonial economy weakened artisan            Decline of traditional handloom sector.
Traditional      classes and indigenous crafts.
Industries &
Arts
Limited Reach    Benefits of education and reforms remained       Limited reforms' penetration culminated in low literacy
