of Reforms       urban and elite-centric, creating an elite       among rural masses.
                 class loyal to British rule.
Psychological    The promotion of Western superiority             Thomas Macaulay's educational vision encouraged the
Subordination    created an inferiority complex among             creation of an intermediary class: "Indian in blood and
                 Indians.                                         colour, but English in taste."

3. Political and Administrative Impact of British Rule in India
   Aspect                       Description                                               Example
Integration of   The British consolidated and integrated          y Battles of Plassey (1757) and Buxar (1764) established
India            power through wars, annexations, and               British control in Bengal, while the Doctrine of Lapse
                 alliances. The British defeated regional           annexed states like Jhansi (1854).
                 powers and integrated India under one            y The Anglo-Mysore, Anglo-Maratha (1817-19), and
                 roof.                                              Anglo-Sikh (1849) wars ended with British annexation
                                                                    of Mysore, Maratha and Punjab territories and brought
                                                                    them all under one British rule.
Administrative   Concentration of legislative, executive,         Administrative centralisation began with the Regulating
Centralization   and financial powers in the Central              Act, 1773 reached peak under the Charter act of 1833,
                 Government, reducing provincial autonomy         which vested exclusive legislative authority in the
                 and establishing a uniform administration        Governor-General of India.
                 across British India.
Subsidiary       These policies weakened Indian states,           The Subsidiary Alliance forced states to maintain British
Alliance and     ensuring British dominance without direct        troops, as with Awadh, while the Doctrine of Lapse
Doctrine of      rule.                                            annexed Satara (1848) and Nagpur (1853).
Lapse
Divide and       The British exploited religious and              Post-1857, the Divide and Rule policy strained Hindu-
Rule             regional divisions to maintain control,          Muslim relations, weakening unified resistance.
                 sowing seeds of communal discord.
Judicial         Codified laws and court hierarchies              New civil and criminal courts integrated the justice system
Reforms          aimed at the ‘Rule of Law’ but were              but were costly for commoners and favoured Europeans,
                 inaccessible and discriminatory.                 undermining Indian rights.


4. Resistance and Nationalism under British Rule in India
      Aspect                         Description                                             Example
 Peasant and           Exploitative revenue policies and land         The Santhal Rebellion (1855-56) in Bengal and Bihar
 Tribal Revolts        evictions sparked widespread rebellions        opposed land encroachments, while Poligars in Tamil
                       against British rule.                          Nadu and Mappilas in Malabar resisted colonial
                                                                      control.
 Famines and           Frequent famines, driven by commercial         Many major famines (1800-1947) killed millions, with
 Economic              agriculture and high taxes, fuelled            the Great Bengal Famine (1943) causing 3-4 million
 Distress              anti-British sentiments.                       deaths, pushing peasants into poverty.
 Revolt of 1857        A unified uprising against British policies,   The revolt united diverse groups against British rule,
                       driven by economic, social, and cultural       ending with the East India Company’s control and
                       grievances, marked a turning point.            inspiring nationalism, despite failing due to a lack of
                                                                      organisation.
 Rise of               English education and Western ideas            Reformers like Raja Ram Mohan Roy and political
 Nationalism           inspired a national consciousness, laying      discussions in newspapers spread ideas of liberty and
                       the groundwork for the freedom struggle        self-rule, uniting Indians across regions.
                       movement.
 Religious and         Reformers used Western ideas to                Swami Vivekananda promoted spiritual nationalism,
 Social Reform         challenge social evils, fostering unity and    social reform and cultural self-confidence, contributing
 Movements             pride.                                         to anti-imperialist sentiments.
The British rule left behind a paradox of modern institutions, infrastructure, and education but also deep-rooted inequalities
and underdevelopment. The legacy remains contested, as colonial tools of control eventually fuelled nationalist awakening
and resistance. As Jawaharlal Nehru observed, “A moment comes when we step out from the old to the new", capturing
India’s eventual break from colonial chains.

chapters.t: Acts and regulations under British India
chapters.text:
5               Acts and Regulations
                        under British India
5.1 Acts and Regulations under East India Company
The Acts and Regulations under the East India Company were legislative measures enacted by the British Parliament to regulate
the Company’s administration, trade, and governance in India. These laws aimed to address corruption, financial instability,
and the expanding political role of the Company, laying the foundation for British colonial rule.

1. Reasons for Acts and Regulations under East India Company
           Reason                                                        Description
 Financial Crisis and            The Company faced financial difficulties due to corruption and inefficient administration,
 Administrative                  leading to declining revenues and loss of public trust, necessitating government intervention.
 Mismanagement                   Example: The East India Company sought a loan of £1 million from the British Government in
                                 1772, which directly contributed to parliamentary intervention and the Regulating Act, 1773.
 Need for British                As the Company expanded territorially, the British government asserted political control,
 Government Oversight and        clarifying that sovereignty in India was exercised on behalf of the Crown rather than the
 Sovereignty                     Company.
 Centralization and              Acts centralised authority by empowering the Governor-General, separating executive
 Administrative Reforms          and legislative functions, and establishing legislative councils and courts to improve
                                 governance and reduce conflicts.
 Trade Monopoly and              The Company’s trade monopoly was gradually curtailed, opening Indian trade to other
 Economic Policy                 British merchants in line with evolving economic policies favouring free trade within the
 Adjustments                     empire.
 Humanitarian Concerns           Disasters like famines highlighted administrative failures, prompting reforms including
 and Social Reforms              missionary activities, promotion of education, and social legislation.
                                 Example: The catastrophic Great Famine of 1876–1878 prompted major shifts in British
                                 colonial policy, most notably the creation of the Indian Famine Code and the establishment
                                 of the Strachey Commission.
 Political Stability             Competitive civil service exams and limited Indian representation were introduced to
 and Inclusion                   improve administrative efficiency and address demands for political participation, especially
                                 after military challenges and revolts.

2. Regulating Act of 1773

        Features                                                       Description
 Governor-General of        Created the post of Governor-General of Bengal (Warren Hastings was the first) with an
 Bengal                     executive council of four members; decisions were made by majority vote, limiting the
                            Governor-General’s power.
 Centralization of          Made the Governors of Madras and Bombay subordinate to the Governor-General of
 Power                      Bengal, establishing centralised authority over the three presidencies.
 Supreme Court at           The Act created the Supreme Court at Calcutta with jurisdiction over British subjects, but the
 Calcutta                   extent of its jurisdiction over Indians became a major source of conflict, leading to later
                            clarifications through the Act of Settlement (1781). Sir Elijah Impey was the first Chief Justice.
 Control over Company       Prohibited Company servants from engaging in private trade or accepting bribes and gifts
 Officials                  from natives to curb corruption.
 Financial Regulations      Limited Company dividends to 6% until repayment of government loans; restricted terms of
                            Court of Directors to four years.
 Reporting and              Required the Company’s Court of Directors to report on civil, military, and revenue matters to
 Oversight                  the British government, increasing parliamentary oversight of Company affairs.


3. Pitt’s India Act of 1784

    Features                                                       Description
Dual System of          Established a dual control system where the British government managed political and
Governance              administrative affairs through the Board of Control, while the Company retained commercial
                        activities managed by the Court of Directors.
Board of Control        A six-member body appointed by the British Crown, including the Secretary of State and
                        Chancellor of the Exchequer, with authority over civil, military, and revenue matters; it required
                        approval of all Company dispatches and access to papers.
Governor-               Reduced council members from four to three, including the Commander-in-Chief; Governor-
General’s Council       General given greater authority, including a casting vote over council decisions. Later, the
                        Amending Act of 1786 and the Charter Act of 1793 granted the Governor-General the further power
                        to entirely override his council's decisions (veto power).
Recognition of          Officially termed the Company’s Indian territories as ‘British possessions', marking formal British
British Possessions     sovereignty over these lands.
Property                Required civil and military officers to declare their properties in India and Britain to prevent
Disclosure              corruption, with penalties for violations.
Separation of           Clearly separated the Company’s political and commercial functions, ensuring government
Powers                  oversight of administration while allowing the Company to continue trade.

4. Charter Act of 1793
         Features                                                       Description
Extension of Company             Renewed the East India Company’s charter and trade monopoly in India for 20 more
Charter                          years.
British Sovereignty Clarified    It was stated that the acquisition of sovereignty by the Company was on behalf of the
                                 British Crown, not in its own right, reinforcing British government control.
Governor-General’s Powers        Granted the Governor-General extensive authority over the presidencies of Madras and
Enhanced                         Bombay, including the power to overrule his council and supersede governors when
                                 present in those presidencies.
Appointments Subject to          Required royal approval for appointments of the Governor-General, governors of Madras
Royal Approval                   and Bombay, and the Commander-in-Chief.
Financial Obligations            The company had to pay the British government an annual sum of five lakh pounds from
                                 Indian revenues after expenses; salaries of Board of Control staff charged to the Company.
Trade Licensing and              Allowed the Company to grant licenses for ‘privilege’ or ‘country trade’ to individuals
Monopoly                         and employees, enabling trade such as opium shipments to China under Company control.
Administrative Changes           Allowed the Governor-General to appoint a Vice President from civilian council
                                 members during his absence from Bengal; changed the composition of the Board of Control
                                 to a President and two junior members, not necessarily Privy Council members.
Judicial and Revenue             Separated revenue administration from judicial functions, leading to the abolition of
Separation                       revenue courts known as Maal Adalats.
Restrictions on Company          Senior Company officials were barred from leaving India without permission;
Officials                        unauthorised departure was considered resignation.

5. Charter Act of 1813
     Features                                                       Description
End of Trade              Abolished the East India Company’s monopoly over Indian trade except for trade in tea and
Monopoly                  trade with China, allowing other British merchants to trade freely in India.
Extension of              Extended the Company’s charter and rule in India for another 20 years under increased British
Company Rule              Crown oversight.
Assertion of Crown        Affirmed the British Crown’s sovereignty over British possessions in India, clearly defining the
Sovereignty               constitutional position of British territories.


Missionary Activities       Allowed Christian missionaries to enter India for religious proselytisation and education, leading
Permitted                   to the appointment of a bishop in Calcutta.
Financial Provisions        Allocated an annual grant of Rs. 1 lakh for the promotion of Indian education, literature, and
for Education               science. First official step toward state involvement in education.
Judicial and Taxation       Empowered local governments to tax people under the jurisdiction of the Supreme Court; Indian
Powers                      courts gained more authority over British subjects.
Company Dividends           Fixed the Company’s dividend at 10.5%; required separation and maintenance of territorial and
and Financial               commercial accounts.
Management
Enhanced British            Increased British government supervision over Company affairs, including appointments and
Government Control          administration, marking a shift towards greater regulatory control.

6. Charter Act of 1833
      Features                                                         Description
End of Company’s             The East India Company ceased to be a commercial entity and became solely an administrative
Commercial Role              body governing British territories in India.
Extension of                 Renewed the Company’s charter for 20 years, but with diminished trade privileges, including the
Company’s Charter            end of monopoly on trade with China and tea.
Centralization of            The Lord Willian Bentinck Governor-General of Bengal was redesignated as the Governor-
Power                        General of India, with authority over all British territories, including Madras and Bombay
                             presidencies, centralising administrative and legislative powers.
Exclusive Legislative        The Governor-General in Council was granted exclusive legislative powers over all British
Authority                    Indian territories, removing legislative powers from the governors of Madras and Bombay.
Appointment of Law           A law expert was added to the Governor-General’s council to advise on legislation, marking the
Member                       beginning of legal codification efforts. Lord Macaulay was appointed as the first law member.
Non-Discrimination           Declared that no Indian should be disqualified from holding any office or employment based
Clause                       on religion, caste, or descent, laying a foundation for future political activism.
Promotion of Social          Urged improvement in conditions of slavery, which eventually led to its abolition in 1843.
Reforms
Freedom for European         Removed restrictions on European immigration and property acquisition in India, facilitating
Settlement                   greater British settlement and colonisation.
Territorial Ownership        It stipulated that Company territories were held “in trust” for the British Crown, marking a
                             shift in sovereignty from the Company to the Crown.
Attempted Civil              Proposed introduction of competitive examinations for civil service appointments, though this
Service Reforms              was opposed and not fully implemented at the time.

7. Charter Act of 1853
        Features                                                        Description
Indefinite Renewal of           The Act renewed the East India Company’s charter without specifying a fixed term, allowing
Company’s Charter               Parliament to end the Company’s rule at any time.
Separation of Legislative       Formally separated the legislative and executive roles within the Governor-General’s Council,
and Executive Functions         establishing a distinct Indian (Central) Legislative Council with six new legislative members.
Expansion and Composition       Increased the council’s size to 12 members, including officials and representatives from
of Legislative Council          provinces like Bengal, Madras, Bombay, and Agra, introducing local representation.
Governor-General’s              Empowered the Governor-General to appoint a Vice-President of the Legislative Council
Powers                          and granted veto power over legislation.
Reduction and Crown             Reduced the Court of Directors from 24 to 18 members, with six nominated by the British
Nomination of Directors         Crown, diminishing the Company’s autonomy.
Introduction of                 The Act introduced an open competitive examination system for recruitment into the Indian
Competitive Civil Service       Civil Services, replacing the earlier system of nominations and patronage by the East India
Exams                           Company's Court of Directors. Implementation followed the Macaulay Committee (1854).


 Creation of New                 Authorised the Court of Directors to create new provinces or presidencies, leading to the
 Presidencies                    appointment of separate governors (e.g., Punjab in 1859).
 Financial Responsibility        Made the Company liable for administrative expenses, including salaries of Board of
                                 Control members and other officials.
 Legislative Process             Established legislative procedures similar to the British Parliament, including public
 Formalization                   debates and select committees, marking a step toward a parliamentary form of governance
                                 in India.
8. Impacts of Acts and Regulations under East India Company
            Impact                                                         Description
 Centralization of Authority       The Acts, starting with the Regulating Act of 1773, centralised power by creating the
                                   Governor-General position, enabling more unified and effective control over the
                                   Company's Indian territories.
 Increased British                 The British Parliament gained the authority to supervise the Company’s administration,
 Government Oversight              finances, and policies, reducing the Company’s autonomy and ensuring accountability to
                                   the Crown and Parliament.
 Establishment of Legal and        The creation of the Supreme Court at Calcutta and the separation of judicial from executive
 Judicial Framework                functions introduced British legal principles and a structured judicial system in India.
 Reduction of Corruption and       Regulations banned private trade and acceptance of gifts by Company officials, aiming
 Administrative Reforms            to curb corruption and improve governance, though with limited success initially.
 Transition from                   Over time, the Acts curtailed the Company’s commercial monopoly and expanded its
 Commercial to Political           administrative role, culminating in the Government of India Act 1858, which ended
 Control                           Company rule and transferred power directly to the British Crown.
 Foundation for Modern             These regulations laid the groundwork for the British Raj’s administrative, legislative, and
 Colonial Governance               judicial systems, influencing India’s colonial governance structure until independence.
The Acts and Regulations under the East India Company marked a crucial transition from commercial enterprise to formal colonial
governance, establishing British political dominance in India. They set the stage for the eventual direct rule by the British Crown,
shaping the administrative and legal framework of colonial India.
5.2 Acts and Regulations under British Crown
1. Government of India Act of 1858
Also known as the Act for the Good Government of India, it was a direct legislative fallout of the Revolt of 1857. It marked the
formal end of Company rule and the beginning of the British Raj.

        Features                                                        Description
 Transfer of Power            Transferred the governance of India from the East India Company directly to the British Crown,
                              ending the era of Company rule.
 Abolition of Dual            Abolished the Board of Control and the Court of Directors, ending the "Dual Government" system
 Government                   established by Pitt’s India Act (1784).
 Creation of Secretary        Established the office of the Secretary of State (SoS) for India, a member of the British Cabinet
 of State (SoS)               responsible for Indian administration, vested with complete authority.
 The India Council            Created a 15-member Council of India to assist the SoS. It was an advisory body where the SoS
                              acted as the Chairman with a casting vote.
 Change in Designation        Redesignated the Governor-General of India as the Viceroy of India, acting as the direct
 (Viceroy)                    representative of the Crown (Lord Canning was the first).
 Unitary Administrative       Centralised all authority in the SoS and the Viceroy; the Provincial governments became mere
 Structure                    agents of the Central Government.
 Crown Sovereignty            Announced through the Queen’s Proclamation (1858) that the Crown would honour existing
 over Princely States         treaties with Princes and cease further territorial annexations.
 Legal Personality            Provided that the Secretary of State in Council could sue and be sued in India and England as a
                              "Body Corporate."


                                    Impacts of Government of India Act of 1858
 End of Company Era          Formally ended the East India Company rule, ending its 250-year transition from a commercial
                             venture to a political sovereign.
 ‘Home’ Government           Shifted the "Center of Gravity" to London. The Secretary of State became the "Grand Mughal,"
 Supremacy                   wielding absolute power over Indian finances and policy from Whitehall.
 Policy stagnation           Post-1857, the British almost abandoned social reforms (like Sati abolition) to avoid offending
                             orthodoxies, marking the beginning of policy stagnation and communal steering.
 Shift in Princely           Ended the Doctrine of Lapse. The British switched from "Annexation" to "Subordination,"
 Relations                   turning Princely States into "breakwaters" against future storms.
 Administrative              Established a rigid, bureaucratic hierarchy. The "Viceroy" became a more prestigious title but was
 Centralization              more strictly controlled by London via the telegram.
 Military                    Triggered the Peel Commission recommendations: increasing the ratio of European to Indian
 Reorganization              soldiers and practicing "communal balancing" in regiments.
 Formalized ‘Economic        The expenses of the 1857 Revolt and the EIC’s buyout were added to India's Public Debt,
 ‘Drain'                     intensifying the "Drain of Wealth" under the Crown's guarantee.
 Racial Antagonism           Solidified the "White Man’s Burden" (Rudyard Kipling) ideology ; the distance between the rulers
                             and the ruled widened as the British became an "occupying garrison".

                                   Limitations of Government of India Act of 1858
 Institutional               Created a "Grand Mughal" in London (Secretary of State) with no accountability to the Indian
 Absolutism                  people; the British Parliament’s oversight remained superficial.
 Advisory Council            The 15-member Council of India was purely advisory; the Secretary of State could override them
 Weakness                    on almost all key political and administrative matters.
 Financial Burden            The "Home Charges" (salaries and pensions of the London office) were entirely funded by Indian
                             taxpayers, increasing the Drain of Wealth without any service in return.
 Absence of Indian           The Act completely ignored the demand for Indian participation in governance; not a single Indian
 Representation              was included in the new executive or advisory structure.
 Bureaucratic Rigidity       It solidified a "Steel Frame" bureaucracy that was distant and unresponsive, prioritising British
                             imperial interests over local developmental needs.
 Policy of Non-              Under the guise of respecting Indian traditions, the British halted all progressive social reforms,
 Intervention                effectively siding with reactionary elements of society.
 Tele-Dictatorship           The introduction of the telegraph allowed London to micromanage the Viceroy, curbing local
                             administrative flexibility and initiative during crises.
 Unchanged Ground            The Act was merely a "Constitutional Post-mortem" of the 1857 Revolt; it changed the form of the
 Reality                     government (Crown vs. Company) but not the nature of exploitation.

2. Indian Councils Act, 1861
The Act is a landmark in Indian Constitutional history as it sowed the first seeds of representative institutions and
legislative devolution as the British response to the realisation that they could no longer govern India without some form
of local consultation.

  Category                                                 Features / Description
 Features         y Portfolio System: Formalised by Lord Canning, members of the Executive Council were made in-charge of
                    specific departments (origin of Cabinet government).
                  y Legislative Expansion: Expanded the Viceroy’s Executive Council for legislative purposes by adding 6 to
                    12 "additional" members (at least half to be non-officials).
                  y Beginning of Representation: Nominated three Indians (Raja of Benares, Maharaja of Patiala, and Sir
                    Dinkar Rao) to the Legislative Council.
                  y Decentralisation: Restored legislative powers to Bombay and Madras Presidencies, reversing the
                    centralisation trend started in 1833.
                  y Ordinance Power: Empowered the Viceroy to issue ordinances during emergencies, valid for six months,
                    without the Council's concurrence.


 Impacts         y Institutional Birth: Laid the foundation for the modern Cabinet system and the parliamentary framework
                   in India.
                 y Policy of Association: Marked the shift toward "associating" Indians with administration to act as a "safety
                   valve" against future revolts.
                 y Legislative Devolution: Empowered the Governor-General to establish new provincial legislative councils.
                   Under this provision, legislative councils were established in Bengal in 1862 and Punjab in 1897
 Limitations     y Powerless Non-Officials: Indian members were hand-picked "loyalists" (Princes/Zamindars) who did not
                   represent the masses or the emerging intelligentsia.
                 y Limited Legislative Scope: The Council could not discuss finances (Budget), ask questions, or vote on any
                   executive action; it was a mere "advisory" committee.
                 y Viceroy’s Veto: The Viceroy held absolute power to overrule the Council or bypass it entirely via his
                   ordinance-making power.
                 y Official Majority: The "official" (British) majority ensured that no legislation contrary to imperial interests
                   could ever be passed.

3. Indian Councils Act, 1892
Congress demands from 1885-1892 influenced these reforms.

  Category                                                 Features / Description
 Features        y Increased Membership: Increased the number of "additional" members in Central (10 to 16) and
                   Provincial Legislative Councils.
                 y Indirect Election: Introduced the "principle of election" (though the word wasn't used); non-official
                   members were nominated based on recommendations by Universities, Municipalities, and Zamindars.
                 y Budgetary Discussion: For the first time, members were allowed to discuss the annual financial statement
                   (Budget) under specific conditions.
                 y Right to Inquire: Members were empowered to address questions to the executive on matters of public
                   interest (requiring 6 days' notice).
 Impacts         y Parliamentary Seed: It marked the transition of Legislative Councils from mere "advisory bodies" toward
                   becoming "deliberative forums."
                 y Validation of INC: Served as a formal acknowledgment of the growing influence of the Indian National
                   Congress and its constitutional demands.
                 y Political Awareness: Budget discussions allowed Indian leaders to critique British economic policies (like
                   the Drain of Wealth) on the floor of the house.
 Limitations     y No Right to Vote: While members could discuss the budget, they had no power to vote on it or move any
                   amendments.
                 y Restricted Questioning: No "supplementary questions" were allowed; the executive could refuse to answer
                   any question without providing a reason.
                 y Official Majority: A permanent "official majority" was maintained at the Center, ensuring the British
                   government could never be outvoted.
                 y Diluted Election: The system of "recommendation" meant the Viceroy still held the ultimate power of
                   appointment, making the "election" a mere formality.

4. Indian Councils Act, 1909
The Indian Councils Act of 1909, popularly known as the Morley-Minto Reforms, represents the British policy of "Divide and
Rule" entering the constitutional sphere. An attempt to reconcile the Moderates while simultaneously isolating the Extremists and
creating communal rifts.

   Category                                                 Features / Description
 Features          y Increased Size: Significantly expanded Legislative Councils; Central Council members rose from 16 to 60.
                   y Separate Electorates: Introduced communal representation for Muslims, where Muslim candidates were
                     elected only by Muslim voters (Lord Minto is known as the "Father of Communal Electorate").
                   y Executive Council Entry: For the first time, Indians were appointed to the Viceroy’s Executive Council
                     (Satyendra Prasanna Sinha became the Law Member).
                   y Enhanced Deliberative Powers: Empowered members to move resolutions on the Budget, ask
                     supplementary questions, and discuss matters of public importance.


 Impacts           y Communal Institutionalisation: Legally recognised communalism providing separate electorates to
                     Muslims, creating a permanent political divide between Hindus and Muslims that eventually led to
                     Partition.
                   y Moderates' Satisfaction: Temporarily appeased the Moderate faction of the Congress, giving them a
                     sense of participation in high governance.
                   y Parliamentary Training: Provided Indian leaders with experience in parliamentary procedures, debates,
                     and critical analysis of government policies.
 Limitations       y The "Shadow" of Democracy: Morley himself clarified that these reforms were not intended to lead to a
                     parliamentary system or "Self-Government" for India.
                   y Official vs. Non-Official: While non-officials gained a majority in Provincial councils, an official
                     majority was strictly maintained at the Central level.
                   y Indirect & Limited Franchise: The election system was highly complex (tiered) and based on high
                     property/educational qualifications, excluding the general masses.
                   y Responsibility Gap: Members could discuss and critique, but the Executive was not accountable to the
                     Legislature; the Viceroy retained absolute veto power.

5. Government of India Act, 1919 (Montagu-Chelmsford Reforms)
  Category                                                Features / Description
 Features        y Diarchy in Provinces: Divided provincial subjects into two parts: Reserved (handled by Governor/
                   Bureaucracy) and Transferred (handled by Indian Ministers).
                 y Bicameralism at Centre: Introduced a dual-house legislature: the Council of State (Upper House) and the
                   Central Legislative Assembly (Lower House).
                 y Direct Elections: Introduced direct elections for the first time in India, though the franchise was highly
                   restricted based on property, tax, and education.
                 y Communal Representation Expansion: Extended the "Separate Electorate" principle to Sikhs, Indian
                   Christians, Anglo-Indians, and Europeans.
                 y Establishment of PSC: Provided for the establishment of a Public Service Commission (eventually set up
                   in 1926) for recruiting civil servants.
                 y Separation of Budgets: Separated Provincial budgets from the Central budget for the first time, granting
                   provinces limited fiscal autonomy.
 Impacts         y Constitutional Milestone: Formally committed the British to the "gradual development of self-governing
                   institutions" in India.
                 y Political Awareness: Despite flaws, the elections and ministerial roles provided Indians with first-hand
                   experience in parliamentary democracy and administration.
                 y Administrative Devolution: Marked a significant step toward Federalism by clearly demarcating Central
                   and Provincial subjects.
 Limitations     y Failure of Diarchy: The "Transferred" subjects (Education, Health) lacked funds, while the "Reserved"
                   subjects (Finance, Police) held real power, making ministers "powerless symbols."
                 y Viceroy’s Autocracy: The Governor-General retained the power to certify bills rejected by the legislature
                   and issue ordinances, maintaining ultimate control.
                 y Governor's Interference: In provinces, the Governor could override ministers' decisions, rendering the
                   concept of "Responsible Government" a facade.
                 y Narrow Franchise: Only about 1-3% of the total population was granted the right to vote, keeping the
                   masses away from the political process.

6. Government of India Act, 1935
The Government of India Act, 1935, is the most voluminous and significant piece of legislation in British Indian history. The Act
provided the "Structural Blueprint" of our current Constitution.


 Category                                            Features / Description
Features      y All-India Federation: Proposed a federation of British Provinces and Princely States (never materialised
                as States refused to join).
              y Division of Powers: Divided legislative powers into three lists: Federal, Provincial, and Concurrent,
                with residuary powers vested in the Viceroy.
              y Provincial Autonomy: Abolished Diarchy in provinces and introduced "Responsible Government,"
                making ministers accountable to the provincial legislature.
              y Diarchy at the Centre: Introduced Diarchy at the federal level; ‘Reserved’ subjects (Defense, Foreign
                Affairs, Ecclesiastical Affairs) vs. ‘Transferred’ subjects (like Local Government, Forests, Education,
                and Health).
              y Institutional Foundations: Established the Federal Court (1937), Reserve Bank of India (1935), and
                Federal/Provincial Public Service Commissions.
              y Bicameralism: Introduced two houses in 6 out of 11 provinces (Bengal, Bombay, Madras, Bihar, Assam,
                and United Provinces).
              y Expansion of Franchise: Voting rights were extended to approximately 30 million people or roughly
                10% of the population. The extension was based on criteria such as property, education, and income,
                which significantly increased the electorate compared to previous reforms.
              y Abolition of the Council of India: The Council of India, established under the Government of India Act
                of 1858, was abolished and replaced by a team of advisors for the Secretary of State for India, thereby
                centralising administrative control.
Impacts       y Constitutional Legacy: Provided the administrative structure (Federal scheme, Office of Governor,
                Judiciary, Emergency powers) for Independent India.
              y Democratic Rehearsal: The 1937 elections under this Act allowed the Congress to form ministries,
                proving Indian capability in governance and policy-making.
              y End of ‘Whitehall’ Grip: Significantly reduced the authority of the Secretary of State over Indian
                affairs, shifting the focus to Indian soil.
Limitations   y ‘All Brake, No Engine’: Jawaharlal Nehru famously described it this way because while it gave
                "autonomy," the Viceroy/Governors held massive discretionary powers.
              y The "Safe-Guards" Trap: British Governors could dismiss ministers or take over administration
                (Section 93), making provincial autonomy "controlled autonomy".
              y Social Fragmentation: Further extended separate electorates to, Women, and Labour, deepening social
                divisions.
              y Rigid Amendment Process: The power to amend the Act remained solely with the British Parliament;
                Indians had no say in their own constitutional evolution.
subs: 5.1 Acts and Regulations under East India Company
5.2 Acts and Regulations under British Crown

chapters.text:
6              British Policies in India

6.1 Economic Policies of British in India
British economic policies (mid-18th century–1947) were driven by colonial exploitation, resource extraction, and market
expansion. By promoting the export of raw materials and import of British manufactures, they led to deindustrialisation,
economic dependence, and widespread impoverishment. As John Sullivan remarked, “Our system acts very much like a sponge,
drawing up all the good things from the banks of the Ganges and squeezing them down on the banks of the Thames.”
                                              UPSC Mains PYQs
     1.     Examine critically the various facets of economic policies of the British in India from the
            mid-eighteenth century till independence. (2014)

1. Phases of Economic Policies of the British in India
      Phases                                                       Description
 Mercantilist Phase    y Direct exploitation where surplus Indian revenues were used to purchase Indian finished goods
 (1757-1813)             for export to England.
                       y Significant wealth drain from India, contributing 2-3% of Britain’s national income and financing
                         Britain’s Industrial Revolution.
                       y After acquiring diwani rights in 1765, focus was on maximizing agricultural revenue through
                         various land revenue experiments.
 Mercantile            y Also called the Colonialism of Free Trade phase, beginning with the Charter Act of 1813 which
 Capitalism Phase        ended the Company’s trade monopoly.
 (1813-1858)           y India became a supplier of raw materials and a market for British manufactured goods.
                       y Resulted in deindustrialization and the establishment of a colonial economy centered on British
                         interests.
 Finance Capitalism    y Marked by direct Crown rule after 1858 and the rise of financial imperialism.
 Phase (1858-1947)     y British capital was organised through banks and export-import firms, consolidating exploitation.
                       y Expansion of infrastructure and financial institutions to serve imperial economic interests.

2. Economic policies of British India between 1757 and 1857
  Economic Policy                                                  Description
 Commercialisation       The introduction of new land relations and revenue systems (such as permanent settlement)
 of Agriculture          shifted agricultural production from subsistence to market-orientated cash crops like indigo and
                         cotton. Peasants were compelled to pay heavy land revenue and debts, faced exploitation by
                         middlemen, and suffered economic instability due to volatile global prices.
 Railways                Initiated by Lord Dalhousie in 1853, railways were constructed primarily to serve British colonial
                         interests by linking interior raw material sources to ports for export. Funded by Indian revenues
                         and British capital with guaranteed returns, railways facilitated military movement and imperial
                         control rather than internal development.
 Deindustrialisation     British policies imposed high tariffs on Indian goods while flooding India with cheap machine-
                         made British products. This destroyed indigenous handicraft industries, caused widespread
                         unemployment, and increased pressure on agriculture, leading to severe poverty and economic
                         decline.

3. Negative Impacts of Economic Policies of British in India
      Impact                               Description                                            Example
 Disruption of         British policies dismantled India’s self-reliant           The imposition of British legal and
 Traditional           economy, turning it into a supplier of raw materials       property systems eroded communal land
 Economy               and a market for British goods, harming local              rights in tribal areas like Chotanagpur,
                       industries and livelihoods.                                disrupting indigenous economies.


Decline of Artisans   The influx of cheap British goods and expanded              The muslin industry in Dacca (Bengal)
and Craftsmen         railway access led to the rapid decline of Indian           collapsed due to competition from
                      handicrafts, while the loss of traditional patrons          Manchester textiles and punitive colonial
                      further worsened the plight of artisans.                    taxation.
Impoverishment        Heavy land revenue demands caused severe hardship           Under the Ryotwari system in Madras
of Peasantry          for peasants, making them vulnerable to exploitation        Presidency, many peasants lost land due to
                      by zamindars and landlords, pushing many into debt          inability to pay fixed revenues, as reported
                      and economic insecurity.                                    by Famine Commission Reports 1901. (Sir
                                                                                  Antony (Anthony) MacDonnell)
Ruin of Zamindars     Many traditional zamindars faced financial collapse due     In Bengal, the Permanent Settlement of
and New               to revenue auctions and strict collection laws, leading     1793 resulted in the auction of zamindari
Landlordism           to land transfers to wealthier classes and the              estates when revenue targets weren’t met,
                      emergence of new landlords who intensified peasant          creating absentee landlordism.
                      burdens.
Agricultural          Overcrowding, high revenue demands, and                     The Report of the Royal Commission on
Stagnation            exploitative landlordism led to stagnation and decline      Agriculture (1928) highlighted poor
                      in agricultural productivity, worsening the conditions      productivity and lack of irrigation and
                      of farmers.                                                 investment in Indian agriculture.
Emergence of          While industries like textiles and coal mining              The jute industry in Bengal and tea
British dominated     developed on a large scale, they were largely               plantations in Assam were dominated by
Modern Industries     controlled by British capital, limiting Indian              British companies with discriminatory
                      participation and hindering indigenous industrial           labour and profit practices.
                      growth.
Poverty and           British economic policies contributed to widespread         The Great Famine of 1876-78 caused over
Famines               poverty and recurrent famines in the late 19th              5 million deaths; the Sir Richard Strachey
                      century, exacerbating food shortages and deepening          commission.(1880) cited revenue pressures
                      India’s economic backwardness.                              and grain export during food scarcity.

4. Positive Impact of Economic Policies of British in India
     Impact                                Description                                             Example
Introduction of       British policies promoted the cultivation of commercial     Tea plantations flourished in Assam and
Commercial            crops like tea, coffee, indigo, cotton, jute, and           Darjeeling under British initiatives; India
Crops                 sugarcane, integrating India into global trade networks     became one of the world’s leading tea
                      and generating export revenue.                              exporters by the early 20th century.
Development of        The British established extensive infrastructure, notably   By 1947, India had over 55,000 km of
Infrastructure        the railway network, telegraphs, roads, and ports,          railway tracks; the first train ran between
                      which improved connectivity, facilitated trade, and laid    Bombay and Thane in 1853.
                      the foundation for India’s future economic growth.
Modernization         The introduction of modern technologies such as             The Indian Telegraph Act was enacted in
and Technology        steamships, telegraphs, and railways transformed the        1885, and by 1902, a wireless telegraphy
Transfer              economic landscape, enhancing communication and             system was tested between Calcutta and the
                      transport efficiency.                                       Andaman Islands.
Shift to a            The British introduced a uniform currency system            The introduction of the Rupee coinage
Monetary              and helped transition India from a barter to a              system and the Paper Currency Act of 1861
Economy               monetary economy, facilitating market transactions          centralised monetary control and
                      and economic integration.                                   streamlined transactions.
Rise of a Modern      British rule led to the emergence of an educated middle     Reformers like Raja Ram Mohan Roy and
Middle Class          class that played a crucial role in India’s social          leaders like Dadabhai Naoroji emerged
                      reform movements and later in the struggle for              from the new educated class fostered by
                      independence and industrial development.                    English education and British institutions.


5. Economic Critiques of British Imperialism
         Critic                                                    Description
 Dadabhai Naoroji         y Formulated the 'Drain of Wealth' theory in his landmark work ‘Poverty and the Un-British
 (1825-1917)                Rule in India (1901)’.
 (Grand Old Man           y Highlighted continuous outflow of Indian wealth to Britain via salaries, pensions, military
 of India)                  expenses, and company profits.
                          y Estimated nearly one-third of India’s revenue was drained, causing economic
                            impoverishment.


 Romesh Chandra           y Authored ‘Economic History of India’.
 Dutt (1848-1909)         y Analysed destructive impact of British policies on economy.
                          y Focused on deindustrialisation and agricultural decline.
                          y Argued exploitative taxation and oppressive land revenue systems destroyed indigenous
                            industries.
                          y Highlighted railways facilitated influx of British goods, worsening economic drain.
 Mahadev Govind           y Described India’s economy as “dependent colonial".
 Ranade (1842-1901)       y He argued India became a raw material supplier and consumer of British goods.
                          y Emphasised the need for industrialisation and infrastructure development to revive the
                            economy.
 Gopal Krishna            y Criticised the disproportionate tax burden on India.
 Gokhale (1866-1915)      y Called for a fairer fiscal system and reduced military expenditure.
                          y Advocated investment in education and infrastructure for economic progress.
 G. Subramaniya Iyer      y As an economic nationalist, he argued India’s economic backwardness was due to British
 (1855-1916)                colonisation.
                          y He emphasised protecting indigenous industries and supported diversification of industries
                            while advocating reduced dependence on agriculture.
                          y Criticised exploitation of peasants by middlemen.

British policies transformed India’s economy by undermining traditional systems, burdening peasants, and promoting
deindustrialisation. Thinkers like Dadabhai Naoroji exposed the wealth drain, resulting in widespread poverty and enduring
challenges that continued beyond independence. As Naoroji aptly stated, 'India is to be bled in the interests of England,'
capturing the exploitative essence of colonial economic rule.


6.2 British Education System in India
The British education system in India was designed to create a class of Indians who would assist in colonial administration. As
Lord Macaulay stated, it aimed to produce individuals “Indian in blood and colour, but English in tastes, in opinions, in morals,
and in intellect". Introduced through the 1835 English Education Act and Wood’s Despatch (1854), it prioritised Western knowledge
over indigenous learning systems.

1. Objectives of British Education System in India
       Objective                                                        Description
 Administrative              To produce a cost-effective, loyal class of "intermediaries" (clerks/babus) to staff the lower rungs
 Convenience                 of the vast colonial bureaucracy.
 Cultural Colonisation       To create a class of Indians "Indian in blood and colour, but English in taste" (Macaulay’s
                             Minute), ensuring the psychological dominance of the West.
 Economic Market             To cultivate Western tastes and lifestyles among Indians, thereby transforming them into
 Expansion                   consumers of British manufactured goods (Wood’s Despatch).
 Political Stability         To use the "Downward Filtration Theory" to educate a select elite who would act as a buffer
                             between the British rulers and the Indian masses.
 Moral & Institutional       To "civilise" the subjects by replacing indigenous learning with "superior" European science/
 Legitimacy                  literature, justifying the British "Civilizing Mission".
 Ideological Hegemony        To instil a sense of inferiority regarding traditional knowledge and language, making British rule
                             appear as a necessary "modernising" force.

2. Education Policies during British Rule
a. Early Colonial Interventions (Pre-1857)
    Policy                                                        Description
 Charter Act      y It marked the first official British commitment to Indian education, allocating ₹1 lakh annually for the
 of 1813            "revival of literature and encouragement of learned natives".
                  y It sparked the Orientalist-Anglicist debate, with Orientalists like William Jones, Dr H.H. Wilson and H.T.
                    Prinsep advocating for Sanskrit, Persian, and Arabic education, while Anglicists like Thomas Macaulay
                    pushed for Western education in English.
 Macaulay’s       y Declared English as the medium of instruction for higher education, dismissing Indian literature as
 Minute             inferior.
 (1835)           y It introduced the Downward Filtration Theory, aiming to educate a small elite to disseminate Western
                    ideas to the masses.
                  y Persian was replaced by English in courts gradually, and funding for traditional institutions like madrasas
                    was curtailed, prioritising English schools.
b. Wood’s Despatch (1854)
     Recommendations                                                        Description
 Department of Public                Recommended the creation of a DPI in each province to supervise and manage
 Instruction (DPI)                   education, ensuring centralised oversight and structured administration.
 Establishment of Universities       Proposed universities at Calcutta, Bombay, and Madras, modelled on the University of
                                     London, to confer degrees and standardise higher education.
 Graded Education Structure          Advocated for a hierarchical system of education (elementary, middle, and high schools)
                                     to ensure a broad educational base across regions.
 Promotion of Vernacular             Emphasised instruction in vernaculars at the elementary level to expand access to
 Education                           primary education among rural and poor children.
 English for Higher Education        Promoted English as the medium of instruction for higher education to spread
                                     European knowledge, science, and philosophy.
 Training of Teachers                Called for the establishment of teacher training institutions to improve the quality of
                                     education and standardise pedagogy across schools.


Grant-in-Aid System               Introduced financial aid to private and missionary-run schools based on performance,
                                  helping expand the educational infrastructure.
Moral and Practical               Aimed to instil moral character and practical skills, producing educated Indians suitable
Education                         for administration and commerce.
Support for Women’s               Indirectly encouraged women’s education through general expansion, though it was not a
Education                         central theme of the Despatch.
Curriculum Based on               Declared the objective of education was to diffuse “the arts, science, philosophy, and
European Knowledge                literature of Europe", emphasising utilitarian and cultural goals.
c. Post-1857 Reforms and Commissions
        Reform                                                       Description
Hunter Commission            Focused on expanding primary education through local boards to improve access in rural
(1882)                       areas. It divided secondary education into academic streams for university preparation and
                             vocational streams for practical skills, aiming to address the neglect of mass education.
Indian Universities Act      Tightened government control over universities to curb rising nationalist sentiments
(1904)                       among students. It restructured university governance, limiting academic autonomy and
                             aligning education with colonial interests, as seen in increased oversight of curricula.
Saddler Commission           Recommended the intermediate college system, which evolved into the modern 10+2+3
(1917)                       educational structure. It emphasised improving university education quality and access,
                             addressing disparities in higher education opportunities across regions.
Hartog Committee (1929)      Criticised the rapid expansion of education that led to declining standards. It advocated for
                             vocational training post-primary education to equip students with practical skills, addressing
                             the unemployment among educated youth.
d. Nationalist Alternatives and Late-Colonial Policies
   Policy                                                       Description
Wardha           y Proposed by Mahatma Gandhi, the Wardha Scheme (also known as Nai Talim or Basic Education)
Scheme (1937)      aimed to create a self-reliant, culturally rooted education system countering the alienation of British
                   English education.
                 Key features include:
                 y Mother-Tongue Instruction: Emphasised education in the child’s native language to ensure
                   accessibility and cultural relevance, rejecting English as the sole medium.
                 y Vocational Training through Handicrafts: Integrated practical skills like spinning, weaving, and
                   carpentry to promote self-sufficiency, as seen in Gandhi’s focus on khadi production.
                 y Free and Compulsory Education: Targeted children aged 7-14, aiming for universal access to foster
                   mass education, unlike the elite-focused British system.
                 y Holistic Development: Focused on physical, mental, and moral growth, with education centred on the
                   child’s environment and community, as practiced in experimental schools like Sevagram.
                 y Social and Civic Responsibility: Aimed to instill values of equality, non-violence, and community
                   service, preparing students for nation-building, evident in its anti-caste and anti-communal ethos.
                 y Decentralised Implementation: Envisioned village-based schools managed by local communities,
                   reducing dependence on colonial structures.
                 y The scheme sought to restore cultural pride and practical learning but faced challenges due to limited
                   funding and opposition from colonial authorities.
Sargent Plan     Envisioned free primary education for ages 6-11 and expanded technical education to prepare India for
(1944)           industrialisation. Though ambitious, it was never fully implemented due to World War II and financial
                 constraints, limiting its impact on mass education.

3. Features of the British Education System

   Feature                                                      Description
English as         English was prioritised as the medium of instruction for higher education post-1835, creating an elite
Medium             class fluent in the colonial language. English replaced Persian in courts, with textbooks introduced to
                   standardise curricula, as seen in the establishment of colleges like Presidency College.


 Structured           The system established universities in Calcutta, Bombay, and Madras (1857) and graded schools
 Institutions         (high, middle, elementary) with formal curricula and annual examinations, as formalised by Wood’s
                      Despatch, ensuring uniformity in education delivery.
 Centralised          Education targeted a select few, expecting elites to trickle down Western knowledge to the masses. This
 Filtration           approach, evident in Macaulay’s Minute, aimed to create a loyal intermediary class but failed to reach
 Theory               broader society.
 Neglect of           Higher education sidelined Indian languages, alienating the masses, despite vernacular schools being
 Vernaculars          promoted post-1854. Macaulay’s dismissal of vernaculars as “poor and rude” in his infamous Minute
                      on Indian Education (1835) limited their use in intellectual pursuits, restricting education access.
 Regulated            Indigenous pathshalas were reformed with fixed fees, timetables, and textbooks, reducing their
 Pathshalas           flexibility. These changes, post-1854, made education less accessible for poor students who needed to
                      work during harvest times, as noted in reports from Bengal and Bihar.

4. Impact of British Education System

        Impact                                                         Description
 Growth of                  English education introduced Western ideas of freedom, democracy, and equality, fostering national
 Nationalism                consciousness. Reformers like Raja Rammohan Roy used rationalism from Western learning to
                            advocate for social changes, such as banning sati, sparking socio-political movements.
 Administrative             Created a class of English-educated Indians who served as clerks and "babus", strengthening
 Efficiency                 British governance. This class, trained in colleges like Hindu College, facilitated bureaucratic
                            control and communication across colonial offices.
 Intellectual               Exposure to Western literature and science broadened intellectual horizons, promoting
 Awakening                  rationalism and questioning traditional practices. The establishment of the printing press and
                            institutions like Elphinstone College disseminated new ideas, influencing thinkers like Gopal
                            Krishna Gokhale.
 Social Reforms             English education inspired movements against social evils like caste discrimination and widow
                            remarriage. Reformers like Vidyasagar, educated in Western ideas, founded girls’ schools and
                            advocated for women’s rights, transforming societal norms.

5. Limitations of British Education System
     Limitation                                                       Description
 Neglect of Mass          Focused on elites, leaving the majority illiterate, with only ~6% literacy by 1911 and ~8% by 1921.
 Education                The filtration theory failed to disseminate education, as seen in the limited reach of English-medium
                          schools to rural areas.
 Neglect of Female        Women were largely excluded, with no early funds allocated for girls’ education. Reformers like
 Education                Bethune later established girls’ schools, but systemic neglect persisted, perpetuating gender
                          disparities in literacy and opportunities.
 Limited Scientific       Emphasis on humanities over science and technology hindered progress. By 1857, only one
 Education                engineering college (Roorkee) and three medical colleges existed, limiting India’s capacity for
                          industrial and scientific advancement.
 Cultural Alienation      English education distanced elites from Indian culture, creating a sense of inferiority. Mahatma
                          Gandhi criticised it for making Indians “strangers in their own lands", as the educated admired
                          British culture over indigenous traditions.
 Decline of               Replaced flexible pathshalas and madrasas, serving millions, with a rigid system. Post-1854
 Indigenous System        reforms imposed strict rules, reducing accessibility for poor students, as evident in the decline of
                          village pathshalas in Bengal and Bihar.
 Economic Disparity       Education favoured urban elites, widening economic gaps. Rural populations, excluded from
                          English education, remained economically backward, as seen in the limited vocational training
                          offered until the Hartog Committee’s recommendations.
The British education system in India laid the foundation for modern education but served colonial interests by promoting
English and Western values while undermining traditional knowledge. It created a class of English-educated Indians who
later became catalysts for socio-political reforms and the freedom movement, ironically using the very tools of education
introduced to subjugate them.


6.3 Land Revenue System in British India
The land revenue system in British India was a fundamental policy designed to maximise revenue collection from agriculture,
which was the primary source of income for the colonial government. It comprised the Ijaradari System, Permanent Settlement
or Zamindari System, Ryotwari System, and Mahalwari System, each differing in the method of revenue collection and land
ownership and implemented in different regions of India.

1. Ijaradari System of 1772
The Ijaradari system was a revenue farming system introduced by Warren Hastings in 1772 in Bengal. This system was prevalent
mainly in Bengal, Bihar, and Odisha during the early British period before being replaced by the Permanent Settlement in 1793.
a. Features of Ijaradari System
          Aspect                                                        Description
 Auction-based revenue          The right to collect land revenue was auctioned annually or for a fixed period to the highest
 collection rights              bidder.
 Non-hereditary tenure          The revenue collection rights were not hereditary but temporary, usually for 1 to 5 years.
 Revenue farming system         Contractors (Ijardars) paid a fixed sum to the government and collected revenue from
                                peasants themselves.
 Focus on maximum               Ijaradars aimed to extract as much revenue as possible from farmers to recover their bid
 revenue extraction             and earn profit.
 Government outsourced          The British government delegated tax collection to private contractors rather than direct
 revenue collection             collection.

2. Permanent Settlement (Zamindari System) of 1793
The Permanent Settlement System was introduced by Lord Cornwallis in 1793. It was primarily implemented in Bengal, Bihar,
and Orissa, and later extended to parts of northern Madras and the Varanasi region.
a. Features of the Permanent Settlement
        Aspect                                                        Description
 Fixed Land                The land revenue to be paid by zamindars to the British government was fixed permanently and
 Revenue                   could not be increased.
 Zamindars as              Zamindars were recognised as hereditary owners of the land with rights to sell, transfer, or
 Landowners                mortgage it.
 Revenue Collection        Zamindars were responsible for collecting revenue from the peasants (ryots) and paying the fixed
 through Zamindars         amount to the government.
 Revenue Share             The government’s share was about 10/11th of the revenue, while zamindars retained 1/11th as
                           their profit.
 Sunset Law (Payment       Zamindars had to pay the fixed revenue by a specified date (sunset); failure to pay led to
 Deadline)                 confiscation and auction of their land.
 Tenant Status             Peasants became tenants with no ownership rights; zamindars issued pattas (written agreements)
                           specifying rent and land details.
 Limited Government        The British government did not interfere in zamindar-peasant relations, leaving zamindars free to
 Intervention              manage tenants.

3. Ryotwari System of 1820
Alexander Read began experiments around 1792, but the Ryotwari System was developed and widely implemented under Thomas
Munro in the early nineteenth century (1820s). It was primarily implemented in the Madras and Bombay Presidencies, where
land revenue was assessed based on detailed surveys of individual holdings and collected directly from the cultivators in cash.
a. Features of the Ryotwari System
           Aspect                                                        Description
 Direct Relationship between      Land revenue was collected directly from individual cultivators (ryots), eliminating
 Government and Ryots             intermediaries like zamindars.


 Ownership Rights to Ryots        Ryots were granted ownership and occupancy rights, with freedom to sell, mortgage, or
                                  transfer land.
 Variable and Periodic            Revenue demands were not fixed permanently; they were periodically revised based on
 Revenue Assessment               land productivity.
 High Revenue Rates               Tax rates ranged from 45% to 60% of the estimated agricultural produce, often paid in cash.

4. Mahalwari Settlement of 1822
The Mahalwari Settlement, introduced by Holt Mackenzie in 1822 and later modified by Lord William Bentinck, was a
land revenue system implemented mainly in the North-Western Provinces, Punjab, United Provinces (UP), and Central
Provinces.
a. Features of Mahalwari Settlement
         Aspect                                                             Description
 Collective Responsibility     Land revenue was collected from a group of villages or an estate called a "Mahal", with
                               collective responsibility for payment.
 Village Headman as            The village headman (lambardar) or local landlords were responsible for collecting and paying
 Revenue Collector             revenue to the government.
 Ownership Rights              The system recognised the village community as the collective owner of the land, while
                               individual cultivators held personal ownership rights over their respective plots.
 Periodic Revision of          Revenue demands were not fixed permanently but revised periodically (usually every 20-30
 Revenue                       years) based on land productivity.
 State’s Share of Revenue      Initially set at about 66% of the rental value, it was later reduced to around 50% to ease the
                               peasant burden.
 Combination of Zamindari      Incorporated features of both systems → village-level collective responsibility like Zamindari
 and Ryotwari Elements         and direct peasant rights like Ryotwari.

5. Issues of British Land Revenue Systems (Zamindari, Ryotwari, Mahalwari)
      Aspect                        Description                                                Example
 Exorbitant and          Oppressive revenue rates exacted             Ijaradari: Revenue demand spiked by 80% post-1765
 Inflexible Taxation     ruthlessly, with no waivers granted          (Diwani), exacerbating the Great Bengal Famine (1770).
                         during droughts, famines, or crop            Ryotwari: ~50-60% tax in Madras.
                         failures.                                    Mahalwari: Brutal extraction continued even during the
                                                                      devastating 1837-38 Doab Famine.
 Land Alienation         Land became a marketable                     Zamindari: Arbitrary peasant evictions in Bengal.
 and Displacement        commodity. Defaulting on revenue             Mahalwari: Massive land transfer to Sahukars and
                         led to mass evictions, confiscations,        Taluqdars in Awadh.
                         and a surge in landless agricultural
                         labourers.
 Rise of Exploitative    Created a parasitic chain of non-            Zamindari: Rampant absentee landlordism and sub-
 Intermediaries          cultivating rent-receivers, deeply           infeudation.
                         entrenching rural indebtedness and           Ryotwari: Direct exploitation by vicious moneylenders (debt
                         the grip of moneylenders.                    traps).
 Agricultural            Complete absence of capital                  Ijaradari: Annual auction-based leases actively discouraged
 Stagnation              investment in land improvement or            sustainable land management/investment.
                         irrigation by the colonial state,            Zamindari: Landlords focused solely on rent extraction,
                         landlords, or impoverished peasants.         ignoring agricultural modernisation, causing long-term yield
                                                                      stagnation.
 Administrative          Characterised by flawed                      Ijaradari: Bidding wars led to inflated, uncollectible
 Coercion and            assessments, extortion by revenue            demands; corrupt European supervisors lacked local
 Corruption              officials, high collection costs, and        knowledge.
                         systemic legal harassment.                   Mahalwari: Assessments based on arbitrary guesswork.
                                                                      Zamindari: Explosion of rent-related litigation severely
                                                                      choked Bengal courts.


 Deepening of            Deliberately empowered traditional         Mahalwari: Disproportionately empowered village headmen
 Social Inequalities     elites and intermediaries to create        (Lambardars).
                         loyalists, heavily skewing rural           Zamindari: Consolidated economic power among hereditary
                         power dynamics.                            elites.

The British land revenue system in India drastically transformed the agrarian structure, often prioritising revenue extraction
over the welfare of cultivators, which led to widespread rural distress and economic hardship. It is often said that the British
could only have conquered such a vast country by heavily taxing the peasants, highlighting the oppressive nature of these
policies and their lasting impact on Indian society.
subs: 6.1 Economic Policies of British in India
6.3 Land Revenue System in British India
pyq: Examine critically the various facets of economic policies of the British in India from the mid-eighteenth century till independence. (2014)

Negative Impacts of Economic Policies of British in India Impact Description Example Disruption of British policies dismantled India’s self-reliant The imposition of British legal and Traditional economy, turning it into a supplier of raw materials property systems eroded communal land Economy and a market for British goods, harming local rights in tribal areas like Chotanagpur, industries and livelihoods. disrupting indigenous economies. Decline of Artisans The influx of cheap British goods and expanded The muslin industry in Dacca (Bengal) and Craftsmen railway access led to the rapid decline of Indian collapsed due to competition from handicrafts, while the loss of traditional patrons Manchester textiles and punitive colonial further worsened the plight of artisans. taxation. Impoverishment Heavy land revenue demands caused severe hardship Under the Ryotwari system in Madras of Peasantry for peasants, making them vulnerable to exploitation Presidency, many peasants lost land due to by zamindars and landlords, pushing many into debt inability to pay fixed revenues, as reported and economic insecurity. by Famine Commission Reports 1901. (Sir Antony (Anthony) MacDonnell) Ruin of Zamindars Many traditional zamindars faced financial collapse due In Bengal, the Permanent Settlement of and New to revenue auctions and strict collection laws, leading 1793 resulted in the auction of zamindari Landlordism to land transfers to wealthier classes and the estates when revenue targets weren’t met, emergence of new landlords who intensified peasant creating absentee landlordism. burdens.
