chapters.t: Governor Generals and Viceroys of India
chapters.text:
7               Governor Generals
                        and Viceroys of India
The Governor-Generals and Viceroys of India were the highest British officials in colonial India, acting as both the representative
of the British Crown and the head of the British administration. This position combined executive, legislative, and military
authority, playing a central role in governing India during the British Raj.
                                                 UPSC Mains PYQs
      1.    Evaluate the policies of Lord Curzon and their long-term implications on the national
            movement. (2020)
      2.    "In many ways, Lord Dalhousie was the founder of modern India." Elaborate. (2013)


1. Governor Generals of Bengal (1773-1835)

a. Warren Hastings (1773-1785)
As the first Governor General of Bengal (1773-1785), Warren Hastings centralised British administration, promoted cultural studies,
and faced wars, laying the foundation for colonial governance.

    Key Policies                                                      Description
 Regulating Act           Established centralised governance under the Governor General, strengthening British control. Laid
 (1773)                   the administrative framework for colonial rule, enhancing political stability.
                          Example: As the Governor-General, he oversaw the establishment of the Supreme Court at Fort
                          William (1774).
 Asiatic Society of       Founded by British philologist and judge Sir William Jones to promote "Oriental research",
 Bengal (1784)            specifically to study the history, antiquities, arts, sciences, and literature of Asia.
                          Example: He actively patronised William Jones to found the Society and wrote the introduction to the
                          first English translation of the Bhagavad Gita (1785).
 Diwani and               Set up district-level courts for civil and criminal justice. Streamlined judicial administration,
 Fauzdari Adalats         integrating Indian legal traditions with British oversight.
                          Example: Hastings promulgated the Judicial Plan of 1772, establishing Sadar Diwani and Nizamat
                          Adalats in Calcutta.
 First Maratha War        Fought to expand British influence, ending with the Treaty of Salbai (1782). Demonstrated British
 (1775-82)                military ambition, straining relations with Indian powers.
                          Example: He dispatched the Bengal Army under General Goddard to rescue the Bombay Presidency,
                          ultimately negotiating the Treaty of Salbai (1782).

b. Lord Cornwallis (1786-1793)
Cornwallis, known as the “Father of Civil Services”, introduced key revenue and judicial reforms, shaping colonial administration
while fighting the Third Mysore War.

     Key Policies                                            Description & Significance
 Permanent                 Fixed land revenue permanently with zamindars as owners and cultivators as tenants. Ensured
 Settlement (1793)         regular revenue for the British but led to peasant exploitation and absentee landlordism.
 Civil Services            Introduced merit-based civil services, banned private trade/gifts, increased salaries to reduce
 Reforms                   corruption, and reserved senior posts for Europeans. Strengthened administrative integrity and laid
                           the foundation for the Indian Civil Services.
                           Example: Cornwallis formalised the Covenanted Civil Services, enforcing strict Europeanisation
                           by barring Indians from all high-ranking administrative posts.
 Judicial Reforms          Separated the judiciary from revenue functions; set up a structured court system, including Circuit
 (Cornwallis Code)         Courts and Sadar Adalats. Introduced the Rule of Law and the separation of powers in India
                           Example: He abolished District Faujdari Courts and transferred criminal judicial powers from
                           District Collectors to Provincial Courts of Circuit.


 Police Reforms            Created a regular police force with darogas under district SPs, replacing zamindari policing.
                           Modernized law enforcement and improved accountability.
                           Example: Cornwallis stripped Zamindars of their policing powers and divided districts into Thanas,
                           each headed by an Indian Daroga.
 Educational               Cornwallis officially approved and funded the establishment of the Sanskrit College at Benares
 Initiative                (1791), acting on the proposal of his resident Jonathan Duncan to promote traditional Indian
                           learning. Signalled early British interest in managing cultural education.
 Military Leadership       Led British forces in the Third Anglo-Mysore War (1790-92) against Tipu Sultan. Secured
                           significant territorial gains.
                           Example: He personally commanded the troops against Tipu Sultan and dictated the humiliating
                           Treaty of Seringapatam (1792).
 Pitt’s India Act          Granted Cornwallis overriding power over his council, enabling sweeping administrative reforms.
 Amendment (1786)          Centralised authority under the Governor-General.

c. Lord Wellesley (1798-1805)
Wellesley expanded British dominance through the Subsidiary Alliance, fought major wars, and established educational institutions,
consolidating colonial power.

    Key Policies                                                     Description
 Subsidiary Alliance     Forced Indian states to maintain British troops, ceding autonomy. Weakened Indian rulers and
                         ensured British hegemony without direct rule.
                         Example: Nizam of Hyderabad (1798) became the first signatory of the Subsidiary Alliance.
 Fort William            Established to train British officials in Indian languages and culture. Improved administrative
 College (1800)          efficiency, aiding colonial governance.
                         Example: Wellesley founded Fort William College to train Company civil servants, but the Court of
                         Directors later replaced it with Haileybury College in England.
 Fourth Mysore           Defeated Tipu Sultan, annexing parts of Mysore. Eliminated a major anti-British power,
 War (1799)              strengthening British control in southern India.
                         Example: He launched the preemptive war that resulted in Tipu's death at Seringapatam, subsequently
                         installing the Wodeyar dynasty under a Subsidiary Alliance.
 Second Maratha          Defeated Scindia, Bhonsle, and Holkar (confederacy of Maratha chiefs), expanding British
 War (1803-05)           influence. Crushed Maratha power, paving the way for British supremacy.
                         Example: He commanded aggressive campaigns through his brother Arthur Wellesley, forcing the
                         treaties of Surji-Anjangaon and Deogaon to severely cripple Maratha power.

d. Lord William Bentinck (1828-1835)
Governor General of Bengal (1828-1833) and India (1833-1835), Bentinck, one of the most reform-oriented Governor-Generals,
abolished sati, introduced English education, and annexed territories, shaping modern India.

   Key Policies                                                     Description
 Abolition of Sati     Banned the practice of sati, a major social reform. Empowered reform movements, challenging
 (1829)                oppressive customs and inspiring nationalism.
                       Example: Bentinck passed Regulation XVII of 1829, declaring the practice of Sati illegal and
                       punishable as culpable homicide.
 English               Adopted English as the official language, influenced by Macaulay’s Minute. Created an educated elite,
 Education             fostering nationalism and modern thought.
 (1835)                Example: Bentinck endorsed Macaulay’s Minute through the English Education Resolution (1835),
                       directing state funds exclusively towards English-medium Western education.
 Charter Act           Prohibited discrimination in employment based on religion or descent. Promoted equality, encouraging
 (1833)                Indian participation in administration.
                       Example: Bentinck became the first Governor-General of India and began integrating Indians into
                       subordinate judicial roles, like Deputy Magistrates.
 Annexation of         British control but caused resentment, contributing to anti-British sentiments.
 Territories           Example: Abandoning the policy of non-intervention, Bentinck annexed Mysore (1831) citing
                       misgovernance, and personally directed the bloodless annexation of Coorg (1834).


2. Governor Generals of India (1835-1858)
a. Lord Metcalfe (1835-1836)
Metcalfe, known as the “Liberator of Press”, briefly served, focusing on press freedom and cultural initiatives, with limited but
notable impact.

       Key Policies                                                     Description
 Press Freedom                Repealed Licensing Regulation (1823), removing press restrictions. Encouraged political
                              discourse, enabling nationalist ideas to spread.
                              Example: Metcalfe enacted the Press Act of 1835, officially abolishing John Adams' draconian
                              licensing rules.
 Calcutta Public Library      Metcalfe was instrumental in establishing this library to promote literacy and knowledge
 (1836)                       access. Supported educational growth, indirectly aiding intellectual awakening.
                              Example: He transferred 4,675 volumes from the College of Fort William, providing the core
                              collection that helped launch the Calcutta Public Library.

b. Lord Dalhousie (1848-1856)
Dalhousie modernised India through infrastructure and education, but his annexations sparked unrest, earning him the title “Founder
of Modern India”.

     Key Policies                                                     Description
 Wood’s Despatch           Under his tenure, a modern education system with universities and schools was established.
 (1854)                    Fostered education, creating nationalist leaders; foundational for modern India.
                           Example: Dalhousie implemented the Despatch by directing the creation of a dedicated Department
                           of Public Instruction (DPI) in every province.
 Railway and               Introduced railways (1853) and telegraphs, enhancing infrastructure. Integrated India
 Telegraph Reforms         economically, aiding national unity, but primarily served British interests.
                           Example: He authored the strategic "Railway Minute of 1853" outlining a pan-India network, and
                           inaugurated India's first commercial passenger train between Bombay and Thane.
 Widow Remarriage          Lord Dalhousie drafted and introduced the Hindu Widows' Remarriage Bill in the legislative
 Act (1856)                council, finally passed and legalised by Lord Canning, promoting social reform. Advanced
                           women’s rights, inspiring progressive movements.
                           Example: Acting on IC Vidyasagar's petitions, Lord Dalhousie drafted and introduced the Hindu
                           Widows' Remarriage Bill in the legislative council, finally passed and legalised by Lord Canning.
 Doctrine of Lapse         Annexed states without natural heirs, fuelled resentment, contributing to the 1857 Revolt,
                           shaping nationalist resistance.
                           Example: Annexed states like Satara (1848), Jaitpur and Sambalpur (1849), Baghat (1850), Chota
                           Udaipur (1852), Jhansi (1853), and Nagpur (1854).
 Postal Reforms            Standardised postal services via Post Office Act. Improved communication, facilitating
 (1854)                    administrative and nationalist networks.
                           Example: Dalhousie passed the Post Office Act (1854) introducing a uniform half-anna postage rate
                           across India, and personally oversaw the laying of 4,000 miles of telegraph lines connecting
                           Calcutta to Peshawar.

3. Viceroys of India (1858-1947)

a. Lord Canning (1856-1862)
Governor General (1856-1858), later first Viceroy (1858-1862), Canning navigated the 1857 Revolt, introduced social and
administrative reforms, and transitioned to Crown rule.

        Key Policies                                                      Description
 Hindu Widows                     Legalised widow remarriage, advancing social reform. Strengthened the reformist agenda,
 Remarriage Act (1856)            empowering women and inspiring social change.
                                  Example: Canning formally enacted the Hindu Widows' Remarriage Act, 1856 (drafted by his
                                  predecessor, Dalhousie) aligned with efforts of IC Vidyasagar to uplift women’s status.


 Establishment of                 Expanded modern education, fostering nationalist thought and intellectual growth.
 Universities (1857)              Example: Lord Canning implemented Wood’s Despatch by establishing the Universities of
                                  Calcutta, Bombay, and Madras in 1857.
 Government of India Act          Transferred rule from the East India Company to the Crown. Marked formal imperial
 (1858)                           control, reshaping governance and fuelling anti-British sentiments.
                                  Example: Lord Canning read Queen Victoria’s Proclamation at the Allahabad Durbar (1858),
                                  formally announcing the transfer of power to the Crown.
 Suppression of Revolts           Crushed the 1857 uprising with military force. Restored British control but deepened Indian
                                  resentment, igniting nationalism.
                                  He also suppressed the Indigo Revolt (1859-60), highlighting colonial oppression.

b. Lord Lytton (1876-1880)
Viceroy Lytton’s (1876-1880) repressive policies, including press censorship and Afghan wars, alienated Indians, while his
ceremonial actions highlighted British imperial ambitions.
    Key Policies                                                       Description
 Vernacular Press        Censored Indian-language newspapers. Curbed free speech, fuelling nationalist resentment and
 Act (1878)              political consciousness.
                         Example: Lytton targeted publications criticising British policies, like Amrita Bazar Patrika. It
                         famously converted overnight into an English newspaper to avoid the Act.
 Arms Act (1878)         Restricted Indians from possessing arms. Disarmed potential rebels, deepening distrust and anti-
                         British sentiment.
                         Example: Lytton enacted the Arms Act (1878), barring Indians from possessing firearms without a
                         license while exempting many Europeans.
 First Delhi Durbar      Proclaimed Queen Victoria Kaiser-i-Hind. Reinforced imperial authority but exposed colonial
 (1877)                  extravagance, alienating Indians.
                         Example: He organised the 1877 Durbar amid the devastating Madras Famine (1876-78), which
                         claimed millions of lives.
 Second Afghan           Fought to counter Russian influence. Drained resources, increasing Indian discontent with colonial
 War (1878-80)           priorities.
                         Example: He pursued ‘Forward Policy’ against Afghanistan, triggering the Second Anglo-Afghan War
                         (1878-80) and culminating in the Treaty of Gandamak (1879), securing a British Resident in Kabul.

c. Lord Ripon (1880-1884)
Viceroy Ripon (1880-1884), known as the “Father of Local Self-Governance”, introduced liberal reforms but faced backlash
over the Ilbert Bill, shaping nationalist aspirations.
   Key Policies                                                       Description
 Repeal of             Restored press freedom. Encouraged nationalist journalism, strengthening political discourse.
 Vernacular Press      Example: Newspapers like Som Prakash and Amrita Bazar Patrika were revived, earlier targeted by the
 Act (1882)            1878 Act.
 Local Self-           Decentralised administration through local bodies. Laid the foundation for democratic governance,
 Governance            empowering Indians.
 (1882)                Example: He issued Resolution of 1882, establishing municipalities and district boards for local
                       administration.
 Ilbert Bill (1883)    Proposed racial equality in judicial trials. Exposed British racial prejudice, galvanising Indian nationalism.
                       Example: He backed Law Member C.P. Ilbert's bill to allow Indian judges to try Europeans, but was
                       ultimately forced into a compromise.
 Hunter Education      Recommended expansion of primary education, greater role for local bodies in managing schools,
 Commission            encouragement of private educational institutions, and increased support for female and vocational
 (1882)                education.
 Factory Act           Improved labour conditions in factories. Initiated labour reforms, addressing industrial exploitation.
 (1881)                Examples:
                       y Child Labour Curbs: Prohibited the employment of children under the age of 7 and limited the workday for
                         children aged 7-12 to 9 hours.
                       y Mandatory Breaks: Introduced a required one-hour rest break during the workday and 4 compulsory days
                         off per month for children.


d. Lord Curzon (1899-1905)
Viceroy Curzon’s (1899-1905) administrative reforms modernised India, but his repressive Partition of Bengal intensified nationalism,
significantly impacting the freedom movement.

       Key Policies                                                      Description
 Partition of Bengal           Divided Bengal for “administrative efficiency". Sparked the Swadeshi Movement, uniting
 (1905)                        Indians against British rule, intensifying nationalism.
                               Example: Curzon conceptualized and executed the division of Bengal along communal lines
                               explicitly to break the political backbone of the Bengali nationalist intelligentsia.
 Indian Universities Act       Reformed higher education, centralising university governance. Improved education quality
 (1904)                        but limited Indian representation, causing resentment.
                               Example: Curzon enacted the Indian Universities Act (1904), based on the Raleigh Commission
                               (1902), tightening government control over universities and curbing the growth of nationalist
                               politics on campuses.
 Ancient Monuments             Protected historical sites. Preserved India’s heritage, boosting cultural pride.
 Preservation Act (1904)       Example: He appointed John Marshall as Director-General of the ASI, and personally oversaw
                               the halt of vandalism at heritage sites like the Taj Mahal.
 PUSA Agricultural             Established to advance agricultural research. Modernized agriculture, improving productivity
 Institute (1903)              but serving British economic interests.
                               Example: Curzon secured a £30,000 grant from American philanthropist Henry Phipps to
                               establish the Imperial Agricultural Research Institute at Pusa (Bihar).

e. Lord Hardinge II (1910-1916)
Viceroy Hardinge(1910-1916) reversed the Bengal Partition, shifted the capital to Delhi, and faced rising nationalism, navigating
India through a turbulent period.

     Key Policies                                                      Description
 Annulment of              Reversed the Bengal Partition to appease nationalists. Temporarily reduced tensions but failed to
 Bengal Partition          curb growing anti-British sentiment.
 (1911)                    Example: Bihar and Orissa were carved out as a separate province in 1912 by Hardinge II.
 Capital Shift to          Moved capital from Calcutta to Delhi. Symbolised imperial permanence but galvanised nationalist
 Delhi (1911)              opposition.
                           Example: He organised the Delhi Durbar III (1911) to celebrate King George V’s coronation.
 McMahon Line              Defined British India-Tibet border. Established geopolitical boundaries, shaping modern India’s
 (1914)                    territorial claims.
                           Example: Hardinge II authorised the Simla Conference (1913-14), directing his representative Sir
                           Henry McMahon to negotiate the boundary with Tibetan delegates.
 Response to Ghadar        Suppressed revolutionary activities. Highlighted rising nationalist militancy, prompting stricter
 Mutiny (1915)             British controls.
                           Example: Hardinge II enacted the draconian Defence of India Act (1915), establishing special
                           tribunals for the summary trial and execution of Ghadarite revolutionaries.

f. Lord Linlithgow (1936-1944)
Viceroy Linlithgow (1936-1944) introduced provincial autonomy but repressed the Quit India Movement and ignored the Bengal
Famine, intensifying anti-British sentiment.

   Key Policies                                                       Description
 Government of          Introduced provincial autonomy and federal framework. Shaped future governance, empowering
 India Act (1935)       Indians but limited by British control. However, GoI 1935 act was passed under Lord Wellington.
                        Example: Linlithgow oversaw the 1937 Provincial Elections but unilaterally dragged India into
                        WWII (1939) without consulting Indian ministries, prompting mass Congress resignations.
 August Offer           Proposed limited Indian participation in governance. Rejected by Congress, escalating demands for
 (1940)                 independence.
                        Example: Through the August Offer (1940), Linlithgow formally conceded the demand for a
                        Constituent Assembly for the first time to secure wartime cooperation.


 Suppression of        Crushed the August Revolution with force. Hardened nationalist resolve, strengthening independence
 Quit India            demands.
 Movement              Example: He ordered the preemptive arrests of the Congress Working Committee on August 9, 1942, a
 (1942)                swift colonial counter-insurgency operation (Operation Zero Hour).
 Bengal Famine         Failed to address famine, diverting food for war. Exposed colonial neglect, fuelling anti-British
 Response (1943)       anger.
                       Example: 3-4 million deaths due to food grain diversion.

g. Lord Mountbatten (1947-1948)
Viceroy Mountbatten (1947-1948) oversaw India’s independence and partition, navigating communal tensions to end British
rule, becoming the first Governor General of independent India.

      Key Policies                                                    Description
 June Third Plan (1947)      Announced the partition of India into India and Pakistan. Facilitated independence but led to
                             communal violence and displacement.
                             Example: Mountbatten personally negotiated the Mountbatten Plan with Nehru and Jinnah,
                             ruthlessly fast-tracking the British exit date from June 1948 to August 1947.
 Indian Independence         Legalised India’s independence and partition. Ended British rule, shaping modern India-
 Act (1947)                  Pakistan relations.
 Formation of Interim        Established Congress-led government. Transitioned to Indian self-rule, preparing for
 Government (1946)           independence.
                             Example: Mountbatten officially administered the oath of office to the first PM of independent
                             India, Pandit Nehru.
 Constituent Assembly        Facilitated elections for India’s constitution-making body. Laid the foundation for
 Elections (1946)            independent India’s governance structure.
                             Example: Lord Mountbatten addressed the Constituent Assembly on 15 August 1947, conveying
                             King George VI’s message formally establishing India as an independent Dominion.
The Governor-General and Viceroy of India held paramount significance as the highest representatives of British authority,
shaping colonial administration and policies that deeply influenced India’s political and social fabric, symbolising British
imperial dominance, but also became a focal point of Indian resistance. Their legacy of centralised governance and institutional
frameworks has continued to impact contemporary India’s administrative and constitutional systems, bridging the colonial
past with modern governance.
pyq: Evaluate the policies of Lord Curzon and their long-term implications on the national movement. (2020)
"In many ways, Lord Dalhousie was the founder of modern India." Elaborate. (2013)

chapters.text:
8                 Popular Resistance to Colonial
                          Rule
8.1 Civil uprisings
Colonial rule deeply affected Indian society, with the Indian ruling class and zamindars suffering most under British conquest.
Various localised uprisings led by deposed rulers, former officials, and feudal lords arose due to British policies like subsidiary
alliances, army discrimination, and exploitative economic measures, but these were brutally suppressed until culminating in the
larger 1857 Revolt.

1. Characteristics of Civil Uprisings

     Characteristic                                                      Description
 Localized and                  Most uprisings were confined to specific regions or communities rather than being widespread
 Small-Scale                    or coordinated across India.
 Led by Diverse Groups          Leadership came from deposed rulers, zamindars, feudal lords (poligars), peasants, tribal
                                groups, and discontented sepoys, reflecting broad social participation.
 Rooted in Multiple             The uprisings combined political (displacement of rulers), economic (exploitative taxation, land
 Grievances                     revenue settlements), social, and cultural causes (interference in religious and social customs).
 Reaction to British            They were primarily responses to British annexation policies, subsidiary alliances,
 Policies                       discriminatory military practices, and harsh revenue demands.
 Role of Tribal and             Tribal and peasant groups formed the backbone of many uprisings, resisting economic
 Peasant Communities            exploitation and loss of traditional rights.
 Religious and Cultural         Some movements had strong religious overtones or were motivated by the defence of
 Dimensions                     traditional customs and practices, like the Sanyasi and Wahhabi movements.

2. Causes of Civil Uprisings

          Cause                                                          Description
 Exploitative Taxation         The British East India Company imposed heavy land revenue demands and taxes, causing
 Policies                      widespread distress among peasants and landlords, leading to protests and uprisings.
                               Example: The Rangpur Dhing (1783) peasant revolt was primarily driven by the exploitative
                               Ijaradari system (annual revenue farming) and exorbitant tax demands.
 Displacement of Local         Annexation policies like the Subsidiary Alliance and Doctrine of Lapse and direct British
 Rulers                        control displaced many traditional rulers, creating resentment among the aristocracy and their
                               supporters.
                               Example: The Velu Thampi revolt of 1809 in the Kingdom of Travancore was primarily
                               triggered by the oppressive terms of the Subsidiary Alliance (the Treaty of 1805).
 Economic Exploitation         British policies favoured their manufactured goods and imposed duties on Indian products,
 and Destruction of            devastating local industries and causing unemployment and poverty.
 Indigenous Industries         Example: The Surat Salt Agitation (1840) protested British salt taxes.
 Corruption and Harsh          British officials and their allies, including landlords and moneylenders, exploited the rural
 Attitude of Officials         population through corruption and oppressive behaviour, fuelling anger and rebellion.
                               Example: The Pabna Rebellion (1873) in Bengal, where peasants organised legally and
                               physically to resist enhanced rents and physical oppression by corrupt zamindars.
 Religious and Social          British reforms and missionary activities were seen as threats to traditional religious and social
 Interference                  customs, causing unrest among various communities.
                               Example: The Wahabi Movement (1830-61) was a religiously motivated resistance.


3. Prominent Civil Uprisings

 Civil Uprising                                                    Description
Revolt by the       y In July 1794, the Raja of Vizianagaram in present-day Andhra Pradesh declared war on the British
Raja of               after they reduced his zamindari area and increased revenue demands.
Vizianagaram        y The War of Padmanabham broke out on July 10, 1794, where the Raja and 300 armed men were
                      killed by British forces.
                    y After this, the British adopted a conciliatory policy towards his son, Narayan Rao.
Dewan Velu          y Between 1808 and 1809, Dewan Velu Thambi led a revolt in Travancore (modern Kerala) against
Thambi Revolt         British interference and the debt caused by the subsidiary alliance.
                    y Despite support from the neighbouring state of Cochin, Velu Thambi was defeated.
                    y Velu Thambi committed suicide at Mannadi Temple in 1809 to avoid capture.
Kutch Rebellion     y From 1816 to 1832, the British treaty with Maharaja Bharamal II allowed interference in
                      Kutch’s internal affairs. Bharamal II’s attempt to expel the British in 1819 failed.
                    y The Regency Council’s administrative reforms and high land revenue assessments caused
                      resentment, leading to renewed rebellion in 1831.
                    y After prolonged military operations, the British adopted a more conciliatory approach.
Paika Rebellion     y In 1817, the Paikas, a peasant militia in Odisha, rebelled under the leadership of Bakshi
                      Jagabandhu after losing their estates due to British land revenue policies.
                    y The uprising spread rapidly, with attacks on government offices and British forces.
                    y The British eventually crushed the revolt; Baxi Jagabandhu was finally arrested in 1825 and died in
                      captivity in 1829.
Waghera Rising      y Between 1818 and 1820, the Waghera chiefs in Baroda, Gujarat, rebelled against the British-
                      supported Gaekwad ruler due to oppressive policies.
                    y They launched incursions into British territory until a peace treaty was signed in 1820, ending
                      the conflict.
Satara Revolt       y In 1840, after the British deposed Chhatrapati Pratapsingh and exiled him, the people of Satara,
                      Maharashtra, led by Dhar Rao Pawar and Narasingh Petkar, captured Badami Fort and raised the
                      Satara flag.
                    y The British quickly recaptured the fort, sentenced the leaders to life imprisonment, but Dhar Rao
                      continued resistance in the region until 1844.
Gadkari Revolt      y In 1844, the British takeover of Satara lands angered the Gadkaris, a Maratha militia in Kolhapur,
                      Maharashtra.
                    y They seized the Samangarh and Bhudargarh forts and set up a parallel government.
                    y The British army eventually suppressed the revolt.
Surat Salt          y In 1844, the British raised salt duties in Surat, Gujarat, provoking protests from the poorer classes.
Agitation           y They organised demonstrations and submitted petitions expressing their resentment.
                    y After negotiations, the British temporarily suspended the salt duty increase and abolished certain
                      town duties to appease the protesters.

4. Impacts of Civil Uprisings

   Aspect                                                          Description
Exposure of       The uprisings revealed deep dissatisfaction among various social groups such as rulers, zamindars, peasants,
Widespread        and sepoys against British economic exploitation, political domination, and cultural interference.
Discontent        Example: The Paika Rebellion (1817) exposed peasant and militia anger over land revenue policies in Odisha.
Challenge to      Although mostly localised and small-scale, these revolts challenged colonial control and exposed
British           vulnerabilities in British rule, forcing the British to respond with both repression and occasional concessions.
Authority         Example: Dewan Velu Thambi’s Revolt (1808-09) directly challenged colonial paramountcy
Sowing Seeds of   These movements united diverse sections of Indian society around a common cause, laying the
Nationalism       foundation for the larger Revolt of 1857 and the growth of Indian nationalist consciousness.
                  Example: The Satara Revolt (1840) united local leaders against British deposition of Chhatrapati
                  Pratapsingh.


 Highlighting         The uprisings underscored the failure of British land revenue and economic policies, which alienated
 Economic             traditional elites and peasants, contributing to increased resistance and unrest.
 Grievances           Example: The Surat Salt Agitation (1844) protested against increased salt duties affecting poorer classes.
 Prompting            In response, the British introduced administrative and military reforms such as reorganising the army
 British Reforms      and revising policies toward Indian rulers, though these measures often failed to address root causes of
                      discontent.
                      Example: : Following the Pabna Agrarian Revolt (1873–76), the government enacted the Bengal
                      Tenancy Act, 1885, which recognised occupancy rights of tenants, regulated rent enhancement, and
                      provided legal safeguards against arbitrary eviction by zamindars
 Awakening            The cumulative effect of these uprisings was a gradual rise in political awareness and resistance
 Political            against colonial rule, influencing subsequent freedom struggles in India.
 Consciousness        Example: The Sanyasi Rebellion (1763-1800) involved peasants and sanyasis resisting British economic
                      policies, inspiring future tribal and peasant movements.
The civil uprisings were localised yet significant expressions of resistance against British colonial policies that disrupted
traditional power structures and exploited economic resources. Though suppressed, these movements sowed the seeds of
Indian nationalism and collective dissent, ultimately paving the way for the larger and more organised Revolt of 1857,
marking the beginning of India’s struggle for independence.

8.2 Tribal Movements in India
Tribal revolts in India during British rule were distinct and persistent movements where indigenous communities fiercely resisted
colonial encroachment on their lands, forests, and traditional ways of life. These uprisings were marked by their militancy and
frequent occurrence, driven by economic exploitation, land alienation, cultural suppression, and the imposition of foreign laws,
and they played a crucial role in asserting tribal identity and autonomy against British colonialism.

                                                  UPSC Mains PYQs
      1.    How did colonial rule affect the tribals in India and what was the tribal response to the
            colonial oppression? (2023)

1. Causes for the Tribal Movements in India under British Rule

      Cause                                                           Description
 Land Alienation      Tribal communities lost their traditional lands and livelihoods due to dispossession by colonial
                      authorities and landlords. The British declared forests as state property, leading to the alienation of tribal
                      territories.
                      Example: The Santhal rebellion (1855-56) was triggered by land dispossession in Santhal Parganas.
 Exploitative         The British introduced new land revenue policies and imposed taxes on tribal products, which deprived
 Revenue              tribals of their customary land rights and caused economic hardship.
 System               Example: The Kol uprising (1831) was fuelled by oppressive revenue demands and loss of traditional rights.
 Forest Policies      The establishment of the Forest Department (1864) and enactment of Forest Acts (1865, 1878)
                      restricted tribal access to forests, adversely affecting their hunting, gathering, and shifting cultivation
                      practices.
                      Example: The Rampa rebellion (1922-24) was a response to forest restrictions in Andhra Pradesh.
 Introduction of      British policies brought traders, moneylenders, and oppressive officials into tribal areas, increasing
 Intermediaries       exploitation, extortion, and distress among tribal populations.
                      Example: The Munda uprising (1899-1900) saw tribals resisting moneylender exploitation in
                      Chotanagpur.
 Cultural             Activities of Christian missionaries were perceived as threats to tribal cultural identity and traditional
 Suppression          beliefs, causing social and cultural disruptions.
                      Example: Birsa Munda briefly converted to Christianity in his youth to gain education but later rejected
                      missionary influence, emphasising the revival of tribal identity and customs.


2. Tribal Response to Colonial Oppression

     Aspect                                                        Description
 Armed               Tribals launched armed uprisings to resist British policies that dispossessed them of land and resources,
 Resistance and      using traditional weapons and guerrilla tactics. These revolts were expressions of fierce opposition to
 Revolts             colonial rule despite often facing military suppression.
                     Example: The Santhal Rebellion, led by Sidhu and Kanhu Murmu, challenged land alienation and British
                     exploitation.
 Preservation of     Tribal movements resisted missionary efforts and colonial attempts to undermine their customs and
 Cultural            religious practices, aiming to protect their cultural heritage. This cultural resistance was vital for
 Identity            maintaining tribal autonomy.
                     Example: Birsa Munda’s movement revived tribal faith and traditions to counter Christian missionary
                     influence.
 Economic            Tribals opposed exploitative revenue systems, moneylender extortion, and forest laws that restricted
 Resistance          their traditional livelihoods. They sought to reclaim control over land and forest resources essential for
                     survival.
                     Example: The Kol uprising protested against land transfers to outsiders and oppressive taxation.
 Political           Many tribal revolts demanded political autonomy or independence from British control, resisting
 Assertion and       administrative encroachment. These movements asserted tribal self-rule and sovereignty over their
 Autonomy            territories.
                     Example: The Khasi Rebellion led by Tirot Sing, fought to maintain control over the Khasi Hills.
 Integration         Over time, tribal resistance aligned with the broader Indian freedom struggle, adopting Gandhian
 with National       non-violence and participating in national campaigns. This helped bring tribal issues into the
 Movement            mainstream independence movement.
                     Example: The Tana Bhagat Movement, led by Jatra Bhagat, joined the Non-Cooperation Movement
                     against British rule.

4. Tribal Revolts Before 1857
Tribal revolts before 1857 were primarily spontaneous and localised uprisings led by traditional tribal chiefs. These movements
mainly resisted British land alienation, forest policies, and economic exploitation, disrupting their traditional way of life.

5. Post 1857 Tribal Revolts
After 1857, tribal revolts became more organised and widespread, often influenced by broader socio-political changes and
nationalist ideas. They combined resistance against colonial economic policies with efforts to preserve tribal identity and sometimes
aligned with the Indian freedom struggle.

Tribal revolts during British rule were vital acts of resistance against exploitation, land alienation, and cultural erasure. As
Birsa Munda declared, “Abua raj ete jana, maharani raj tundu jana” (Let the kingdom of our own people come, let the rule
of the queen be ended), a call for self-rule that echoes in today’s tribal movements like the Niyamgiri protests, where
communities continue to defend their land and identity against corporate and state encroachment.

8.3 Peasant Movements
Peasants in colonial India suffered under British policies that commercialised agriculture, disrupted traditional agrarian relations,
and imposed exploitative land revenue systems. Initially localised and unorganised, peasant movements gradually merged with the
national freedom struggle, gaining wider political support.

Peasant revolts before 1857 were often dismissed by British sympathisers as mere law-and-order issues, while nationalist
narratives highlighted them as part of the anti-colonial struggle, sometimes overlooking other dimensions of the oppressed
peoples’ fight. Leadership typically emerged from within the peasants’ own cultural milieu, enabling authentic expression
of their grievances and resistance.

Peasant movements played a crucial role in awakening political consciousness among the rural masses and challenging entrenched
colonial and feudal exploitation. Their legacy continues to influence India’s socio-political landscape, highlighting the enduring
struggle for agrarian justice and rights.

8.4 Sepoy Mutinies
Long before the Great Revolt of 1857, the British East India Company faced a series of localised uprisings by its native Indian soldiers
(sepoys). Ranging from religious grievances to pay disputes, these scattered rebellions laid the groundwork for anti-colonial sentiment
and exposed fractures within the Company's military structure
