8.5 Revolt of 1857
The Revolt of 1857 was a significant uprising against British East India Company rule, marking a pivotal moment in India’s
struggle against colonial domination. As Veer Savarkar eloquently stated, “Today is the 10th of May! It was on this day that, in
the ever-memorable year of 1857, the first campaign of the War of Independence was opened by you, Oh Martyrs... ”.

                                                UPSC Mains PYQs
      1.    The Revolt of 1857 was the culmination of the recurrent big and small local rebellions
            that had occurred in the preceding hundred years of British rule. (2019)
      2.    Explain how the Uprising of 1857 constitutes an important watershed in the evolution
            of British policies towards colonial India. (2016)


1. Causes of the Revolt of 1857
Building on the immediate cause of the Revolt of 1857, which was the introduction of the Enfield rifle cartridges rumoured
to be greased with cow and pig fat and deeply offended Hindu and Muslim sepoys, several underlying political, economic,
administrative, and social causes also fuelled the uprising.


a. Politico-Administrative Causes of the Revolt of 1857
      Cause                                                            Description
Doctrine of Lapse       Dalhousie’s Doctrine of Lapse annexed states without natural heirs, rejecting adopted heirs (in states
and Annexation of       like Satara, Jhansi, Nagpur). Furthermore, the arbitrary annexation of Awadh (1856) for alleged
Awadh                   misgovernance deeply angered local nobility, soldiers, and peasants.
Disrespect to Indian    Indian sovereigns were humiliated and stripped of authority. For example, Bahadur Shah II was
Rulers                  reduced to a titular figure, and his successors were barred from residing in the Red Fort, alienating the
                        traditional elite.
Centralisation and      British centralisation severely weakened local chieftains, tribal leaders, and princes. The traditional
Loss of Local           aristocracy, landlords, and zamindars lost their estates and privileges, undermining indigenous
Authority               governance structures.
Exclusion from          Indians were systematically denied important civil and military posts. This strict policy of ‘racial
High Positions          exclusion’ frustrated educated Indians and the traditional ruling classes, rapidly diminishing their status.
Western Education       The forced imposition of Western education policies, alien legal frameworks, and unfamiliar
and Legal               administrative practices were viewed as a direct assault on Indian traditions, deepening the divide
Framework               between the rulers and the ruled.
Judicial Apathy         The introduction of an alien, expensive, and complex British judicial framework replaced
                        accessible indigenous justice mechanisms. It heavily favoured moneylenders and Europeans,
                        resulting in systemic denial of justice to common Indians.
Military Discontent     Sepoys faced discriminatory recruitment, poor treatment, and limited promotions compared to
                        Europeans. The introduction of Enfield rifle cartridges, rumoured to be greased with cow and pig
                        fat, acted as the immediate explosive trigger.

b. Economic Causes of the Revolt of 1857
     Cause                                                            Description
Drain of Wealth        The British extracted vast resources from India to finance their empire and military campaigns
                       abroad. Indian revenues were diverted to Britain, causing economic impoverishment and
                       resentment among all classes.
Land Revenue           Systems like Permanent Settlement, Mahalwari, and Ryotwari imposed heavy taxes on peasants and
Policies               landlords alike. This led to widespread land alienation, debt, and rural distress, fuelling anger
                       against colonial rule.
Destruction of         The influx of cheap, machine-made British goods devastated Indian handicrafts and textile industries.
Traditional            Artisans and weavers lost their livelihoods, increasing unemployment and economic hardship in
Industries             urban and rural areas.
Agricultural           Forced cultivation of cash crops such as indigo and opium reduced food production, causing famines
Exploitation           and food shortages. Indigo farmers faced severe oppression, adding to rural discontent.

Loss of                British reforms displaced many zamindars and traditional landlords, disrupting local power
Aristocracy            structures and creating instability. This alienated the aristocracy, who had previously supported or
                       tolerated British rule.
Widespread             Heavy taxation combined with cash crop cultivation led to food scarcity and recurrent famines. The
Poverty and            rural population suffered extreme poverty, which intensified the overall dissatisfaction that contributed
Famines                to the revolt.

c. Social Causes of the Revolt of 1857
      Cause                                                            Description
Racial                  The British maintained a sense of racial superiority, treating Indians, including elites, with
Discrimination          contempt and segregation. This racial arrogance created deep resentment among Indians who felt
                        humiliated and marginalised.
                        Example: Sepoys faced discrimination in pay and promotions compared to Europeans.


Religious              British reforms challenged traditional customs, such as the abolition of Sati and the legalisation of
Interference           widow remarriage, which alienated conservative Hindu sections. These changes were seen as
                       intrusive and disrespectful to Indian religious practices.
                       Example: Fear of forced Christianisation spread due to missionary activities.
Missionary             Aggressive efforts by Christian missionaries to convert Indians created widespread fear and
Activities             suspicion among Hindus and Muslims. This intensified communal anxieties and contributed to the
                       uprising.
                       Example: The Caste Disabilities Removal Act (Lex Loci Act, 1850) legally permitted Christian
                       converts to inherit ancestral property, cementing Indian fears of a state-sponsored conspiracy to
                       destroy indigenous faiths.
Alien Rule and         The British remained socially distant and aloof, refusing to integrate with Indian society, which
Social Aloofness       deepened feelings of alienation and mistrust. Indians perceived colonial rulers as outsiders who
                       disrespected their traditions.
                       Example: British officials’ refusal to adopt Indian customs or languages.
Loss of Religious      Traditional religious leaders like pandits and maulvis lost influence under British rule, weakening
and Social Prestige    established social hierarchies. This loss of status fuelled discontent among influential social groups.
                       Example: British legal and administrative reforms like Inam Commission of 1852 (confiscated
                       thousands of rent-free estates sustaining temples and mosques) sidelined religious authorities.
Disruption of Social   Policies like land revenue reforms and the Doctrine of Lapse disrupted traditional power
Order                  structures, destabilising local governance and social relations. This created uncertainty and unrest
                       among communities dependent on these structures.
                       Example: Annexation of princely states led to the loss of local authority.
Cultural               British attempts to impose Western education, legal systems, and administrative practices were
Insensitivity          seen as disrespectful to Indian traditions and customs. This alienated many Indians and deepened the
                       divide between rulers and the ruled.

2. Local Rebellions that led to the Revolt of 1857

    Rebellion                                            Core Features and Impact
Sanyasi Rebellion      Spanning Bengal and Bihar, this conflict arose from famine and extractive revenue systems that
(1763-1800)            threatened the survival of religious mendicants. It served as an early reaction to colonial economic
                       exploitation. Bankim Chandra’s Anandamath immortalised this struggle.
Vellore Mutiny         A violent uprising in Tamil Nadu triggered by coercive changes to religious dress and codes. This
(1806)                 was a critical precursor to the Great Revolt, highlighting deep-seated military grievances. The new
                       turban order by General Agnew acted as the immediate spark.
Paika Rebellion        Odisha’s traditional landed militia rebelled against predatory land tax policies and administrative
(1817)                 displacement. Led by Bakshi Jagabandhu, it represented a broad-based resistance to socio-
                       economic marginalisation.
Santhal Rebellion      A massive tribal surge in the Rajmahal hills against oppressive zamindars and Company officials.
(1855-56)              Triggered by land alienation and usury, it sought to re-establish tribal self-governance. Led by
                       Sidhu and Kanhu, it necessitated martial law.
Kol Rebellion          Indigenous tribes of Chotanagpur rose against the transfer of tribal lands to outsiders (Dikus). It
(1831-32)              was a fierce defence of customary rights against administrative encroachment. Buddhu Bhagat
                       provided notable leadership during this agrarian crisis.
Velu Thambi            The Dalawa of Travancore resisted British political meddling and crushing subsidies. His Kundara
Revolt (1808-09)       Proclamation called for a holy war to preserve regional sovereignty against the Subsidiary Alliance
                       system.
Satara Revolt          A reaction in Maharashtra to the deposition of Raja Pratap Singh and fiscal strain. It represented a
(1840)                 collective grief over lost independence coupled with agrarian misery under British residency.
Gadkari Revolt         Hereditary fort guards in Kolhapur (Maharashtra) protested the abolition of their status and rent-
(1844)                 free lands. This was a direct result of British-led administrative reorganisation which caused
                       widespread displacement of the old military orders.


Koya Revolts              Tribal groups in the Eastern Ghats resisted the usurpation of forest lands by landlords under the
(1832-42)                 Company's aegis. It was a violent struggle to protect tribal ecology and autonomy from revenue-
                          centric colonial policies.
Singpho Rebellion         In Upper Assam, tribal leaders fought against the annexation of their territories and commercial
(1839-42)                 monopolies. This was a localized but potent challenge to British frontiers and administrative control
                          in the Northeast.

3. Causes for the failure of the Revolt of 1857

      Aspect                                                           Description
Lack of Unified            The revolt lacked a clear and coherent political agenda beyond overthrowing British rule, resulting
Programme and              in disorganised efforts and no unified vision for post-rebellion governance.
Ideology                   Example: Leaders pursued localized goals without a common plan-Rani Lakshmibai sought to
                           restore her adopted son's succession rights, while Nana Saheb fought to reclaim his discontinued
                           Peshwa pension.
Lack of Unity              The revolt was geographically limited, combined with the active support of major native princes,
Among Indians              modern educated Indians, and certain regional groups to the British critically weakened collective
                           resistance.
                           Example: Punjab remained loyal to the British due to its hostility towards the Bengal Army and
                           British incentives such as land grants, assured military recruitment, and protection of local
                           chieftains' estates.
Absence of Support         The educated elite largely refrained from supporting the revolt, viewing it as backwards and
from Educated              believing British rule was essential for India’s modernisation and progress.
Indians                    Example: Many Bengali intellectuals and zamindars stayed neutral or sided with the British.
Military Superiority       British forces had better training, advanced weaponry, superior artillery, and the support of
of the British             loyal Indian rulers, which gave them a decisive advantage in suppressing the rebellion.
                           Example: The British had superior artillery and disciplined European regiments during the Siege of
                           Delhi.
Limited Support            Many princely states and local chiefs sided with the British, motivated by their own interests in
from Indian Princes        preserving power and privileges, thereby undermining the rebellion’s strength and reach.
and Chiefs                 Example: The Nizam of Hyderabad and the Maharaja of Patiala supported the British during the
                           revolt.
Use of Brutal British      The British responded with harsh reprisals, mass executions, and severe punishments, which
Tactics                    intimidated potential rebels and effectively crushed further resistance.
                           Example: The massacre at Kanpur and the execution of rebels in Delhi served as brutal warnings.

4. Significance and Consequences of the Revolt of 1857

    Aspect                                                           Description
Changes in              The British reorganised the army by increasing European troops and dividing Indian regiments along
Military                caste, community, and regional lines to prevent future mutinies.
Organisation            Example: The European-to-Indian ratio was fixed at 1:2 in Bengal and 1:3 in Madras/Bombay,
                        while recruitment shifted to ‘martial races’ (Sikhs, Gurkhas, Pathans), sidelining Awadhi and Bihari
                        sepoys.
Company Rule to         The governance of India shifted from the East India Company to direct British Crown rule through
Crown Rule              the Government of India Act, 1858. A Secretary of State for India, assisted by a council, was
                        appointed to oversee administration.
                        Example: Queen Victoria was proclaimed Empress of India in 1877, symbolising direct Crown control.
Divide and Rule         The colonial state actively weaponised religious identities to prevent future anti-British solidarity.
Policy                  Example: Initially holding Muslims primarily responsible for the mutiny, the British subjected them to
                        severe institutional discrimination post-1857, laying the foundation for modern communalism.
New Policy              The aggressive annexation policy was abandoned, allowing princely rulers to adopt heirs and maintain their
towards Princely        territories. This restored some autonomy to local rulers and secured their loyalty to the British Crown.
States                  Example: The Doctrine of Lapse was discontinued, and rulers like the Nizam of Hyderabad retained their
                        kingdoms.


 Catalyst for           Despite its military failure, the revolt laid the ideological foundation for the freedom struggle. It
 Nationalism and        showcased unprecedented Hindu-Muslim cooperation against a common colonial oppressor.
 Unity                  Example: Hindu and Muslim sepoys unanimously united to proclaim the Mughal Emperor, Bahadur
                        Shah II, as the symbolic leader.
 Exposure of            It brutally exposed the depths of Indian dissatisfaction against economic extortion and political
 British                domination, forcing a complete reassessment of colonial policies.
 Vulnerabilities        Example: The sheer scale of the uprising forced the British to pivot from aggressive expansionism to
                        defensive consolidation to secure their most profitable colony.
 Imposition of          The massive financial cost of suppressing the mutiny was entirely transferred to the Indian exchequer,
 Financial Burden       drastically inflating public debt and necessitating new extractive taxation.
                        James Wilson introduced India’s first Income Tax in 1860 to offset the massive colonial deficit incurred
                        during the suppression of the revolt.
 Legal and              The British completely overhauled the disjointed judicial architecture to tighten administrative control
 Judicial               and establish a uniform, state-centric criminal justice system to penalize dissent.
 Codification           Enactment of the Indian Penal Code (IPC, 1860) by Lord Thomas Babington Macaulay and the
                        Criminal Procedure Code (CrPC, 1861), alongside the establishment of unified High Courts in 1861.
 Strategic              The colonial state aggressively accelerated the construction of railways, telegraphs, and postal
 Infrastructure         networks, shifting the primary focus to rapid military mobilisation and intelligence transmission.
 Expansion              Railway lines were strategically redesigned to directly connect major inland military cantonments with
                        deep-water ports, ensuring swift deployment of European troops during future uprisings.

5. Revolt of 1857: Exclusively A Peasant Revolt or Not
     YES: Why the Revolt of 1857 can be                               NO: Why the Revolt of 1857 cannot be
       considered a Peasant Revolt                                    considered solely a Peasant Revolt
 Peasant-Centric Character: Marxist historians          Multi-Class Uprising: Modern historians view the revolt as a broad
 highlight the strong agrarian dimension of the         coalition of sepoys, peasants, zamindars, princes, artisans, and
 revolt, with peasants forming a major section of       dispossessed rulers rather than a purely peasant movement.
 the participants.
 'Peasant in Uniform': Most sepoys were                 Feudal Leadership: The revolt was led mainly by displaced rulers,
 recruited from peasant families (especially from       taluqdars, zamindars, and nobles such as Rani Lakshmibai and Nana
 Awadh and Bihar) and carried rural grievances          Saheb, not by peasants.
 into the army.
 Agrarian Exploitation: Oppressive land revenue         Limited Geographical Spread: Peasant participation was concentrated
 systems (Permanent Settlement, Ryotwari, etc.),        in regions like Awadh, Rohilkhand, Bundelkhand, and parts of Bihar,
 heavy taxation, indebtedness, and dispossession        while large agrarian regions such as Punjab, Bengal, the Bombay
 fuelled widespread rural discontent.                   Presidency, and most of South India remained largely unaffected.
 Mass Rural Participation: After the sepoy              Divergent Objectives: Peasants opposed excessive taxation and
 mutiny began, peasants joined in large numbers,        exploitation, whereas princes and landlords primarily sought restoration
 attacking moneylenders, indigo planters, revenue       of their political authority and privileges lost due to British policies such
 officials, and British administrative centres.         as the Doctrine of Lapse.
The 1857 Revolt endures as a powerful emblem of resistance against colonialism, often hailed as India’s First War of Independence,
inspiring subsequent generations in the fight for freedom. Despite failing to achieve its immediate goals, it united diverse sections
of Indian society and laid the foundation for Indian nationalism.
pyq: How did colonial rule affect the tribals in India and what was the tribal response to the colonial oppression? (2023)

chapters.text:
9               Socio-Religious Reform
                        Movements
Socio-religious movements in 19th-century India aimed to reform and modernize society by addressing social evils like sati, caste
discrimination, and gender inequality, while promoting education and rational religious thought. These movements, led by reformers
such as Raja Ram Mohan Roy and Ishwar Chandra Vidyasagar, sought to harmonise Indian traditions with modern values
and played a key role in awakening social consciousness during colonial rule.
                                                  UPSC Mains PYQs
      1.     Trace the rise and growth of socio-religious reform movements with special reference to
             Young Bengal and Brahmo Samaj. (2021)
      2.     Examine the linkages between the nineteenth century's "Indian Renaissance" and the
             emergence of national identity. (2019)
      3.     The women's questions arose in modern India as a part of the 19th century social
             reform movement. What are the major issues and debates concerning women in that
             period? (2017)

1. Nature of Socio-Religious Reform Movements
           Aspect                                                        Description
 Socio-religious             Movements combined social reform (abolishing social evils) with religious revival (purifying
 Character                   faith and practices), balancing modernity & tradition and social change & religious faith.
 Urban Middle-Class          Primarily led by educated urban elites aiming to modernise society and religion.
 Leadership
 Rational and                Emphasised reason, education, and humanism against superstition and priestly dominance.
 Humanistic
 Social Reform Focus         Targeted issues like caste discrimination, sati, child marriage, untouchability, and women's rights.
 Religious Purification      Many sought to return to original religious texts and practices, rejecting rituals and idol worship.
 Limited Mass Appeal         Mostly influenced urban areas; rural masses were less impacted directly.
 Role in Nationalism         Helped shape Indian identity and contributed indirectly to the freedom struggle.

2. Causes of Socio-Religious Reform Movements in India
       Factors                                                         Description
 Impact of British        y British colonial rule exposed the weaknesses and decay in Indian social institutions (identified
 Rule                       by reformers themselves) and created conditions favourable for intellectual growth and
                            opposition to exploitative social practices. Freedom of the press helped spread awareness about
                            social evils like sati and child marriage.
                          y Example: Raja Ram Mohan Roy’s campaign against sati.
 Influence of             y Western education introduced ideas of rationalism, humanism, liberty, equality, and scientific
 Modern Western             thinking, inspiring Indians to question orthodox practices and superstitions.
 Education                y Example: Sir Syed Ahmed Khan’s Aligarh Movement promoting modern education among Muslims.
 Opposition to            y Missionary efforts to convert lower-caste groups and marginalised communities sparked a defensive
 Missionary                 reaction.
 Activities               y Example: Movements like the Arya Samaj and the Singh Sabha Movement arose explicitly to
                            counter these influences, revitalize indigenous religions, and stop conversions
 Social Conditions        y Indian society was rigid, caste-ridden, and marked by social evils such as untouchability, child
 and Decay                  marriage, and gender discrimination, creating a strong desire for reform.
                          y Example: Jyotirao Phule’s work against caste discrimination and for women’s education.
 Growth of                y The rise of Indian nationalism emphasised social reform as essential for national unity and progress.
 Nationalist              y Example: Arya Samaj’s role in promoting self-respect and social equality linked to nationalist
 Sentiments                 ideas.


Emergence of New       y The rise of the urban middle class, capitalist class, and modern intelligentsia demanded social
Social and Economic      modernisation aligned with their interests .
Groups                 y Example: The new capitalist and middle classes found traditional religious restrictions such as
                         bans on sea travel or rigid caste rules to be obstacles to modern trade, business expansion, and
                         professional mobility
Spread of              y Expansion of vernacular press and educational institutions increased awareness and debate
Vernacular               on social and religious issues.
Education and          y Example: Reformist journals and pamphlets such as Shatapatre (The Hundred Letters) by
Print Culture            leaders like Gopal Hari Deshmukh attacked caste hierarchy, religious superstitions, and the
                         subjugation of women.
Exposure to            y Increased interaction with Western liberal, democratic, and humanitarian ideas encouraged
Western Ideas and        Indians to seek social and religious reforms.
Culture                y Example: Young Bengal movement inspired by French Revolution ideals.
Opposition to          y Reformers sought to eliminate superstitions, idol worship, and priestly dominance to rationalise
Blind Faith and          religion.
Superstition           y Example: Arya Samaj’s emphasis on Vedic purity and rejection of idol worship.

3. Major socio-religious Reformers and Movements in India during British rule
  Reformer/                                                       Description
  Movement
Raja Rammohan         y Rammohan Roy, often called the first great leader of modern India, was deeply troubled by the
Roy and Brahmo          stagnation and corruption in caste-bound society.
Samaj                 y He blended Eastern and Western thought, founded the Atmiya Sabha (1814) to discuss
                        monotheism, and led a campaign against social evils like sati, idolatry, and caste rigidity.
                      y He emphasized reason, monotheism based on Vedas and Upanishads, and promoted modern
                        education (helped found Hindu College, English School, Vedanta College).
                      y He pioneered Indian journalism (Sambad Kaumudi, Mirat-ul-Akhbar) and advocated for human
                        dignity and internationalism.
                      y Roy founded the Brahmo Samaj (1828) aimed to purify Hinduism, reject priestly dominance, and
                        include positive aspects of other faiths.
                      y Later leaders like Debendranath Tagore and Keshab Chandra Sen expanded its reach and
                        radicalised its agenda (women’s rights, caste reform).
Young Bengal          y Led by Henry Vivian Derozio, this radical trend emerged in Bengal in the late 1820s and 1830s.
Movement              y Derozio inspired students at Hindu College to think freely, challenge authority, and value liberty
                        and equality.
                      y The group attacked outdated customs and advocated for women’s rights and education.
                      y Though their ideas were ahead of their time and failed to reach the masses, they promoted public
                        debate on issues like press freedom, labour rights, and judicial reforms.
Debendranath          y Debendranath Tagore blended traditional Indian learning with modern Western ideas.
Tagore and            y He founded the Tattvabodhini Sabha (1839) to spread Rammohun Roy’s ideas, counter
Tattvabodhini           Christianity, and promote Vedantism.
Sabha                 y The Sabha published Bengali texts, encouraged rationalism, and promoted the study of India’s
                        past.
                      y Debendranath reorganised the Brahmo Samaj in 1843, supporting widow remarriage, women’s
                        education, and social reforms, while questioning the infallibility of Vedic scriptures.
Ishwar Chandra        y Vidyasagar, principal of Sanskrit College (1851), introduced Western thought and opened the
Vidyasagar              college to non-Brahmins.
                      y He wrote a Bengali primer, helped develop modern Bengali prose, and was a champion of women’s
                        rights. He wrote ‘Bidhobabivah’ on widow’s right to remarry (1855), ‘Bahubivah’ on banning of
                        polygamy (1871) and Balyabivah on the flaws of child marriage.
                      y His efforts led to the Hindu Widow Remarriage Act (1856) and the first legal widow remarriage
                        among upper castes.
                      y He also promoted girls’ education (helped found Bethune School) and campaigned against child
                        marriage and polygamy.


 Jyotiba Phule and    y Phule dedicated his life advocating for the rights of women and lower castes; rationalism, equality,
 Satyashodhak           social ethics.
 Samaj                y He fought caste oppression and rejected ritualism, idol worship, and priestly dominance.
                      y He established schools for girls and lower castes, set up ashrams and orphanages for widows and
                        abandoned women, and promoted gender equality.
                      y In 1873, he founded the Satyashodhak Samaj to educate society about caste prejudice and free the
                        oppressed.
                      y He coined the term ‘Dalit’ and authored works like Gulamgiri and Sarvajanik Satyadharma Pustak,
                        emphasising equality and justice; Shetkaryacha Asud (1881): linking caste oppression with
                        economic exploitation of peasants.
 Savitribai           y A pioneering social reformer and poet, Savitribai was instrumental in advancing women’s
 Phule                  education and rights.
                      y She started the first girls’ school (1848, Pune) with Jyotirao Phule; started Mahila Seva Mandal
                        (1852); organised barbers’ strikes against widow head-shaving; ran night schools for labourers;
                        and led the women’s section of Satyashodhak Samaj.
                      y She also worked on famine relief and wrote influential literary works.
 Bal Shastri          y One of the earliest reformers in Bombay, he attacked Brahmanical orthodoxy and sought to reform
 Jambhekar              popular Hinduism.
                      y He started the weekly Darpan (1832) to dispel ignorance and promote knowledge.
 Paramahansa          y Founded in Maharashtra (1849) by Durgaram Mehtaji and Dadoba Pandurang, this group
 Mandali                worked secretly to promote monotheism, break caste rules, and fight idolatry.
                      y Members shared food cooked by lower castes and advocated women’s education and widow
                        remarriage.
 Gopal Hari           y A leading social reformer in Maharashtra, Deshmukh broadened the scope of reform through his
 Deshmukh               writings in the Marathi weekly Prabhakar.
 (Lokahitawadi)       y His ‘Shatpatre’ letters addressed a wide range of social issues, and he argued that if religion
                        opposed social reform, religion itself should be changed.
 Kandukuri            y A pioneering social reformer in Andhra Pradesh, Veeresalingam championed widow remarriage,
 Veeresalingam          women’s education, and the eradication of social vices.
                      y He established schools, authored influential works in Telugu, and built the Brahmo Mandir in
                        Rajahmundry, earning him the title “father of modern Telugu prose” and a reputation as the
                        Rammohun Roy of Andhra.
4. Socio-Religious Reform Movements after 1858
Many Indians recognized that social and religious reform was crucial for the country's comprehensive modernization and the
growth of national unity, and after 1858, these reform movements gained momentum due to rising nationalist sentiments, new
economic forces, the spread of education, Western ideas and culture, and increased global awareness.
  Movement/Leader                                                    Description
 Ramakrishna                y Ramakrishna Paramhansa emphasized spiritual realization through devotion, meditation,
 Mission and Swami            and tolerance for all faiths, teaching that “service to humanity is service to God.”
 Vivekananda                y Swami Vivekananda, his disciple, spread these ideals globally and founded the Ramakrishna
                              Mission in 1897 to combine spiritual upliftment with social service.
                            y He opposed superstitions, caste rigidity, and untouchability, advocating for rational
                              Vedanta, liberty of thought, and the need to learn organisational skills from the West, teaching
                              that “service to humanity is service to God".
 Veda Samaj                 y Established in Madras by Sridharalu Naidu and Keshab Chandra Sen, inspired by Brahmo
 (1864)                       Samaj.
                            y The movement promoted monotheism, social equality, widow remarriage, and opposed
                              polygamy and child marriage.
                            y It sought to eliminate caste distinctions and sectarian beliefs, and to reform marriage and
                              funeral rituals.
 Arya Samaj                 y Founded in Bombay by Swami Dayanand Saraswati, Arya Samaj sought to reform Hinduism
 (1875)                       by returning to the authority of the Vedas, rejecting idolatry, caste discrimination, and
                              superstitions.
                            y It promoted women’s education, social equality, and the Shuddhi movement for reconverting
                              Hindus.
                            y The organization played a key role in spreading education (DAV schools, Gurukul system)
                              and fostering self-respect and national pride. Internal divisions led to the formation of
                              Dayanand Anglo-Vedic (DAV) and Gurukul groups.


Prarthana Samaj      y Founded by Atmaram Pandurang, M.G. Ranade became one of its most influential leaders later
(1867)                 in Maharashtra.
                     y This movement aimed to modernise Hindu religious practices, promote monotheism, and remove
                       priestly dominance and caste orthodoxy.
                     y It drew inspiration from Bhakti saints and Christianity, advocated for social reforms like widow
                       remarriage and women’s education, and spread to South India through leaders like Viresalingam.
Theosophical         y Founded by Madame Blavatsky and Colonel Olcott in the USA, the society promoted the
Society                revival of ancient religions and philosophies, universal brotherhood, and national pride.
                     y Annie Besant led its Indian chapter, contributing to women’s advancement, education (Central
                       Hindu School), and the fight against untouchability.
                     y The society’s work instilled self-confidence and cultural pride among educated Indians.
Deccan Education     y Established in Pune by G.G. Agarkar, B.G. Tilak and Vishnushastri Chiplunkar, the society
Society (1884)         aimed to promote mass education independent of government or missionary control.
                     y It founded Fergusson College and became a centre for both social reform and political
                       awakening in Maharashtra, with leaders like Ranade and Chiplunkar contributing to its
                       activities.
Dev Samaj            y Founded by Pandit Shiv Narayan Agnihotri in Lahore, Dev Samaj rejected orthodox
(1887)                 Hinduism, caste restrictions, and rituals, focusing on morality, co-education, and inter-caste
                       marriage.
                     y It emphasised the worship of a living guru and advocated for women’s education and ethical
                       living.
                     y Agnihotri’s leadership marked a shift from Brahmo Samaj ideals to a guru-centric
                       approach.

5. Muslim Reform Movements
    Movement                                                 Description
Wahabi Movement    y Led by Syed Ahmed Barelvi; inspired by Muhammad ibn Abdul Wahhab and Shah Waliullah.
(1830-1860)        y Primarily a religious revivalist movement with its hub in Patna, missions in major regions like
                     Bombay and Bengal.
                   y It became a peasant-led struggle against landlords in Bengal.
Faraizi Movement   y Founded by Haji Shariatullah and Dudu (Dadu) Mian to purify Islamic practices and emphasise
(1838-1848)          obligatory duties (faraiz).
                   y Also led an anti-tax, anti-zamindar/indigo-planter campaign in Bengal.
                   y United farmers against landlord oppression in Dhaka, Faridpur, Barisal, Mymensingh, and Comilla.
Mohammedan         y Founded by Abdul Latif in Calcutta.
Literary Society   y Promoted modern education and discouraged polygamy among middle-class Muslims.
(1863)             y Supported Hindu‑Muslim unity.
Deoband            y Established Dar‑ul‑Uloom Deoband by Nanautavi and Gangohi.
Movement           y Rejected Sufi innovations.
(1866)             y Focused on Quran/Hadith-based moral revival.
                   y Opposed Aligarh modernist thought but welcomed INC formation.
Aligarh Movement   y Spearheaded by Sayyid Ahmed Khan; founded the Muhammadan Anglo-Oriental (MAO)
(1875 onward)        College (later, Aligarh Muslim University/AMU).
                   y Advocated Western science, women's education and freedom of thought.
                   y Sir Syed Ahmad Khan heavily critiqued the traditions of purdah and polygamy.
                   y Developed Urdu press and Muslim middle-class unity.
Ahmadiyya          y Led by Mirza Ghulam Ahmad in Punjab.
Movement           y Established schools/colleges; emphasized Islam’s humanitarian values.
(1889)             y Promoted Hindu‑Muslim unity and peaceful global outreach.

6. Sikh Reform Movements
    Movement                                                  Description
Kuka (Namdhari)    y Started by Baba Balak Singh and Bhagat Jawaharmal and later led by Baba Ram Singh.
Movement (1872)    y Emphasised one‑God worship, prayer, and moral discipline.
                   y Opposed caste, infanticide, and child marriage; adopted simple “Anand” weddings.


Singh Sabha               y Founded in Amritsar and Lahore by Thakur Singh Sandhawalia and others.
Movement (1873)           y Focused on Sikh education, Gurmukhi, literature; set up Khalsa College (1892).
                          y Countered syncretism, worked within British‑acceptable limits.
Akali Movement            y Emerged from the Singh Sabha; led by Akalis like Kartar Singh.
(1920-1925)               y Fought for gurudwara reform via nonviolent satyagraha.
                          y Enacted Sikh Gurdwaras Act (1922, revised 1925); placed shrines under Shiromani Gurdwara
                            Parbandhak Committee (SGPC) control.
Nirankari Movement        y Founded by Baba Dayal Singh in 1851.
(mid‑19th C.)             y In the 1840s, he advocated for the restoration of Sikhism to its roots, emphasising the worship of
                            one God and nirankar (formless).
                          y Rejected priestly caste privileges and idol‑based worship; emphasised Naam Simran.
                          y Strengthened distinct Sikh identity.

7. Parsi Reform Movements
     Movement                                                        Description
Rahnumai                   y Founded in Bombay by Dadabhai Naoroji, Nauroji Furdonji, S. S. Bengalee and R. Cama.
Mazdayasnan Sabha          y Published Rast Goftar; campaigned for girls’ education, marriage reform, and raising
(1851)                       women’s status.
                           y Helped modernise Parsi social customs, making them the most westernised group.

8. Factors that Helped to Mitigate Caste-Based Discrimination
      Factor                                                        Description
Unwitting Role of       y The British colonial administration codified and rigidified caste identities through censuses and
British Rule              laws, but also introduced legal reforms and affirmative action that inadvertently helped
                          marginalised castes.
                        y Example: Free sale of land upset caste equations by enabling lower castes to acquire property and
                          challenge traditional dominance.
Role of Social          y Reformers challenged caste discrimination by promoting education, social equality, and activism
Reform Movements          against untouchability and caste oppression.
                        y Example: Jyotirao Phule’s efforts for lower-caste education.
Principles              y The Indian National Congress and other nationalist groups promoted ideals of equality, social
Governing the             justice, and unity beyond caste divisions as part of the freedom struggle.
National Movement       y Example: Gandhi’s Harijan movement aimed at eradicating untouchability and integrating Dalits
                          into society.
Spread of               y Expansion of modern education and vernacular press increased awareness about caste injustices
Education and             and empowered lower caste groups to demand rights and equality.
Awareness Among         y Example: Establishment of schools for Dalits by reformers and increased literacy leading to
Caste Groups              political mobilization.

9. Steps Taken to Ameliorate Women’s Position
       Aspect                                                         Description
Social and Religious       y Reformers challenged social evils like sati, child marriage, and purdah; promoted widow
Reform Movements             remarriage and women’s education.
                           y Example: Raja Ram Mohan Roy’s campaign against sati; Ishwar Chandra Vidyasagar’s efforts
                             for widow remarriage; Savitribai Phule’s pioneering work in women’s education.
Legislative and Legal      y Laws were enacted to protect and empower women, abolishing discriminatory practices and
Measures                     promoting rights.
                           y Example: Sati Abolition Act (1829), Widow Remarriage Act (1856), Child Marriage Restraint
                             Act (1929), etc.
Expansion of Women’s       y Establishment of schools and colleges for girls increased literacy and social awareness.
Education                  y Example: Bethune School (Calcutta) was founded in 1849 by John Elliot Drinkwater Bethune
Formation of Women’s       y Women-led organisations mobilised for rights, education, and political participation.
Organizations              y Example: The All India Women’s Conference (AIWC) was founded in 1927 by Margaret cousins.


 Political Empowerment        y Inclusion of women in the freedom struggle and legislative bodies enhanced their public role.
 and Representation           y Example: Participation of women like Sarojini Naidu, Kamala Nehru in nationalist movements.
 Awareness and Cultural       y Spread of print media, reformist literature, and campaigns raised awareness about gender
 Change                         equality and women’s rights.
                              y Example: Publications by reformers like Pandita Ramabai; campaigns against child marriage
                                and purdah.

10. Contributions of Women Reformers
The 19th and 20th-century socio-religious reform movements witnessed a crucial paradigm shift: from "reform for women" (initiated
by male reformers like Raja Rammohan Roy and Ishwar Chandra Vidyasagar) to "reform by women" where women asserted their
own agency, challenged orthodoxies, and built institutions.

     Reformer           Key Organizations                                             Details
                           / Literature
 Savitribai Phule      Bhide Wada School            Opened the first school for girls in India (1848), focusing on the
 and Fatima            (1848), Mahila Seva          intersection of caste and gender, actively educating untouchable
 Sheikh                Mandal                       communities and campaigning against female infanticide.
 Pandita               Arya Mahila Samaj            Institutional support for widows: A Sanskrit scholar who challenged
 Ramabai               (1882), Sharada Sadan        Hindu orthodoxy. Provided safe havens and vocational training for destitute
                       (1889), Mukti Mission        widows and child brides, fostering economic independence.
 Tarabai               Stri Purush Tulana           Authored what is considered India's first modern feminist text ‘Stri Purush
 Shinde                (1882)                       Tulana (1882)’ → fearlessly critiqued double standards in religious
                                                    scriptures and the societal treatment of women and patriarchy.
 Swarnakumari          Sakhi Samiti                 Economic Self-Reliance: Promoted indigenous handicrafts and aimed to
 Devi                  (1886)                       create a sense of unity and self-reliance among Indian women, aiding
                                                    widows and unmarried girls.
 Begum Rokeya          Anjuman-e-Khawatin-e-        Islamic Social Reform: Championed modern education for Muslim girls
 Sakhawat              Islam, Sultana's Dream       and heavily critiqued the extreme purdah system. Her literary work
 Hossain               (1905)                       envisioned a utopian, science-driven matriarchy.
 Ramabai               Poona Seva Sadan (1909)      Vocational Empowerment: Expanded the scope of women's work beyond
 Ranade                                             the domestic sphere by providing institutionalized training for women to
                                                    become nurses, teachers, and midwives.
 Dr.                   Women's Indian               Legislative Advocacy: The first female legislator in India. Successfully
 Muthulakshmi          Association (1917)           campaigned for the abolition of the Devadasi system and was instrumental
 Reddy                                              in pushing for the Sarda Act (raising the marriage age). An influential
                                                    editor of the WIA's journal, Stri-Dharma.
11. Linkages between Indian Renaissance and National Identity
    Aspect                                                          Description
 Cultural           y Revival and reinterpretation of India’s ancient cultural and spiritual heritage fostered pride and
 Nationalism          unity across diverse communities.
                    y Example: Swami Vivekananda’s emphasis on India’s spiritual greatness inspired national pride.
 Social Reform      y Reform movements aimed at abolishing social evils like sati, child marriage, and caste discrimination,
 and Unity            promoting social equality and cohesion.
                    y Example: Ishwar Chandra Vidyasagar’s efforts for widow remarriage and female education.
 Political          y Exposure to Western political ideas and critiques of colonial rule led to political consciousness and
 Awakening            demand for self-rule.
                    y Example: Formation of the Indian National Congress in 1885 and leaders like Dadabhai Naoroji
                      advocating for Indian rights by exposing Britain's economic exploitation.
 Role of            The spread of modern education and vernacular/English press helped disseminate nationalist ideas and
 Education          mobilise public opinion.
 and Press          Example: Newspapers like "The Hindu" and Brahmo Samaj publications such as Brahmo Dharma.
 Religious          y Reinterpretation of religious philosophies emphasised universal values and tolerance, reducing
 Reform and           sectarian divides.
 Harmony            y Example: Swami Vivekananda’s promotion of Vedantic unity and interfaith dialogue.


12. Impacts of Socio-Religious Movements
     Aspect                                                         Description
Abolition of Evil    y The movements played a crucial role in abolishing social evils such as sati, child marriage, purdah, and
Practices              dowry, thereby improving the status and rights of women and challenging oppressive customs deeply
                       rooted in society.
                     y Example: Raja Ram Mohan Roy’s campaign led to the abolition of sati through the Sati Regulation Act,
                       1829.
Women                y By advocating for women’s education, widow remarriage, and legal rights, these movements laid the
Empowerment            foundation for gender equality and greater participation of women in social and political spheres.
                     y Example: Ishwar Chandra Vidyasagar championed widow remarriage, resulting in the Widow
                       Remarriage Act, 1856.
Promotion of         y Reformers established schools and colleges promoting modern, scientific education accessible to
Education              all sections, including women and marginalised communities, which catalysed social mobility and
                       intellectual awakening.
                     y Example: Raja Rammohan Roy helped watchmaker and philanthropist David Hare in founding
                       institutions like the Hindu College in Calcutta, promoting modern education.
Challenge to Caste   y The movements actively opposed caste discrimination and untouchability, promoting social equality
System                 and dignity for lower castes, thus paving the way for a more egalitarian society.
                     y Example: Jyotirao Phule and Savitribai Phule worked extensively for the education and upliftment of
                       lower castes and women in Maharashtra.
Religious Reform     y They sought to reform religious practices by rejecting idol worship, superstitions, and ritualism,
and Rationalism        promoting monotheism, rational thought, and religious tolerance to build a more inclusive spiritual
                       outlook.
                     y Example: Swami Dayananda Saraswati’s Arya Samaj rejected idol worship and emphasized Vedic
                       monotheism.
Rise of National     y By fostering pride in Indian culture and spirituality while challenging colonial stereotypes, these
Consciousness          movements inspired patriotism and unity that contributed significantly to the freedom struggle.
                     y Example: The teachings of Swami Vivekananda inspired nationalist pride and unity among Indians.
Social Pluralism     y Emphasising interfaith harmony and universal values, the movements encouraged religious tolerance
and Harmony            and social cohesion among diverse communities, helping to reduce communal tensions.
                     y Example: The Brahmo Samaj promoted universal brotherhood and rejected caste and religious
                       exclusivism.

13. Limitations of Socio-Religious Movements
         Aspect                                                        Description
Limited Mass Support        y Most reform movements remained confined to educated urban elites and upper castes, with limited
                              reach among rural masses and lower castes. Deep-rooted social customs and traditional beliefs in
                              villages restricted the spread of reform ideas, limiting their broader social impact.
                            y Example: Brahmo Samaj and Arya Samaj were largely urban and upper-caste-based, while
                              practices such as child marriage and caste discrimination continued to persist widely in rural India.
Promotion of Revivalist     y By emphasising ancient scriptures and cultural pride, some reformers unintentionally
Thinking                      encouraged mysticism and hindered the full adoption of scientific rationalism. It shifted the
                              focus on religious purification, overlooking other cultural aspects like art, literature, and
                              science.
                            y Example: Back to the Vedas call and Shuddhi movement of Arya Samaj encouraged
                              revivalist tendencies.
Reinforcement of            y At times, these movements strengthened communal and caste divisions, contributing to
Communal and Caste            social fragmentation and the emergence of modern communalism.
Identities                  y Example: The Arya Samaj’s focus on Hindu revivalism sometimes alienated Muslim
                              communities, contributing to communal tensions.
Resistance from             y Conservative sections of society strongly opposed reforms such as abolition of sati and
Traditionalists               child marriage, slowing down social progress.
                            y Example: The abolition of sati faced stiff resistance from orthodox Hindu communities and
                              movements like Bharat Dharma Mahamandala (1887).


 Internal Divisions         y Disagreements and splits within movements weakened their unity and overall effectiveness.
 Among Reformers            y Example: The Brahmo Samaj split into the Adi Brahmo Samaj and the Sadharan Brahmo
                              Samaj due to ideological differences.
 Dependence on Colonial     y Many legal reforms often required British colonial backing, raising questions about the
 Support                      movements’ autonomy and genuine grassroots support.
                            y Example: The Widow Remarriage Act, 1856, was passed under British administration,
                              reflecting colonial influence on reform legislation.
 Neglect of Economic        y The movements largely ignored economic inequalities and poverty, which limited their
 Issues                       appeal and impact among the economically disadvantaged sections of society.
                            y Example: Socio-religious reformers rarely addressed agrarian distress or poverty, which
                              fuelled peasant uprisings instead.
The socio-religious reform movements of the 19th century played a transformative role in eradicating oppressive
customs, promoting social equality, and fostering a sense of national consciousness that laid the foundation for modern
India. As Sir Syed Ahmad Khan aptly said, “India is a beautiful bride and Hindus and Muslims are her two eyes,”
emphasising that the nation’s strength and beauty depend on the harmony and unity of its diverse communities, a
message that remains crucial in contemporary India.

Swarnakumari Sakhi Samiti Economic Self-Reliance: Promoted indigenous handicrafts and aimed to Devi (1886) create a sense of unity and self-reliance among Indian women, aiding widows and unmarried girls. chapters.t: Formation and Evolution of Indian National Congress (INC)
chapters.text:
10                  Formation and Evolution of
                         Indian National Congress (INC)
The Indian National Congress (INC) was founded in December 1885 in Bombay by 72 delegates, with A. O. Hume played a key
role in uniting regional leaders and W.C. Bonnerjee served as its first president. Its formation as a national political forum marked
a significant step in India’s freedom movement, though it also sparked debates like the Safety-valve Theory.

1. Reasons behind the formation of the Indian National Congress (INC)
        Reason                                                          Description
 Growth of Political       y Western education, English language, and liberal ideas fostered an educated middle class with a
 Consciousness               common political outlook.
                           y Example: The INC’s first session in December 1885 at Bombay brought together 72
                           y delegates from across India, representing diverse regions.
 Precursor Political       y Regional political bodies developed organisational experience and constitutional methods of agitation.
 Associations              y Example: British Indian Association (1851), Poona Sarvajanik Sabha (1870), Indian Association (1876).
 Need for an All-India     y Fragmented regional organisations required a common national body to coordinate demands.
 Platform
 Economic                  y Drain of wealth, deindustrialisation, high land revenue, and famines generated economic
 Grievances                  nationalism.
                           y Example: Dadabhai Naoroji's Drain Theory.
 Demand for                y Indian leaders sought greater representation in legislative councils and a structured
 constitutional            y platform to engage in civic and political dialogue with the British government.
 reforms
 Rise of Press             y Newspapers spread nationalist ideas and created an informed political public.
                           y Example: The Hindu, Amrita Bazar Patrika, Kesari.
 Safety Valve Theory       y It is being said that A. O. Hume facilitated the INC’s formation, partly as a “safety valve” to
 (A.O. Hume)                 channel rising Indian discontent into peaceful and constitutional means.

2. Objectives of the INC
      Aspect                                                          Description
 Nation Building       y Fostered a collective Indian identity, regardless of their cultural, linguistic, and religious differences
 and Unity               for a unified movement.
                       y Example: Congress united Indians of diverse backgrounds during the freedom struggle.
 Formulation of        y Formulating and presenting popular demands to the British government was indeed a primary
 Popular                 objective of the Indian National Congress (INC) upon its formation in 1885.
 Demands               y Example: Expansion of legislative councils and greater participation of Indians in governance;
                         protection of civil liberties, including the freedom of speech and expression; and the reduction of
                         military expenditure, fairer taxation, and the protection of Indian industries.
 Promoting             y Conducted annual sessions in different cities to ensure regional participation and strengthen pan-
 National Unity          Indian solidarity.
 Across Regions        y Example: Annual INC sessions were held in cities like Bombay (1885), Calcutta (1886), and
                         Lucknow (1916), ensuring representation from all regions.
 Creating a            y Provided a common platform for Indians to express grievances and coordinate collective action
 Political Forum         against colonial rule.
 for United Action     y Example: INC enabled mass movements such as the Non-Cooperation (1920), Civil Disobedience
                         (1930), and Quit India (1942) movements.
 Raising Political     y Educated Indians about their rights and political issues through speeches, petitions, and public
 Awareness               meetings.
                       y Example: Congress mobilized public opinion during the Salt March (1930) and through widespread
                         campaigns and petitions.


 Developing              y Nurtured representative leaders capable of guiding the nationalist movement and negotiating with
 All-India                 the British.
 Leadership              y Example: Leaders like Gandhi, Nehru, Patel, and Azad emerged from Congress ranks, shaping the
                           freedom struggle and post-independence India.
 Securing Political      y Sought reforms such as fair taxation and better representation, laying the groundwork for greater
 Reforms                   self-governance.
                         y Example: Congress’s efforts contributed to reforms like the Indian Councils Act of 1892 and 1909
                           (Morley-Minto), which increased Indian representation.

3. Role and Impact of the INC in the Indian National Movement
    Aspect                                                           Description
 Mass                 y INC mobilised masses from all regions and communities, making the freedom struggle a truly national
 Mobilization           movement.
                      y Example: During the Non-Cooperation Movement, millions from urban and rural India participated in
                        protests, boycotts, and demonstrations.
 Leadership           y The Congress provided visionary leaders who shaped the direction and ideology of the independence
                        movement.
                      y Example: Gandhi’s leadership during the Civil Disobedience Movement inspired mass participation and
                        non-violent resistance.
 Major                y INC organized and led major nationwide campaigns against British rule.
 Movements            y Example: The Quit India Movement in 1942 saw widespread protests, strikes, and arrests across the
                        country.
 Non-Violent          y The Congress popularized non-violent methods as the principal strategy against colonial oppression.
 Resistance           y Example: The Salt Satyagraha (Dandi March) demonstrated peaceful defiance of unjust British laws.
 Inclusivity          y INC represented diverse ideologies and communities, making the movement broad-based and
                        inclusive.
                      y Example: Both moderates like Gokhale and extremists like Tilak, as well as leaders from various
                        backgrounds, worked together within the Congress.
 Strategic            y The Congress adapted its strategies to changing circumstances, shifting from petitions to mass
 Adaptation             agitation when needed.
                      y Example: After the partition of Bengal in 1905, Congress moved from moderate constitutional methods
                        to the Swadeshi and Boycott Movements.
 International        y INC brought the Indian cause to the global stage, garnering international support and sympathy.
 Attention            y Example: Congress leaders attended the Round Table Conferences in London to voice Indian aspirations
                        for self-rule.
 Social               y The Congress addressed key social issues alongside political demands, promoting social justice and
 Reform                 equality.
                      y Example: Congress campaigned against untouchability and for women’s rights, integrating social reform
                        with the cause of the freedom struggle.
 Symbol of            y INC became the embodiment of the independence movement, inspiring unity and patriotism.
 Freedom              y Example: The adoption of the tricolour flag and the slogan “Swaraj is my birthright” by Tilak
                        galvanised the masses.
 Democratic           y The Congress laid the foundation for democracy and civil liberties in independent India.
 Foundation           y Example: The Karachi Resolution of 1931 articulated fundamental rights and set the vision for a
                        democratic republic.
The Indian National Congress, established in 1885, began as a moderate platform seeking reforms and greater Indian participation
under British rule, but soon evolved into the principal leader of the independence movement through mass mobilization and civil
disobedience. Its journey from a reformist body to a broad-based national movement not only secured India’s freedom but also
laid the groundwork for the country’s democratic and secular framework.

chapters.t: Moderate Phase of the Indian National Congress (1885–1905)
chapters.text:
11                 Moderate Phase of the Indian
                        National Congress (1885–1905)
The Moderate Phase (1885–1905) marked the first organised phase of the Indian National Movement. The leaders sought constitutional
reforms through peaceful means and laid the ideological, organisational, and political foundations of Indian nationalism.
                                                  UPSC Mains PYQs
      1.     To what extent did the role of the moderates prepare a base for the wider freedom
             movement? Comment. (2021)

1. Prominent Moderate Leaders
           Leader                                                      Major Contribution
 Dadabhai Naoroji                 Drain Theory; "Grand Old Man of India"; first Indian to become MP in British Parliament
 Gopal Krishna Gokhale            Servants of India Society; constitutional reforms; mentor of Mahatma Gandhi
 Surendranath Banerjea            Indian Association; political awakening through public meetings
 Pherozeshah Mehta                Municipal reforms; Bombay Presidency leadership
 W.C. Bonnerjee                   First President of INC (1885)
 R.C. Dutt                        Economic critique of British land revenue policies
 D.E. Wacha                       Economic and financial criticism of colonial administration

2. Ideological Foundations of the Moderates
           Principle                                                      Explanation
 Faith in British Liberalism       Believed British Parliament would grant justice if Indian grievances were properly presented.
 Constitutional Agitation          Preferred legal and peaceful methods over confrontation.
 Political Education               Believed public opinion was essential for democratic progress.
 Gradual Reforms                  Favoured incremental political reforms instead of immediate independence. Utilised a strategy
                                  often referred to as the 3Ps method.
                                  y Prayers: Appealing to the British government's sense of justice and fair play.
                                  y Petitions: Sending formal requests, memoranda, and deputations to British authorities and
                                     Parliament.
                                  y Protests: Organizing public meetings, delivering speeches, and using the press to voice
                                     constitutional dissent.
 Economic Nationalism              Exposed colonial exploitation through scientific economic analysis.

3. Role of Moderates in Preparing the Base for the Freedom Movement
                           Role                                                          Examples
 Political Awakening: Popularised the idea of                 Formation of Indian National Congress (1885); annual sessions
 nationalism and united educated Indians across regions.      fostered national unity.
 Creation of National Leadership: Produced the first          Dadabhai Naoroji, Surendranath Banerjea, Pherozeshah Mehta,
 generation of nationalist leaders.                           Gopal Krishna Gokhale.
 Economic Critique of British Rule: Exposed the               Drain Theory (Dadabhai Naoroji), R.C. Dutt's critique of land
 exploitative nature of colonialism through economic          revenue policies.
 analysis.
 Constitutional & Democratic Traditions: Introduced           Demands for elected legislative councils; use of the Indian
 methods such as petitions, resolutions, debates, and         Councils Act, 1892.
 legislative participation.


 Development of National Consciousness: Instilled a           Campaigns on civil rights, representation, and Indianisation of
 sense of common political identity beyond caste,             services.
 religion, and province.
 Promotion of Civil Liberties: Defended freedom of            Opposition to the Vernacular Press Act (1878) and advocacy for
 speech, press, and association.                              press freedom.
 Administrative Reforms: Raised demands for                   Simultaneous ICS examination in India and England; separation
 accountability and efficiency in governance.                 of judiciary from executive.
 Training Ground for Future Leaders: Their methods            Gopal Krishna Gokhale mentored Mahatma Gandhi.
 and institutions nurtured later nationalist leaders.
 Internationalisation of Indian Cause: Took Indian            Dadabhai Naoroji was elected to the British House of Commons
 grievances to British public opinion and Parliament.         (1892).

4. Limitations of the Moderates
                      Limitations                                                        Examples
 Narrow Social Base: Confined mainly to educated             Limited participation of peasants, workers, and women.
 urban elites.
 Faith in British Justice: Overestimated the liberalism      Continued reliance on petitions despite repeated rejection of
 of the British Government.                                  demands.
 Slow Pace of Agitation: Constitutional methods              Frustration after the Indian Councils Act, 1892 provided only
 yielded limited political gains.                            marginal reforms.
 Lack of Mass Mobilisation: Failed to transform              Mass participation emerged only during the Swadeshi Movement
 nationalism into a popular movement.                        (1905).

Although the Moderates secured few immediate concessions, they transformed political consciousness, exposed the economic
foundations of colonial rule, and built the organisational base upon which Extremists and Gandhian mass movements later flourished.
As Bipin Chandra observed, the Moderates were the "political teachers" of modern India, laying the indispensable foundation of the
national movement.
pyq: To what extent did the role of the moderates prepare a base for the wider freedom movement? Comment. (2021)

Limitations of the Moderates Limitations Examples Narrow Social Base: Confined mainly to educated Limited participation of peasants, workers, and women. urban elites. Faith in British Justice: Overestimated the liberalism Continued reliance on petitions despite repeated rejection of of the British Government. demands. Slow Pace of Agitation: Constitutional methods Frustration after the Indian Councils Act, 1892 provided only yielded limited political gains. marginal reforms. Lack of Mass Mobilisation: Failed to transform Mass participation emerged only during the Swadeshi Movement nationalism into a popular movement. (1905)

chapters.text:
12                  Swadeshi Movement

The Swadeshi Movement, launched in 1905 as a response to the partition of Bengal, aimed to promote indigenous goods and
boycott British products to achieve economic self-reliance. It played a crucial role in fostering national unity and awakening political
consciousness across India.
1. Causes of the Swadeshi Movement
          Cause                                               Description and Substantiation
 Partition of Bengal           y Announced by Lord Curzon under the pretext of administrative convenience, but aimed to
 (1905)                          divide Bengal on communal lines to weaken the growing nationalist unity.
                               y Example: Hindu-majority western Bengal separated from Muslim-majority east.
 Safeguarding Bengali          y The Partition of Bengal also targeted the politically active Bengali intelligentsia and disrupted
 Nationalism                     the cultural, linguistic, and economic unity of Bengal. The movement aimed to protect Bengali
                                 unity and national identity through several key strategies.
                               y Example: Strategies included Anti-Partition Protests, Boycott of British Goods, etc.
 Failure of Moderate            Public disillusionment with constitutional methods (petition and prayer) of early Congress
 Politics                       leaders pushed the youth toward more assertive forms of protest.
 Rise of Assertive              Young leaders like Tilak, Bipin Chandra Pal, and Aurobindo Ghosh advocated ‘Swaraj,
 Nationalism                    Swadeshi, and Boycott’ as means to real independence, not just reform.
 Economic Exploitation         y British policies ruined indigenous industries; demand grew for economic self-reliance
 by British                      through Swadeshi industries.
                               y Example: British textiles flooded markets, destroying local handloom sectors.
 Global                         Inspiration from Ireland’s Home Rule Movement and Japanese victory over Russia (1905)
 Influence                      emboldened Indian nationalists with hope that imperial powers could be challenged.

2. Role of Various Social Groups in the Swadeshi Movement
     Group                          Role and Contribution                                              Example
 Students            Played a leading role in boycotting government schools         Widespread student boycotts after Carlyle
                     and colleges; joined national institutions; became active      Circular (1905); Bengal National College saw
                     volunteers in samitis and picketing activities.                high student enrolment.
 Women               Participated in picketing, spinning khadi, and attending       Participated in Raksha Bandhan initiated by
                     public meetings; invoked traditional symbolism to assert       Tagore; women from middle-class households
                     nationalist identity.                                          joined rallies.
 Farmers/            Limited but symbolic participation through support of          Though limited, rural Bengal supported the
 Peasants            Swadeshi goods and local resistance; inspired by               boycott of Manchester cloth; village samitis
                     nationalist messaging.                                         spread the message.
 Artisans            Benefited from and contributed to the revival of               Revival of khadi, handloom weaving, and
                     indigenous industries and crafts; their work became            traditional crafts under Swadeshi branding.
                     symbols of economic nationalism.
 Teachers and        Resigned from colonial institutions; promoted national         Aurobindo Ghosh left a comfortable, high-paying
 Educators           education; contributed to curriculum and administration        position in the Baroda State Service to serve as a
                     of Swadeshi schools.                                           Principal of Bengal National College (1906).
 Business            Supported the establishment of indigenous industries           Indian-owned industries like Bengal Chemicals
 Class               and banks; funded Swadeshi initiatives and national            and Mohini Mills received funding from
                     institutions.                                                  nationalists.

13                 Extremist Phase of INC

The Extremists, emerging prominently after the 1905 Partition of Bengal, adopted a more assertive and extremist approach
compared to the Moderates, advocating for Swaraj (complete self-rule) through methods like boycott, Swadeshi, and mass
mobilization. Leaders such as Bal Gangadhar Tilak, Lala Lajpat Rai, and Bipin Chandra Pal (Lal-Bal-Pal) played a crucial role
in radicalizing the Indian National Movement by emphasizing national pride, direct confrontation with British rule, and fostering
a sense of unity and cultural revival among Indians.

1. Ideological Foundation of Extremists in the Indian National Movement
The Extremists (1905-1920) rejected Moderate constitutionalism, drawing on indigenous nationalism, cultural pride, and self-
reliance to demand Swaraj, reshaping India’s freedom struggle.

14                 Revolutionary
                        Movements in India
The revolutionary movement in India emerged as a radical response to British colonial rule, aiming to overthrow the British through
militant nationalism and armed struggle. Their objectives were to inspire fear among colonial officials and awaken nationalist
consciousness, employing methods such as assassinations, dacoities, and revolutionary conspiracies to challenge British authority.
1. Causes of Early Revolutionary Movements in India

                        protests and the Swadeshi Movement.
 International        y Ethiopia’s victory over Italy in 1896 and Japan’s defeat of Russia in 1905 shattered the myth of
 Influences             European invincibility, inspiring Indian revolutionaries to challenge Britain.
 Dissatisfaction      y Frustration grew among nationalists due to the moderate Congress leaders’ inability to secure
 with Moderates         meaningful concessions, leading many to adopt more radical approaches.
                      y Example: Failure of moderates to stop Bengal Partition led to the rise of extremists like Tilak and
                        Bipin Chandra Pal.

                        militant methods.

3. First Phase of Revolutionary Movements in India
The first phase of revolutionary movements in India (1905-1917) marked a shift towards militant nationalism, driven by
disillusionment with moderate methods and inspired by global anti-colonial struggles. Revolutionaries employed violent tactics
such as assassinations and bombings to challenge British rule and awaken nationalist consciousness, particularly in Bengal,
Maharashtra, and Punjab.

4. Second Phase of Revolutionary Movements in India
The second phase of revolutionary movements in India, emerging mainly in the 1920s, was characterized by a shift towards
organized armed struggle inspired by global events like the Russian Revolution and Irish independence. Disillusioned by the
sudden withdrawal of the Non-Cooperation Movement, revolutionaries embraced socialist and militant ideologies, conducting
notable actions such as the Kakori Train Robbery and the Chittagong Armoury Raid to challenge British colonial rule.
