The revolutionary movement in India, driven by fearless leaders and their unwavering commitment, proved that while the British
could kill the revolutionaries, they could never extinguish their ideas or spirit. As Bhagat Singh famously said, “They may kill me,
but they cannot kill my ideas. They can crush my body, but they will not be able to crush my spirit,” embodying the enduring
legacy of sacrifice and inspiration that fuelled India’s struggle for independence.

15                 Revolutionary
                        Activities Abroad
Indian revolutionaries abroad played a pivotal role in India’s freedom struggle by organising, publishing literature, and building
international networks to challenge British colonial rule. Their activities in countries like Britain, France, Germany, and the
United States not only mobilized the Indian diaspora but also generated global awareness and support for the cause of Indian
independence.
1. Causes for Revolutionary Activities Abroad
           Cause                                                          Description
 Oppressive British             Harsh colonial rule, economic exploitation, and suppression of political rights motivated

 World War I as an              The outbreak of World War I weakened British control and created a chance for
 Opportunity                    revolutionaries to seek alliances with Britain’s enemies, especially Germany, to plan
                                uprisings against colonial rule.
 Influence of Western           Exposure to Western education, political thought, and revolutionary movements inspired
 Ideas                          Indian students and expatriates to mobilise for independence abroad.
 Need for Safe Havens           Stringent Press Acts, sedition laws, and surveillance in India forced revolutionaries to
                                operate from foreign lands where they could publish literature, plan actions, and gather
                                resources without immediate threat.
 Support from Indian            Large Indian communities in places like North America, Southeast Asia, and Europe
 Diaspora                       provided logistical, financial, and moral support for revolutionary activities.
 Formation of International     Indian revolutionaries established connections with anti-British groups and governments
 Networks                       (Germany, Irish Republicans) to coordinate global efforts against British rule in India.
 Discontent Among Indian        Indian soldiers stationed abroad, especially during World War I, were targeted for recruitment
 Soldiers                       into revolutionary plots due to grievances over racial discrimination, poor service
                                conditions, religious concerns, and nationalist propaganda.
 Inspirational Incidents        Events like the Komagata Maru incident and the Singapore Mutiny fuelled anger and a
                                sense of injustice, spurring further revolutionary activities among Indians abroad.

anti-colonial currents and inspiring future generations of nationalists. Despite facing immense challenges, these efforts laid a
strong foundation for international solidarity and support in India’s quest for independence.

16                 Mahatma Gandhi

Mahatma Gandhi was a visionary leader who spearheaded India’s struggle for independence through his philosophy of nonviolent
resistance, known as Satyagraha. Born as Mohandas Karamchand Gandhi on October 2, 1869, in Porbandar, Gujarat, he was
called to the Bar at the Inner Temple (London) in 1891. From 1893 to 1914, he lived in South Africa, where he confronted racial
discrimination and developed his unique political philosophy rooted in truth (Satya) and non-violence (Ahimsa). Gandhi's ability
to unite disparate segments of Indian society and lead them in a peaceful but effective resistance to British colonialism inspired
civil rights movements around the world. He is rightfully regarded as the Father of the Nation.

1. Gandhian Philosophy
     Aspect                                                            Description
 Truth               Gandhi regarded truth as the foundation of all virtues, emphasizing truthfulness in thought, speech, and
 (Satya)             action. He believed the ultimate truth is God and morality, and living by truth leads to personal and
                     social transformation.
 Non-violence        Ahimsa is active love and the highest moral law, going beyond mere absence of violence to include
 (Ahimsa)            refraining from harm in thought, word, and deed. It is the core of Gandhi’s ethical and political
                     philosophy, advocating peaceful resistance to injustice.
 Satyagraha          Meaning ‘holding firmly to truth,’ satyagraha is Gandhi’s method of nonviolent resistance and civil
                     disobedience against oppression, relying on moral courage and self-suffering rather than physical force
                     to achieve justice.
 Sarvodaya           Translated as ‘universal uplift’ or ‘welfare of all,’ sarvodaya reflects Gandhi’s vision of social and economic
                     progress that benefits every individual, emphasising equality, justice, and community well-being.
 Antyodaya           specifies that true progress requires prioritizing the poorest, weakest, and most marginalized person first
                     - lifting the "last person" in society
 Swaraj              Beyond political self-rule, swaraj for Gandhi meant self-restraint, personal responsibility, and freedom
                     for all individuals, including economic and social autonomy rooted in moral and spiritual development.
 Trusteeship         A socio-economic concept where wealthier individuals act as trustees for society’s welfare, using their
                     resources for the common good rather than personal accumulation, promoting ethical capitalism and
                     reducing inequality.
 Swadeshi            Emphasizes self-sufficiency and reliance on locally made goods, promoting economic independence
                     from foreign powers and fostering rural industries, symbolized by the spinning wheel (charkha).
 Simplicity &        Advocates for simple living, minimal consumption, and self-control as means to reduce exploitation,
 Self-discipline     promote sustainability, and cultivate inner moral strength.
 Equality and        Gandhi fought against untouchability, caste discrimination, and gender inequality, promoting dignity
 Social Justice      and rights for marginalized communities as essential to true freedom and social harmony.

Differences between Mahatma Gandhi and Ambedkar Aspect Mahatma Gandhi Dr. B. R. Ambedkar View on Caste Opposed untouchability; initially supported a Called for total annihilation of caste; strongly System reformed varna system; later advocated criticized Hindu texts like Manusmriti for abolition of caste; coined the term "Harijan" legitimising caste oppression. (rejected by Ambedkar). Approach to Moral and spiritual reform through nonviolence Legal and political empowerment through Social Reform (Satyagraha) and mass mobilization. state-led reforms and constitutional safeguards. Separate Opposed separate electorates fearing Hindu Supported separate electorates to secure political Electorates for unity disruption; fasted leading to Poona Pact representation and rights for Dalits. Dalits (1932)

Contemporary Relevance of Mahatma Gandhi Aspect Description Non-violence and y Gandhi’s philosophy of non-violence (Ahimsa) and Satyagraha remains a powerful tool for Satyagraha peaceful protest and conflict resolution worldwide, inspiring movements for civil rights, justice, and government accountability. It promotes moral resistance rather than violence, relevant in today’s struggles against oppression and authoritarianism. y Example: Martin Luther King Jr.'s civil rights movement in the U.S. and Nelson Mandela's anti- apartheid struggle in South Africa were inspired by Gandhian non-violence. Social Justice and y His campaigns against caste discrimination, untouchability, and gender inequality continue to Equality influence efforts toward building inclusive societies, emphasising dignity and rights for marginalised groups. y Example: In a society where manual work was associated with degraded status, Gandhi insisted that all labour held equal value. By daily spinning the charkha (spinning wheel) and performing menial tasks himself, he broke social taboos and promoted an equal economic playing field. Sustainable y Gandhi’s advocacy of simple living, self-sufficiency, and harmony with nature aligns closely with Development and modern environmental concerns like climate change, overconsumption, and sustainable resource Environmentalism use. His emphasis on reducing waste and promoting local economies offers lessons for ecological balance. y Example: In a society where manual work was associated with degraded status, Gandhi insisted that all labour held equal value. By daily spinning the charkha (spinning wheel) and performing menial tasks himself, he broke social taboos and promoted an equal economic playing field. Decentralization y Gandhi’s vision of self-reliant villages and decentralized governance (Gram Swaraj) is relevant and Gram Swaraj for empowering rural India, promoting local development, and strengthening democracy at the grassroots. y Example: The 73rd Constitutional Amendment (1992)

chapters.text:
17                  Home Rule movement

The Home Rule Movement, initiated in 1916 by leaders like Bal Gangadhar Tilak and Annie Besant, aimed to demand self-
government for India within the British Empire through constitutional means. It mobilized widespread public support, laying
the groundwork for the later mass movements in the Indian freedom struggle.
1. Causes for formation of the Home Rule Movement
              Cause                                                        Explanation
 Failure of the 1909               These reforms disappointed Indians as they were provided limited representation and
 Morley-Minto Reforms              failed to meet nationalist aspirations.
 Stagnation after the Surat        Nationalist momentum slowed due to Congress split into Moderates and Extremists; and
 Split (1907)                      Tilak’s imprisonment (1908-14).
 Repression of Revolutionary       Harsh suppression of revolutionaries post-Swadeshi movement created a need for a more
 Activities                        sustainable and assertive political programme.
 Impact of World War I             War-time economic hardships (taxation, price rise, forced recruitment) created
                                   widespread dissatisfaction and a ripe environment for mass mobilisation.
 Revival of Extremist              Tilak's return from Mandalay (1914) and Annie Besant's activism created synergy
 Leadership                        between assertive politics and mass mobilisation.
 Irish Home Rule                   The movement took direct inspiration from the Irish struggle for autonomy under British
 Inspiration                       rule, and sought similar concessions for India.
 Disillusionment with              Many Moderate leaders grew disillusioned with the Congress's annual-only approach
 Congress Inactivity               and lack of assertive action, prompting the formation of year-round Home Rule Leagues.
 Perceived Opportunity             Annie Besant’s declaration, “England’s need is India’s opportunity” captured the
 Amidst British Crisis             nationalist belief that Britain’s wartime vulnerability could be used to press for self-rule.
 Failure of Congress to Act        Besant and Tilak initially proposed Leagues within the Congress in 1915, but failure to
 on Home Rule Demand               gain official support pushed them to act independently by 1916.
2. Structure of Tilak’s and Annie Besant’s Home Rule Leagues
       Aspect                          Tilak’s League                                        Besant’s League
 Founded - HQ            April 1916 - Poona (now Pune).                    September 1916 - Adyar (Madras).
 Area of Operation       Maharashtra (excluding Bombay city),              All-India basis, including Bombay city and excluding
                         Karnataka, Central Provinces, and Berar.          Tilak’s area of HRL.
 Organisational          Regional focus with 6 main branches.              Nationwide network with over 200 branches across
 Reach                                                                     India.
 Key Leaders             Bal Gangadhar Tilak, G.S. Khaparde                Annie Besant, George Arundale (organising
                                                                           secretary), B.P. Wadia, C.P. Ramaswamy Aiyar
 Approach                More assertive and politically direct,            Emphasized constitutional methods, propaganda,
                         advocating passive resistance.                    and political education.
 Membership              Stronger in traditional nationalist strongholds   Broader appeal across moderate Congressmen, women,
 Base                    of Western and Central India.                     and politically dormant regions like Gujarat and Sindh.
 Activities              Lectures, reading rooms, publications,            Public meetings, newspapers (New India), libraries,
                         local league meetings.                            propaganda, social and civic awareness campaigns.
 Focus Areas             Swarajya (self-rule), linguistic states, and      Self-government, anti-liquor laws, opposition to
                         vernacular education. He declared, "Swaraj        repressive laws. Political Education
                         is my birthright and I shall have it" at the      Constitutional Reforms, etc.
                         1916 Belgaum session of the HRL.
 Relative Structure      Tightly controlled and focused.                   Loose and more inclusive (diverse regions and interests).


3. Objectives of the Home Rule League Movement
           Objective                                                      Explanation
Attainment of Self-Government         The core aim was to achieve self-government for India within the British Empire,
(Home Rule)                           similar to dominion status granted to colonies like Australia or Canada.
Promote Political Awareness           To educate and politically mobilize Indians across regions, especially in politically
Among Masses                          dormant areas like Sindh and Gujarat.
Establish Year-Round Political        Unlike the Congress, which met annually, the Leagues aimed for continuous and
Agitation                             decentralized activism through branches and local-level engagement.
Bridge the Gap Between                Sought to unify Congress factions post-Surat Split and involve both ideologies in a
Moderates and Extremists              common platform for national awakening.
Pressurize the British During        y Utilized Britain’s preoccupation with World War I to demand constitutional
WWI                                    reforms and make Indian self-rule a priority.
                                     y Example: Besant’s call - “England’s need is India’s opportunity.”
Create an Organised Network of        Formation of reading rooms, propaganda centres, lecture tours, and grassroots
Political Action                      branches to build an organised national movement.

4. Significance of the Home Rule Movement
     Dimension                                                      Explanation
Revival of National      y It marked a resurgence in political activity after the lull following the Surat Split (1907) and
Movement                   suppression of the Swadeshi and revolutionary phases.
                         y Tilak’s release in 1914 and Besant’s activism reignited nationalist sentiment.
Year-Round Political     y Unlike Congress’s annual sessions, the Leagues ensured continuous grassroots political
Agitation                  mobilisation and engagement.
                         y Besant’s League had over 200 branches; Tilak’s League ran regular public meetings and reading
                           rooms.
Pan-India Expansion      y Extended political consciousness beyond traditional centres like Bengal and Bombay to
of Nationalism             newer regions.
                         y The movement spread to Gujarat, Sindh, Karnataka, Central Provinces, and southern India.
Unity Across             y Temporarily bridged the gap between Moderates and Extremists within the Congress.
Ideological Lines        y Besant and Tilak collaborated despite different styles; Moderates like Subramania Iyer
                           supported the cause.
Influence on Future      y Groomed a new generation of nationalist leaders who would lead the freedom struggle in later
Leaders                    decades.
                         y Jawaharlal Nehru, Jinnah, K.M. Munshi, and C.R. Das were influenced by Home Rule League
                           ideals.
Pressure on British      y Created a national demand that Britain could not ignore India’s demand of self-rule,
Government                 especially during WWI.
                         y Resulted in the Montagu Declaration (1917), promising responsible government in India.
Legitimised Demand       y Made the idea of self-rule mainstream and non-seditious in public and official discourse.
for Self-Government      y Home Rule was now framed within the British Empire, making it acceptable to wider
                           audiences.
Use of Modern            y Popularised organised propaganda through newspapers, pamphlets, lecture tours, and
Political Tools            volunteer networks.
                         y Besant’s New India and Tilak’s Kesari became major platforms for nationalist expression.

5. Reasons for the Decline of the Home Rule Movement
        Reason                                                       Explanation
Montagu Declaration           Promised responsible government in India, pacifying many Moderates and reducing the
(1917)                        urgency of the Home Rule demand.
Arrest and Release of         While her arrest sparked protests, after her release in September 1917 and becoming Congress
Annie Besant                  President (1917), Besant adopted a more conciliatory approach toward the reforms.


 Lack of Organisational        The movement lacked a unified national structure; Tilak’s and Besant’s Leagues operated
 Cohesion                      separately with varying strategies.
 Tilak’s Departure to          Tilak left to fight a libel case in London (against Valentine Chirol), leaving a leadership
 England (1918)                vacuum in his faction.
 Limited Mass Base             The movement largely remained confined to the educated middle class and failed to mobilise
                               peasants, workers, minorities, etc. on a large scale.
 Rise of Gandhian Mass         Gandhi’s arrival and his success in Kheda, Champaran, and Ahmedabad (1917-18) offered a
 Politics                      new, more inclusive and mass-based path to nationalism.
 Communal and Social           Some Muslims, Anglo-Indians, and non-Brahmins saw the movement as dominated by
 Divisions                     high-caste Hindus and stayed away.
 Impact of Montagu-            Further divided nationalist ranks; Moderates were appeased, and Besant herself became less
 Chelmsford Reforms            decisive about further pushing Home Rule.
 (1918)
The Home Rule Movement asserted India’s rightful demand for freedom as a birthright and challenged the subjugation of Indian
interests to British imperial priorities. As Annie Besant stated, “India demands Home Rule for two reasons, one essential and
vital, the other less important but necessary: Firstly, because Freedom is the birthright of every Nation; secondly, because
her most important interests are now made subservient to the interests of the British Empire without her consent.”

18                Non Cooperation movement

The Non-Cooperation Movement, launched by Mahatma Gandhi in 1920, was a nationwide campaign aimed at attaining Swaraj
through non-violent non-cooperation with the colonial government. It encouraged Indians to boycott British goods, institutions,
and honours to assert their demand for self-rule (Swaraj).
                                                UPSC Mains PYQs
      1.    Bring out the constructive programmes of Mahatma Gandhi during Non-Cooperation
            Movement and Civil Disobedience Movement. (2021)

1. Causes of the Non-Cooperation Movement
           Cause                                                        Explanation
 Rowlatt Act                   Passed despite unanimous Indian opposition; allowed imprisonment without trial. It
 (1919)                        symbolised British betrayal post-WWI and was seen as an attack on civil liberties. Sparked
                               nationwide hartals and Satyagraha campaigns led by Gandhi.
 Jallianwala Bagh              General Dyer’s brutal massacre of peaceful protestors in Amritsar led to national outrage. It
 Massacre (April 1919)         exposed the true face of British imperialism and eroded faith in constitutional methods.
 Montagu-Chelmsford            Failed to satisfy Indian aspirations. Nationalists found them “disappointing and
 Reforms (1919)                unsatisfactory”, especially after British promises during WWI.
 Economic Hardships            Wartime inflation, heavy taxation, forced recruitment, and a deadly flu epidemic caused
 Post-WWI                      immense suffering. 12-13 million died (Census 1921). The hardship created fertile ground for
                               mass mobilisation.
 Convergence of Khilafat       The Allied powers' treatment of the Ottoman Empire after World War I and the threatened
 and Nationalist               position of the Ottoman Caliph (confirmed through the Treaty of Sèvres, 1920) angered Indian
 Sentiments                    Muslims. Gandhi supported the Khilafat cause to unite Hindus and Muslims in a joint anti-colonial
                               struggle, enhancing the movement’s inclusivity, paving the way for a pan-India movement.
 Gandhi’s Leadership           Gandhi’s success in Champaran, Kheda, and Ahmedabad gave him credibility as a mass
 and Rise of Mass              leader. His strategy of non-violent Satyagraha provided a unifying, moral force to mobilise
 Politics                      the masses.
 Loss of Faith in              The limited gains from participation in councils, coupled with repressive British policies,
 Constitutional Politics       convinced leaders and masses that constitutional methods were ineffective. Thus, many
                               nationalists began resigning from titles & posts and boycotting institutions.

2. Features and Programme of the Non-Cooperation Movement (1920-22)
    Feature / Programme                                                    Explanation
 Non-violent Non-Cooperation         Gandhi emphasised peaceful resistance, non-violent withdrawal of cooperation from
                                     British institutions, laws, and symbols of authority.
 Boycott of Government               Included boycott of government schools, colleges, courts, legislative councils, and
 Institutions                        official functions. People were urged to disengage from colonial governance structures.
 Resignation from Government         Indians were encouraged to resign from civil services, police, army, and local bodies as
 Services                            a form of protest against British rule.
 Surrender of Titles and             Nationalists were asked to surrender British-conferred titles like 'Sir' by Rabindranath
 Honours                             Tagore and ‘Rai Bahadur’ by Jamna Lal Bajaj to delegitimise colonial prestige.
 Boycott of Foreign Goods            Emphasis was placed on rejecting British-manufactured goods (especially textiles) to
                                     promote Indian industries and economic self-reliance.
 Promotion of Swadeshi,              Hand-spinning and wearing Khadi were promoted to revive cottage industries and
 Charkha and Khadi                   reduce dependence on British goods. Gandhi made it a symbol of national pride.


Establishment of National           Parallel institutions like Kashi Vidyapeeth, Jamia Millia Islamia, and Bihar Vidyapeeth
Institutions                        were founded to replace British-run schools and colleges.
Establishment of Panchayats         These were set up as alternatives to British courts to settle local disputes and promote
and Arbitration Courts              self-governance.
Voluntary courting of arrest        Volunteers were arrested on purpose as a symbolic protest against repressive British
                                    laws, reinforcing civil disobedience.
Tilak Swaraj Fund                   Launched to fund nationalist activities; targeted Rs. 1 crore collection to support
                                    charkhas, volunteers, and national education.
Unity of Khilafat and Swaraj        The programme fused the religious concerns of Muslims (Khilafat) with the political
Demands                             demand for Swaraj, aiming for mass Hindu-Muslim unity.
Phased Plan of Action               Gandhi proposed a gradualist approach starting with boycotts, and escalating to tax
                                    refusal and full civil disobedience if demands were not met.
Mass Mobilisation and               Congress workers, students, and volunteers mobilised villagers, picketed liquor shops,
Volunteer Corps                     and promoted nationalist values across India.

3. Spread and Regional Variations during Non-Cooperation Movement
   Region                       Nature of Participation                                           Example
Punjab            Strong anti-British sentiment post-Jallianwala Bagh;         Akali agitation for Gurudwara reform gained
                  protests led by Akali movement against British control       mass support alongside non-cooperation
                  over Sikh Gurudwaras.                                        campaigns and often shared nationalist support.
Uttar Pradesh     Peasant movements against high rents and forced              Baba Ramchandra mobilized peasants in Rae
(Awadh)           begar; aligned with Non-Cooperation under Kisan              Bareli and Pratapgarh.
                  Sabhas.
Andhra            Powerful no-tax campaigns and boycott of British             Students left government schools in Guntur and
Pradesh           institutions; active student and teacher participation.      Nellore; nationalist education spread.
Bihar             Boycott of schools and law courts; peasant                   Rajendra Prasad led movements in Patna and
                  mobilisation against zamindars and colonial taxation.        Muzaffarpur.
Gujarat           Strong participation in picketing liquor shops and           Gandhi’s influence in Ahmedabad and Kheda
                  boycott of foreign cloth; organised campaigns under          made Gujarat a hub for disciplined satyagraha.
                  Gandhi’s guidance.
Bengal            Extensive student movement and boycott of foreign            Subhas Chandra Bose and C.R. Das led
                  goods; Muslim participation strong due to Khilafat           successful boycotts in Calcutta.
                  sentiment.
Tamil Nadu        Focused on social boycott of foreign goods, picketing        C. Rajagopalachari led the campaign in Salem;
                  of liquor shops, and spread of khadi.                        women actively participated in picketing
                                                                               campaigns.
Maharashtra       Urban middle-class and workers supported hartals and         Non-cooperation activities were organised in
                  Swadeshi; rural support was more muted.                      Poona and Nagpur under Tilakites and
                                                                               Gandhians.
Assam             Active boycott of British goods and schools;                 Participation of Assamese intelligentsia and
                  participation was mainly among students, middle              student unions from Gauhati and Dibrugarh.
                  classes and sections of tea garden labour.
Kerala            Linked with agrarian issues and communal                     Moplah rebellion (1921) began as agrarian
(Malabar)         grievances; movement turned violent in some pockets          discontent but turned into a violent anti-
                  (e.g., Moplah uprising).                                     landlord movement.

4. Reasons for Withdrawal of the Non-Cooperation Movement
         Reason                                                       Explanation
Chauri Chaura               A violent clash in Gorakhpur (U.P.) where protestors set a police station on fire, killing 22
Incident                    policemen. Gandhi believed the people were insufficiently trained in non-violence. Spontaneous,
(February 1922)             sometimes emotional responses of the masses risked the ethical foundation of Satyagraha.


Eruption of Violence in     Instances like the Moplah Rebellion (Malabar) and localised violent outbursts made Gandhi
Other Regions               realise the danger of the movement spiralling out of control.
Fear of Mass                In places like Rae Bareli, Pratapgarh, and Awadh, local peasant movements were turning
Movement Turning            increasingly radical, occasionally looting bazaars and clashing violently with landlords, which
Revolutionary               went against Gandhi's strict principle of Ahimsa (absolute non-violence). Congress leaders,
                            including Gandhi, wanted a controlled mass movement, not a social revolution.
Weakening of Khilafat       The Non-Cooperation Movement was deeply tied to the Khilafat Movement (protesting the
Issue                       dismantling of the Ottoman Caliphate). By 1922, Turkey itself moved toward becoming a secular
                            republic under Mustafa Kemal Atatürk, making the original Khilafat issue increasingly irrelevant
                            to Indian Muslims.
Lack of Organisational      Absence of parallel institutions to fully replace British schools, courts, and services limited the
Infrastructure              practical impact and sustainability of the boycott.
British Repression and      The colonial government responded with harsh repression, mass arrests, press censorship, and
Arrests                     banning volunteer corps. Most top Congress leaders were jailed.
Internal Congress           Differences within Congress leadership (for instance, over council boycotts and strategy) created
Differences                 hurdles in sustaining momentum. Leaders such as C.R. Das differed with Gandhi over
                            strategy, particularly after the withdrawal, eventually leading to the formation of the Swaraj Party.
Strategic Pause             Gandhi believed in moral preparation before major resistance. He intended the withdrawal
by Gandhi                   not as a failure, but as a means to reorganise and restrategise the national movement for future
                            campaigns.

5. Significance of the Non-Cooperation Movement
   Significance                                                      Explanation
Beginning of Mass         y Marked the transition from elite-led to mass-based politics. Millions of peasants, workers,
Nationalism                 women, students, and traders participated across India.
                          y Example: Over 300 branches of Oudh Kisan Sabha were set up within months under Jawaharlal
                            Nehru and Baba Ram Chandra in Awadh.
Gandhi as the             y Elevated Gandhi from a regional activist to the undisputed leader of the national movement. He
National Leader             redefined politics with his principles of non-violence and Satyagraha.
                          y Example: Gandhi’s leadership in Champaran and Kheda laid the groundwork for his national
                            appeal.
Hindu-Muslim Unity        y The fusion of Khilafat and Swaraj demands led to unprecedented communal unity in mass
at its Peak                 protests.
                          y Example: Joint participation in the 1920 Calcutta Congress by leaders like Shaukat Ali and
                            Gandhi.
Delegitimisation of       y Massive boycotts of institutions like schools, courts, and titles signalled a withdrawal of
British Authority           legitimacy from colonial rule.
                          y Example: Lawyers like C.R. Das and Motilal Nehru gave up their legal practice; students
                            joined national schools.
Promotion of              y Revived indigenous industries and promoted Khadi and Charkha as tools of economic
Swadeshi and                resistance.
Self-Reliance             y Example: Imports of foreign cloth dropped from ₹102 crore to ₹57 crore between 1921-22.
Women’s Political         y Women entered public spaces in large numbers picketing liquor shops, spinning khadi, joining
Participation               rallies.
                          y Example: Women gave up purdah and donated jewellery to the Tilak Swaraj Fund.
Creation of               y National schools, arbitration courts, and local panchayats were established, laying the
Alternative                 foundation for self-rule.
Institutions              y Example: Jamia Millia Islamia and Kashi Vidyapeeth were founded as national educational
                            institutions.
Civil Disobedience        y Established the efficacy of mass non-violent civil resistance, which shaped later movements like
as a Strategic Model        Civil Disobedience (1930) and Quit India (1942).


Politicisation of the      Spread political awareness to rural areas and non-traditional political classes like tribals and
Masses                     plantation workers.
                           Example: Alluri Sitaram Raju in Andhra led tribals in revolt against forest laws, inspired by Gandhi
                           in his initial days of revolt.
Tested the Limits          The Chauri Chaura incident tested Gandhi’s doctrine of non-violence, influencing his approach to
of Non-Violence            future campaigns.

6. Limitations of the Non-Cooperation Movement
     Limitation                                                        Explanation
Inadequate Mass              y Many participants misunderstood the essence of non-violence and discipline. Spontaneous
Discipline                     violent incidents occurred despite Gandhi’s instructions.
                             y Example: Chauri Chaura incident where protestors burned a police station, killing 22
                               policemen, led to the abrupt withdrawal of the movement.
Lack of Organisational       No sufficient national alternatives were ready to replace government schools, courts, and
Infrastructure               services, leading to practical difficulties in sustaining the boycott.
Limited Reach in             The movement failed to penetrate uniformly across India. The response was weak in regions like parts
Certain Regions              of Madras Presidency, Punjab cities, and Maharashtra (due to caste divisions and local politics).
Internal Divisions           C.R. Das and Motilal Nehru accepted the boycott during the movement but later favoured council
within Congress              entry after its withdrawal, leading to the Swaraj Party (1923).
Over-reliance on             The movement was heavily centred around Gandhi's personal charisma and ideology. There was
Gandhi’s Leadership          no second line of leadership ready to take charge when Gandhi was arrested or stepped back.
Unrealistic                  y Masses often had their own interpretation of “Swaraj”, expecting immediate land
Expectations of Masses         redistribution, rent remission, or local justice. This deviated from Congress's moderate
                               objectives.
                             y Example: In Awadh, peasants believed Gandhi had sanctioned land redistribution and no-tax
                               campaigns.
Religious Basis of           Though it fostered short-term Hindu-Muslim unity, the merging of religious and political
Khilafat Tie-Up              agendas (Khilafat and Swaraj) was later criticised for weakening long-term secular politics.
No Concrete Political        Despite large-scale mobilisation, the movement did not result in any tangible political
Gains                        concessions from the British. Swaraj remained undefined and elusive.
Severe British               The British banned volunteer corps, censored the press, and arrested key leaders. This
Repression                   crippled the momentum and morale of the movement.
Withdrawal Seen as           The sudden withdrawal after the Chauri Chaura incident disappointed and demoralised many
Premature by Many            supporters who felt the movement was gaining traction.

7. Constructive Programmes of Mahatma Gandhi during Non-Cooperation Movement
 Constructive                                                       Explanation
 Programme
Promotion of            Encouraged hand-spinning and hand-weaving to promote Swadeshi, generate rural employment, and
Khadi and               reduce dependence on British textiles.
Charkha
National                Established and promoted national educational institutions such as Kashi Vidyapeeth, Gujarat
Education               Vidyapith, Jamia Millia Islamia, and Bihar Vidyapeeth to provide education free from colonial
                        influence.
Hindu–Muslim            Strengthened communal harmony by integrating the Khilafat issue with the national movement and
Unity                   promoting inter-religious cooperation.
Removal of              Worked for the upliftment of the so-called "Depressed Classes" through social reform, equal access to
Untouchability          public spaces, and campaigns against untouchability.
Village Industries      Promoted cottage and village industries to strengthen the rural economy and achieve self-reliance.
(Gramodyog)


 Prohibition           Organised picketing of liquor shops and advocated prohibition to improve public health and social
                       welfare.
 Women's               Encouraged women to join the freedom struggle through picketing, spinning khadi, fundraising, and
 Participation         public mobilisation.
 Communal and          Promoted unity across caste, religion, and region to build a broad-based national movement.
 Social Harmony
 Volunteer Corps          Organised disciplined volunteers to spread nationalist ideas, conduct constructive work, and assist in
 (Seva Dal)               peaceful mobilisation.
The Non-Cooperation Movement marked a significant shift towards mass participation and non-violent resistance against
British rule, laying the foundation for future freedom struggles. As Gandhi stated, “Non-cooperation with evil is as much a
duty as is cooperation with good," highlighting the ethical imperative behind the movement.

Institutions y Example: Jamia Millia Islamia and Kashi Vidyapeeth were founded as national educational institutions. Civil Disobedience y Established the efficacy of mass non-violent civil resistance, which shaped later movements like as a Strategic Model Civil Disobedience (1930)

Limitations of the Non-Cooperation Movement Limitation Explanation Inadequate Mass y Many participants misunderstood the essence of non-violence and discipline. Spontaneous Discipline violent incidents occurred despite Gandhi’s instructions. y Example: Chauri Chaura incident where protestors burned a police station, killing 22 policemen, led to the abrupt withdrawal of the movement. Lack of Organisational No sufficient national alternatives were ready to replace government schools, courts, and Infrastructure services, leading to practical difficulties in sustaining the boycott. Limited Reach in The movement failed to penetrate uniformly across India. The response was weak in regions like parts Certain Regions of Madras Presidency, Punjab cities, and Maharashtra (due to caste divisions and local politics). Internal Divisions C.R. Das and Motilal Nehru accepted the boycott during the movement but later favoured council within Congress entry after its withdrawal, leading to the Swaraj Party (1923)

chapters.text:
19                 Civil Disobedience Movement

The Civil Disobedience Movement was launched by Gandhi after Viceroy Lord Irwin rejected his letter (31 January 1930) containing
the Eleven Demands, with the strategic choice to defy the salt law, a basic necessity heavily taxed and monopolised by the British.
This culminated in the historic Dandi March in 1930, where Gandhi and his followers openly broke the salt laws, marking the
official start of the movement and a new phase of mass nonviolent resistance.
                                                 UPSC Mains PYQs
      1.    Many voices had strengthened and enriched the nationalist movement during the
            Gandhian Phase. Elaborate. (2019)

1. Features of the Civil Disobedience Movement (CDM)
        Feature                                                          Description
 Non-violent                 The movement was based on Gandhi’s principle of non-violence (ahimsa), involving peaceful
 Resistance                  protests such as refusal to obey unjust laws, picketing, and boycotts.
 Breaking of Salt            The symbolic act of defying the British salt monopoly by making salt from seawater, initiated by
 Laws                        Gandhi’s Dandi March in 1930, marked the formal start of the movement.
 Mass Participation          Diverse sections of society including peasants, women, students, business communities, and
                             tribal groups actively participated, making it a truly nationwide movement.
 Boycott of British          Widespread boycott of foreign cloth, liquor, and other British goods aimed to weaken the
 Goods                       colonial economy and assert Indian self-reliance.
 Refusal to Pay              Indians refused to pay taxes such as salt tax, land revenue, and other government levies at many
 Taxes                       places (e.g., parts of Gujarat and U.P.) as a form of economic non-cooperation.
 Resignation from            As part of Mahatma Gandhi’s broader noncooperation strategy, Indian officials, village headmen (like
 Government Services         chowkidars), and legal professionals gave up their posts to undermine British administrative control.
 Picketing and Court         Protesters picketed liquor shops and foreign cloth stores and boycotted British courts by
 Boycotts                    refusing to participate in litigation under colonial laws.

2. Causes of the Civil Disobedience Movement
           Cause                                                         Description
 Rejection of Eleven         Gandhi’s eleven demands, which included the abolition of the salt tax, reduction of land
 Demands                     revenue, and release of political prisoners, were ignored by the British government, compelling
                             Gandhi to initiate the Civil Disobedience Movement as a form of protest.
 Demand for Complete         The declaration of Purna Swaraj (complete independence) at the Lahore Congress Session in
 Independence                1929 marked a decisive shift from seeking dominion status to demanding full freedom,
                             galvanizing the Indian masses to actively participate in the movement.
 Salt Tax and                The British government’s monopoly over salt production and the heavy taxation on this
 Monopoly                    essential commodity symbolized colonial economic exploitation, making the salt law an effective
                             and relatable rallying point for mass civil disobedience.
 Frustration over            The rejection of the Nehru Report’s proposal for dominion status by the British authorities led
 Dominion Status             to widespread disillusionment among Indian leaders and intensified the demand for complete
                             independence through more assertive means.
 Protests Against            The British crackdown on revolutionary nationalists and political activists, including arrests
 Arrests of                  and repression (especially of Bhagat Singh), fuelled public anger and increased support for a
 Revolutionaries             mass movement challenging colonial authority.


Failure of                 The inability of the Indian National Congress and other leaders to secure meaningful concessions
Constitutional             during the Round Table Conferences demonstrated the futility of constitutional methods,
Negotiations               prompting a return to direct mass action.
Symbolic Assertion         Gandhi’s strategic decision to break the salt law at Dandi was a powerful symbolic act aimed at
through Dandi March        uniting Indians across social strata in nonviolent resistance, thereby igniting widespread
                           participation in the Civil Disobedience Movement.

3. Participation in the Civil Disobedience Movement
 Social Group                                                       Description
Urban Areas           Support for Gandhian ideology was relatively lower than during the Non-Cooperation Movement,
                      with fewer lawyers quitting practice and limited student involvement, partly due to the rising
                      influence of revolutionary nationalists like Bhagat Singh.
Muslims               Muslim participation was significantly less compared to the Non-Cooperation Movement, and
                      communal tensions were evident in some regions.
Merchants             The Calcutta Marwaris, led by G.D. Birla, showed solidarity with the nationalist cause, with business
                      communities, especially Gujaratis, providing strong support in the movement’s early phase.
Women                 Women actively engaged in picketing liquor shops, opium dens, and foreign cloth stores, marking a
                      liberating and empowering experience for them.
Peasants              Large-scale peasant protests were organized against British policies, with widespread participation in
                      rural areas.
Tribal                In regions like the Central Provinces, Maharashtra, and Karnataka, tribal groups defied oppressive forest
Communities           laws by grazing cattle without paying grazing fees and cutting restricted timber in reserved forests
Students and          Students played a vital role by organizing protests, strikes, and boycotts, and were actively involved
Youth                 in the Salt Satyagraha, energizing the movement.

4. Why was Salt chosen for the Civil Disobedience Movement?
      Aspect                                                         Description
Essential               Salt was a basic necessity used daily by every Indian, regardless of class or region, making it a
Commodity for All       unifying symbol for mass participation in the movement.
British Monopoly        The British government imposed a strict monopoly on salt production and sales through the Salt
and Taxation            Act of 1882, forcing Indians to buy heavily taxed salt from government depots, which symbolized
                        economic exploitation and colonial control.
Economic Burden         The salt tax disproportionately affected the poorest sections of society, as salt was indispensable for
on the Poor             food and health, making the tax a direct and visible form of oppression.
Symbolic and            Breaking the salt laws by making salt from seawater was a simple, nonviolent act of defiance that
Practical Protest       challenged British authority and was easy for millions to replicate, thereby mobilizing
                        widespread civil disobedience.
Strategic Impact on     Salt was a major source of revenue for the British colonial government, so attacking the salt tax
British Revenue         directly hit the economic interests of the British Raj, increasing the movement’s political
                        significance.
Historical              The Salt March and subsequent protests attracted national and international attention,
Significance and        highlighting the injustice of British rule and galvanizing Indian nationalist sentiment.
Visibility

5. Civil Disobedience Movement at Different Places
       Place                                                          Description
Dandi (Gujarat)         The movement formally began on April 6, 1930, when Mahatma Gandhi broke the salt law at
                        Dandi after the 24-day Dandi March (March 12 - April 6, 1930).
                        Gandhi led the march accompanied by 78 followers, inspiring a nationwide civil disobedience
                        campaign against the British salt monopoly.


Champaran               The Chaukidari tax boycott (landlocked); Nakhas Talab (Patna) was chosen for symbolic salt making
(Bihar)                 led by Ambika Kant Sinha.
Tamil Nadu              C. Rajagopalachari led the salt satyagraha with a march from Tiruchirapalli to Vedaranniyam in
                        1930, breaking the salt law on the Tanjore coast.
                        The movement also included picketing foreign cloth shops and anti-liquor campaigns.
Bengal                  Subhas Chandra Bose and J.M. Sengupta organized salt satyagraha and other civil disobedience
                        activities in 1930, mobilizing urban and rural populations against British rule.
Malabar (Kerala)        Leaders like K. Kelappan and P. Krishna Pillai organised salt marches from Calicut to Payyanur
                        in 1930, encouraging widespread participation in the movement.
Peshawar (North-        Khan Abdul Ghaffar Khan led the movement by organizing a volunteer brigade and mobilizing
West Frontier           local support. Notably, soldiers of the Garhwal Rifles refused to fire on unarmed protestors
Province)               during this period (1930-31).
Dharasana               After Gandhi’s arrest, Sarojini Naidu, Imam Sahib, and Manilal led the famous raid on the
(Gujarat)               Dharasana Salt Works in 1930, continuing the momentum of the salt satyagraha through
                        nonviolent protest.
United Provinces        Jawaharlal Nehru led no-revenue and no-rent campaigns in 1930-31, mobilizing peasants and
(UP)                    urban workers in civil disobedience against British taxation policies.
Orissa                  Gopalbandhu Choudhuri led satyagraha activities in coastal regions such as Balasore, Cuttack,
                        and Puri districts during the movement, involving local populations in protests against colonial
                        laws.
Punjab                  CDM was primarily led by Maulana Habib-ur-Rehman Ludhianvi and Lala Duni Chand of
                        Ambala. They mobilised the masses through non-violent protests, boycotts, and civil defiance of
                        British colonial laws.

6. British Government’s response to the Civil Disobedience Movement
  Government                                                       Description
   Response
Repression              y The British authorities arrested over 60,000 people, including Mahatma Gandhi and other
and Arrests               leaders, using police brutality, lathi charges, tear gas, and bullets to suppress protests. Many
                          women and children were imprisoned.
                        y Example: Gandhi’s arrest in May 1930 during the Salt Satyagraha.
Use of Force and        y Martial law was imposed in some areas to give military authorities greater powers to crush
Martial Law               protests. Public gatherings were banned, homes raided, and newspapers censored.
                        y Example: Martial law was declared in some parts of Bengal and Gujarat during peak protests.
Economic Measures       y Heavy fines and penalties were imposed on participants to deter involvement. The government
and Penalties             strictly enforced laws and prosecuted those defying British regulations.
                        y Example: Peasants in Gujarat faced fines and land seizures for refusing to pay taxes.
Negotiations and        y The British government negotiated with Gandhi, resulting in the Gandhi-Irwin Pact (March 1931),
Gandhi-Irwin Pact         which included the release of political prisoners and allowed peaceful picketing of salt. Gandhi
                          agreed to suspend the movement temporarily to attend the Second Round Table Conference in
                          London.
Censorship and          y The British declared the Indian National Congress and its associated organizations illegal,
Banning                   censored correspondence, and restricted communication to weaken the nationalist movement.
Organizations           y Example: The Congress was declared illegal many times during the course of the movement.
Violent Clashes and     y Despite repression, violent incidents occurred in places like Calcutta, Karachi, and Gujarat.
Public Backlash           Gandhi maintained his commitment to nonviolence and appealed for peace, which increased
                          international sympathy for India’s cause.
                        y Example: Violence erupted in Calcutta in 1930, but Gandhi condemned it firmly.

7. Significance of the Civil Disobedience Movement
         Significance                                                  Description
Mass Mobilization and           The movement involved a wide cross-section of Indian society, including peasants, women,
National Unity                  students, and urban workers, fostering unprecedented mass participation and strengthening
                                the sense of national unity across regions and communities.


Institutionalization of        It established non-violence (ahimsa) and civil disobedience as powerful and effective tools
Non-violent Resistance         against colonial rule, shaping the ideological framework for future freedom struggles like the
                               Quit India Movement.
Challenge to British           By openly defying laws such as the salt tax, the movement directly challenged the
Authority                      legitimacy of British colonial rule, disrupting administrative and economic control and
                               exposing British vulnerabilities.
International Attention        The movement attracted global media coverage and international sympathy, embarrassing
and Sympathy                   the British government and highlighting the moral and political injustices of colonialism
                               on the world stage.
Political Radicalization       The movement marked a shift towards more radical demands like Purna Swaraj (complete
and Congress Growth            independence), leading to the growth of Congress’s political influence and the rise of socialist
                               ideas among younger Congress leaders (the Congress Socialist Party came later (1934)).
Foundation for Future          It laid the groundwork for subsequent campaigns, including the Quit India Movement, by
Movements                      building organisational strength, political awareness, and a culture of mass resistance.
Empowerment of                 Women, peasants, and tribal communities actively participated, gaining political
Marginalized Groups            awareness and social empowerment, which broadened the base of the nationalist
                               movement.

8. Limitations of the Civil Disobedience Movement
        Aspect                                                       Description
Limited Participation      y Congress failed to fully address the specific political demands of many Dalit leaders,
of Dalits and Depressed      resulting in limited participation from sections of the Depressed Classes.
Classes                    y Example: Leaders like Dr B.R. Ambedkar criticised the Congress for not adequately
                             addressing Dalit issues during the movement.
Low Muslim                 y Muslim participation remained limited due to political differences, communal tensions, and the
Participation and            Muslim League's stance.
Communal Distrust          y Example: Muslim League’s limited support and communal riots in some regions during the
                             movement.
Disappointment Among       y Rich peasants were disillusioned when the movement was called off in 1931 without revision
Rich Peasants                of oppressive revenue rates, weakening their support.
                           y Example: Peasants in Gujarat and UP expressed dissatisfaction after the suspension of the
                             movement without economic relief.
Limited Support from       The Congress’s close ties with business elites led to the industrial working class’s grievances
Industrial Working         being overlooked, resulting in limited participation.
Class                      Example: Labour strikes were less prominent during CDM compared to earlier movements like
                           the Non-Cooperation Movement.
Dependence on              y The movement’s momentum was heavily reliant on Gandhi; his arrests caused disruptions
Gandhi’s Leadership          and loss of direction.
                           y Example: After Gandhi’s arrest in May 1930, the movement faced organizational challenges
                             and local leaders took varied approaches.
Failure to Achieve         y Despite mass participation, the movement did not force the British to grant full independence,
Complete Independence        leading only to partial concessions.
                           y Example: The Gandhi-Irwin Pact of 1931 led to temporary suspension of the movement
                             without achieving Purna Swaraj.
Economic Hardships         y Boycotts caused economic difficulties, and harsh British repression including arrests and
and British Repression       violence weakened the movement’s sustainability.
                           y Example: Mass arrests of thousands of protesters and violent crackdowns in places like
                             Calcutta and Gujarat.


9. Comparison between the Non-Cooperation Movement and the Civil
    Disobedience Movement
     Aspect            Non-Cooperation Movement (NCM)                     Civil Disobedience Movement (CDM)
 Goal                To unify Hindus and Muslims and attain             To achieve Purna Swaraj (complete
                     Swaraj (self-rule), without explicitly demanding   independence) from British rule.
                     complete independence.
 Methodology         Refusal to cooperate with the British              Open violation of specific laws, especially the salt
                     administration by boycotting government            laws, refusal to pay taxes, and active civil
                     schools, offices, courts, and foreign goods.       disobedience against British policies.
 Nature of           Passive resistance through non-cooperation and     Active defiance by breaking laws considered unjust,
 Protest             boycott.                                           such as making salt illegally.
 Mass                Large-scale participation, including significant   Widespread participation with notable involvement
 Participation       involvement of the Muslim class; women’s           of women, peasants, and business groups; Muslim
                     participation was limited.                         participation was relatively lower.
 Geographical        NCM was a nationwide movement, though the          It was more widespread across India, covering
 Spread              CDM had a broader and deeper rural penetration.    both urban and rural areas extensively.
 Involvement of      High involvement of lawyers quitting practice      Decreased participation from intelligentsia like
 Intelligentsia      and students boycotting government schools.        lawyers and students compared to NCM.
 Labour              Simultaneous labour uprisings and strikes          Labour movements were less prominent during
 Movement            were prominent during this period.                 CDM.
 Organizational      Developing organizational framework of the         A more mature and stronger organisational
 Structure           Congress.                                          structure enabling better coordination.
 Government          Arrests and repression but fewer in number         More intense repression with approximately three
 Repression          compared to CDM.                                   times more imprisonments than NCM.
 Withdrawal          Called off abruptly in 1922 after the Chauri       Suspended after the Gandhi-Irwin Pact (1931),
                     Chaura incident due to violence.                   resumed in 1932, and finally withdrawn in 1934.

The Civil Disobedience Movement exemplified the principle that “Civil disobedience becomes a sacred duty when the state
becomes lawless or corrupt” as articulated by Mahatma Gandhi. It united diverse sections of Indian society in a disciplined,
nonviolent struggle against colonial injustice, laying a strong foundation for India’s eventual independence.

chapters.text:
20                  Quit India Movement

The Quit India Movement was launched by the All India Congress Committee at Bombay on 8 August 1942 through the Quit India
Resolution, which was a mass civil disobedience campaign launched by the Indian National Congress demanding an immediate
end to British rule. Gandhi’s powerful “Do or Die” call, urging Indians to act decisively, galvanised the nation, imprinting the
resolve for freedom in public consciousness and inspiring widespread participation in the struggle for independence.

                                                  UPSC Mains PYQs
      1.      What were the events that led to the Quit India Movement? Point out its results. (2024)


1. Features of Quit India Movement
    Aspect                                                          Description
 Objectives         It aimed for the immediate end of British rule, Formation of a Provisional Government of Free India after
                    British withdrawal, and the declaration of a free India. The resolution clearly reflected the unequivocal
                    demand for self-governance.
 Nature             Unlike earlier non-violent movements, this was a mass uprising that accommodated limited violence and
                    focused on total defiance, involving all societal sections, not just trained activists. Gandhi’s shift from
                    traditional Satyagraha was evident when he permitted self-defence actions against a strong oppressor,
                    aligning with the public’s radical mood.
 Leaderless         Following the arrests of senior leaders on August 8-9, 1942, it transformed into a grassroots movement
 Movement           driven by young activists and local leaders. Figures like Aruna Asaf Ali sustained momentum through
                    underground networks, ensuring the movement’s continuity despite British repression.
2. Causes of the Quit India Movement
           Cause                                                         Explanation
 Failure of the Cripps       Cripps offered Dominion status after WWII with no immediate power transfer. Congress
 Mission (1942)              demanded full independence, leading to deep disappointment.
                             Gandhi called the offer a “post-dated cheque on a crashing bank.”
 World War II                y Wartime inflation, food shortages, high taxation, and forced procurement caused severe
 Economic Hardships            public distress.
                             y Example: To fund World War II and suppress the Quit India Movement (QIM), the British
                               colonial administration printed vast amounts of unbacked paper currency. This drastically
                               devalued the currency, severely escalating the hardships of the working classes and peasantry
 British repression and      Continued colonial exploitation and refusal to negotiate with elected Indian leadership despite
 unkept promises             Congress’s decision to support war efforts of colonial power. This bred resentment among nationalists.
 Discontent from             The failure of earlier movements like Civil Disobedience and Individual Satyagraha created
 Earlier Movements           frustration and a readiness for more radical action.
 Growing Nationalist         By 1942, people were impatient for complete freedom. Nationalist fervour had spread to
 Sentiment                   students, workers, and the rural poor.
 British Military            y Fall of Singapore and Burma raised fears of Japanese invasion; exposed British vulnerability
 Reverses in Asia              and stirred urgency among Indians to take charge.
                             y Example: Retreat of British forces from Burma in 1942.
 Support for Pakistan        British encouragement of communal demands and the Muslim League's Pakistan resolution alienated
 by British                  Congress and further strained Congress–League relations and deepened political polarisation.
 Gandhi’s Radical            Gandhi, sensing public anger, called for “Do or Die”, a shift from his earlier cautious approach.
 Stand
 Popular Belief in “Now      The combination of global conflict, British weakness, and nationalist unity created a sentiment
 or Never”                   that this was the final opportunity to oust the British.


3. Spread of the Quit India Movement (1942-44)
   Region            Nature of Participation and Activities                                Example
Bihar            Became a hub of underground resistance with          Example: Jayaprakash Narayan and Rammanohar
                 active involvement of students, peasants, and        Lohia led the underground Azad Dastas near the
                 socialist leaders. Violent clashes and sabotage      Nepal border.
                 were common.
Uttar Pradesh    Widespread public unrest marked by student           Example: BHU served as the primary student nerve
                 protests, armed attacks on police stations, and      centre for the uprising in the region. Students led
                 looting of colonial offices.                         strikes, disrupted government operations, and
                                                                      organised secret networks to distribute nationalist
                                                                      literature and coordinate sabotage.
Bengal           Saw urban sabotage and militant actions,             Example: Worker and peasant uprisings in Calcutta
                 along with rural participation from tribals and      and rural Bengal; strategic sabotage of communication
                 peasants who merged their local struggles with       lines.
                 nationalist goals.
Maharashtra      Known for guerrilla-style resistance and             Example: Formation of the Prati Sarkar (parallel
                 well-coordinated underground networks led by         government) in Satara (1943-45) under Nana Patil
                 socialists and grassroots leaders.                   and Y.B. Chavan; Usha Mehta operated a clandestine
                                                                      radio station in Bombay.
Gujarat          Strong student and worker participation with         Example: The brief establishment of an “Azad
                 a focus on underground publishing and                Government” in Ahmedabad.
                 symbolic acts of defiance.
Odisha           Witnessed active tribal and student                  Example: The Nandapur and Umerkote Protests of
                 mobilisation, especially in resisting colonial       August 1942 characterised by overwhelming
                 economic and forest policies.                        participation from local tribal (Adivasi) targeted
                                                                      symbols of colonial exploitation, including British
                                                                      police stations, liquor shops, and revenue offices
Tamil Nadu       Trade unions and youth organisations played a        Example: the Buckingham and Carnatic (B&C) Mills,
                 key role through strikes, protests, and              Simpson and Company, and the Madras Port Trust
                 nationalist propaganda.                              launched crippling, weeks-long strikes in August
                                                                      1942.
Assam            Movement led by student activists, targeting         Example: Students in Gauhati and Dibrugarh led
                 railway lines and telegraph services to disrupt      sabotage campaigns and boycotts of colonial
                 colonial administration.                             institutions.

4. Impact of the Quit India Movement
   Dimension                                Impact                                            Example
Radicalisation of        Marked a shift from passive to assertive          Gandhi’s call for “Do or Die” inspired mass
National Movement        resistance; reflected growing mass impatience     civil disobedience, strikes, sabotage, and
                         and readiness for direct action.                  guerrilla tactics.
Mass Participation       Witnessed unprecedented participation from all    Aruna Asaf Ali on August 9, 1942, after British
                         social groups, including students, peasants,      authorities arrested Mahatma Gandhi hoisted
                         women, and even government employees.             the INC flag at Gowalia Tank Maidan before a
                                                                           massive crowd, defying a police lathi charge
Emergence of             Locally formed “people’s governments”             Parallel governments under Chittu Pandey in Ballia
Parallel                 challenged British authority and took over        (U.P.), Jatiya Sarkar in Tamluk (Bengal), and Prati
Governments              administrative roles in some regions.             Sarkars in Satara (Maharashtra) were formed.
Underground              Due to mass arrests of senior leaders,            Leaders like Jayaprakash Narayan,
Revolutionary            underground networks ensured continuity           Rammanohar Lohia, Usha Mehta kept resistance
Networks                 through secret communications and planning.       alive through covert operations.
British Repression       Over 100,000 arrests, widespread violence, and    Churchill’s war cabinet faced moral criticism;
and Global Pressure      economic disruption weakened British              the U.S. and China pressed for Indian
                         legitimacy and drew global criticism.             independence.


 End of Illusions          Made clear to Indians that the British would not     Gandhi’s moral authority strengthened despite
 about British Intent      voluntarily give up power unless forced by           suppression of the movement.
                           unified, mass resistance.
 Catalyst for Post-        Though suppressed, the movement paved the            The British began considering transfer of power
 War Developments          way for later events like the INA trials, naval      seriously after WWII, evident in the Cabinet
                           mutiny, and the final phase of negotiations          Mission (1946).
                           leading to independence.

5. Limitations of the Quit India Movement
          Limitation                                                         Explanation
 Lack of Organised                 y Most top Congress leaders were arrested within 24 hours of launch, leaving the
 Leadership                          movement without direction and coordination.
                                   y Example: Gandhi, Nehru, and Patel were jailed on 9 August 1942.
 Absence of Strategic              The movement lacked a detailed programme or strategy to sustain momentum after initial
 Planning                          repression, leading to disjointed and spontaneous acts.
 Limited Participation of          Muslim League, Hindu Mahasabha, Communist Party, and many princely states opposed
 Some Groups                       or stayed aloof from the movement, weakening its national unity.
 Suppression by British            The movement was brutally crushed with mass arrests, public shootings, censorship, and
 Force                             intelligence operations.
 Increased Communal                Muslim participation was minimal; the period was used by the League to consolidate
 Polarisation                      support, deepening the communal divide.
 Regional and Class Gaps           While students and the urban middle class participated enthusiastically, many other parts of
                                   India, including Punjab and the southern presidencies, were relatively quiet and saw far less
                                   militant disruption.
 Did Not Achieve Immediate         The British did not quit India immediately; independence came five years later through
 Goal                              constitutional negotiations and global pressure post-WWII.
Though the Quit India Movement did not achieve immediate objectives, it convinced the British that India had become ungovernable
without Indian cooperation, accelerating decolonisation. It remains, as the former Vice President of India observed, "an epitome of
what people are capable of achieving if they work together with determination and dedication".
