Sarkars in Satara (Maharashtra) were formed. Underground Due to mass arrests of senior leaders, Leaders like Jayaprakash Narayan, Revolutionary underground networks ensured continuity Rammanohar Lohia, Usha Mehta kept resistance Networks through secret communications and planning. alive through covert operations. British Repression Over 100,000 arrests, widespread violence, and Churchill’s war cabinet faced moral criticism; and Global Pressure economic disruption weakened British the U.S. and China pressed for Indian legitimacy and drew global criticism. independence. End of Illusions Made clear to Indians that the British would not Gandhi’s moral authority strengthened despite about British Intent voluntarily give up power unless forced by suppression of the movement. unified, mass resistance. Catalyst for Post- Though suppressed, the movement paved the The British began considering transfer of power War Developments way for later events like the INA trials, naval seriously after WWII, evident in the Cabinet mutiny, and the final phase of negotiations Mission (1946)

chapters.t: Indian National Army (INA) and Subhas Chandra Bose
chapters.text:
21                 Indian National Army (INA)
                        and Subhash Chandra Bose
The Indian National Army (INA) was first formed in September 1942 by Captain Mohan Singh from Indian prisoners of war in
Southeast Asia under the guidance of Rash Behari Bose and the Indian Independence League. After its initial collapse due to
disagreements with the Japanese, Subhash Chandra Bose reorganised and revitalised the INA in 1943, transforming it into a disciplined
liberation force of ~45,000 volunteers.


1. Role and Contributions of Subhash Chandra Bose in the Indian National Movement
         Aspect                                                            Description
 Early Political              He partnered with C.R. Das in key political initiatives, served as Chief Executive Officer of
 Collaboration                Calcutta under him, and faced imprisonment for his activism.
 Youth and Labour             Energised the youth, advanced the trade union movement, became Mayor of Calcutta, and
 Leadership                   served as President of the All India Trade Union Congress (from 1929 to 1931), strengthening
                              grassroots mobilisation.
 Role in Congress             Advocated for complete independence, rejected dominion status, participated in the Salt
                              Satyagraha, and criticised the suspension of the Civil Disobedience Movement and the Gandhi-
                              Irwin Pact (Bhagat Singh not saved).
 Progressive Reforms          Worked with Jawaharlal Nehru and other left-leaning Congress leaders in promoting socialist ideas and
                              economic planning within the Congress. He also pushed Congress to adopt progressive resolutions.
                              Example: The National Planning Committee (NPC) was established in October 1938 by the Indian
                              National Congress (INC) under the presidency of Subhash Chandra Bose. Chaired by Jawaharlal
                              Nehru, it was India's first major initiative to formulate a systematic, centralised economic plan aimed at
                              rapid industrialisation and poverty alleviation.
 Congress Leadership          Elected Congress President at Haripura (1938) and Tripuri (1939); resigned due to ideological
 & Forward Bloc               differences with Gandhi and founded the Forward Bloc to unite leftist and progressive forces,
                              especially in Bengal.
 Resistance and               Frequently arrested and detained by the British for his involvement in civil disobedience and
 Detention                    anti-colonial activities, particularly during World War II.
 Revival of the INA           Escaped British custody, reorganised the Indian National Army (INA) in 1943, transforming it into
                              a disciplined force of around 45,000, comprising prisoners of war (PoWs) and expatriates from
                              Southeast Asia.
 Provisional                  Established the Azad Hind Government in Singapore in October 1943, recognised by Axis
 Government of Free           powers (Japan, Germany, and Italy), presenting an alternative Indian authority and boosting
 India                        international support for the independence cause.
 Military Campaigns           Led the INA alongside Japanese forces into Northeast India, hoisted the tricolour at Moirang,
                              and participated in the Imphal and Kohima campaigns, symbolising armed resistance despite
                              military setbacks.


Inspirational            Motivated thousands with slogans like “Give me blood, and I will give you freedom!” and
Leadership               “Delhi Chalo” instilling a spirit of unity, sacrifice, and assertive nationalism among Indians.
Women’s                  Founded the Rani Jhansi Regiment, led by Captain Lakshmi Swaminathan, pioneering
Empowerment              women’s active participation in the freedom movement and setting a precedent for gender
                         inclusivity.
Legacy and               Bose’s leadership made the INA a potent symbol of Indian unity and resistance, galvanising
Symbolism                public sentiment, weakening colonial morale, and accelerating the momentum toward
                         independence.

2. Genesis and Growth of INA


3. Role of the Indian National Army (INA) in India’s Freedom Struggle
     Aspect                                                       Description
Military               The INA, allied with Japan, fought in the Burma-India theatre during World War II, advanced up to
Campaigns              the Imphal and Kohima campaigns, and hoisted the tricolour at Moirang, Manipur in 1944,
                       though ultimately defeated by Allied forces.
Provisional            Bose established the Provisional Government of Free India (Azad Hind) in Singapore in 1943,
Government             declared war on Britain and the US, and gained recognition from the Axis powers.
Nationalist            INA’s struggle inspired mass patriotic feelings, demonstrated communal unity among Hindus,
Sentiment              Muslims, and Sikhs, and empowered women through the Rani Jhansi Brigade.

4. Role of Women in the Indian National Army (INA)
       Aspect                                                      Description
Formation of             The INA established the Rani Jhansi Brigade, led by Captain Lakshmi Swaminathan (later
Women’s Regiment         Lakshmi Sehgal), with around 1,500 women volunteers from diverse backgrounds.
Transition to Combat     Initially assigned support and nursing duties, many women of the Rani Jhansi Brigade later
Roles                    participated as combatants, notably during the Imphal campaign in 1945.


 Breaking Social              Women’s active involvement marked a shift from passive participation to direct action,
 Barriers                     challenging traditional gender roles in the freedom struggle.
 Legacy and                   The courage and leadership of INA women set a precedent, inspiring greater participation of
 Inspiration                  women in subsequent national movements and in independent India.

5. INA Trials
      Aspect                                                           Description
 Background            Following the defeat of the Axis powers in 1945, the British decided to court-martial selected INA
                       officers for collaborating with Japan and waging war against the British Crown.
 Early INA Trials      Before the famous Red Fort trials, several former British Indian Army personnel who had joined or
 (1943–44)             assisted the INA were tried under the Indian Army Act and Burma Army Act for civil offences and for
                       "waging war against the King" under the Indian Penal Code. These proceedings attracted little public
                       attention.
 Selective             After the Burma Campaign, thousands of INA personnel were captured. The British adopted a policy of
 Prosecution           prosecuting only selected officers, primarily those accused of treason, murder, torture, and abetment to
 Policy                murder.
 Red Fort Trials       The most prominent INA trials were held at the Red Fort, Delhi, from November 1945 to May 1946.
 (1945–46)             The first major trial involved Shah Nawaz Khan, Prem Kumar Sahgal, and Gurbaksh Singh
                       Dhillon, whose prosecution symbolised the unity of Hindus, Muslims, and Sikhs and transformed the
                       proceedings into a nationwide nationalist movement.
 INA Defence           The Indian National Congress established the INA Defence Committee (1945) to provide legal
 Committee             defence for the accused officers, mobilise public opinion, and organise relief and assistance for INA
                       personnel and their families.
 Prominent             The defence team included eminent lawyers and national leaders such as Bhulabhai Desai, Asaf Ali,
 Defence               Sharat Chandra Bose, Tej Bahadur Sapru, Kailash Nath Katju, and Jawaharlal Nehru, who
 Lawyers               appeared in his barrister's robes after many years.
 National              The trials ignited unprecedented public protests, student demonstrations, labour strikes, and support from
 Impact                soldiers, transforming the INA into a powerful symbol of nationalism and weakening British moral
                       authority.
 Historical            The INA Trials significantly influenced the final phase of India's freedom struggle by strengthening
 Significance          nationalist unity, contributing to unrest within the armed forces (including the Royal Indian Navy
                       Revolt, 1946), and convincing the British that continued rule over India was becoming increasingly
                       untenable.
S.C. Bose infused India's freedom struggle with the spirit of courage, sacrifice, and uncompromising nationalism through his leadership
of the Indian National Army (INA). Though the INA did not achieve military success, Bose inspired millions and strengthened the
momentum for independence. His iconic call, "Give me blood, and I shall give you freedom!", remains a timeless symbol of
patriotism and selfless service.

22                  Royal Indian Navy (RIN)
                         Mutiny (1946)
The Royal Indian Navy (RIN) Mutiny of 1946, often termed the “Last War of Indian Independence,” was a major revolt by Indian
sailors against British colonial rule, sparked by grievances over harsh working conditions, racial discrimination, and inadequate
food. Spreading rapidly from Bombay to other naval bases, the uprising saw over 20,000 sailors participate, highlighting the rising
tide of nationalist sentiment in the closing days of British rule.
                                                 UPSC Mains PYQs
      1.      In what ways did the naval mutiny prove to be the last nail in the coffin of British
              colonial aspirations in India? (2014)

1. Background to the Royal Indian Navy (RIN) Mutiny
     Aspect                                                          Description
 Transformation      World War II reshaped global politics, weakened colonial empires, and inspired nationalist
 Triggered by        movements in India. The Royal Indian Navy (RIN) expanded rapidly, becoming ten times larger by
 WWII                1945 than it was in 1939.
 Exposure and        Indian soldiers and sailors, deployed worldwide, gained new perspectives, read about Allied promises of
 Awakening           freedom, and began questioning when India would gain independence.
 Changing            Wartime needs forced the British to recruit beyond the traditional ‘martial races’ including broader sections
 Recruitment         of Indian society. Economic hardship and famine also drove more Indians to join the armed forces.
 Shift in            Experiences of discrimination, poor treatment, and inferior pay compared to British counterparts led to
 Attitudes           growing resentment and a transformation in the mindset of Indian servicemen.
 Prelude to          This discontent resulted in at least nine minor mutinies between March 1942 and April 1945, setting the
 Mutiny              stage for the larger RIN mutiny of 1946.
2. Causes of the Royal Indian Navy (RIN) Mutiny
        Cause                                                          Description
 Discrimination            Indian sailors endured racial bias, poor-quality food, and were not allowed to voice grievances
                           against European officers.
 Poor Treatment            They were relegated to menial duties and had inadequate access to medical facilities compared to
                           their British counterparts.
 Racist                    Commander F.M. King of HMIS Talwar allegedly used racist and abusive language towards Indian
 Leadership                ratings, triggering widespread protests.
 Inspiration from          The Quit India Movement and the Indian National Army’s struggle inspired the sailors to take
 Movements                 collective action.
 INA Trials and            The trials of INA officers, seen as symbols of resistance, ignited strong nationalist sentiments
 Nationalist Fervour       among the sailors.


3. Impact of the Royal Indian Navy (RIN) Mutiny
         Aspect                                                            Description
 Catalyst for                  The mutiny exposed deep dissatisfaction within the armed forces and demonstrated the
 Independence                  weakening grip of British rule.
 Mass Mobilization             The RIN mutiny began in Bombay and quickly spread to 78 ships and 21 shore establishments
                               across India, involving nearly 20,000 sailors.
 Erosion of British            Displayed crumbling loyalty among Indian armed forces; around 20,000 sailors participated,
 Authority                     signaling a shift in allegiance.
 Political Repercussions       The mutiny reinforced British concerns about the reliability of the Indian armed forces and
                               strengthened the urgency of transferring power, reflected in the Cabinet Mission (1946).
 Psychological Impact          Weakened British morale and boosted Indian confidence and unity in the independence
                               movement.
 International Attention       The mutiny drew global media coverage, highlighting the unsustainability of British rule in India.
 Last Nail in the Coffin       The revolt is widely regarded as a decisive event that hastened the end of British colonial
                               aspirations in India.

4. RIN Mutiny as the last nail in the coffin of British colonial aspirations in India
       Aspect                                                           Description
 Mass Support              The mutiny received widespread backing from the Indian public, and various branches of the armed
                           forces, including the Royal Indian Air Force. It triggered nationwide protests, with masses joining
                           the Bombay strike, creating widespread unrest.
 Exposure of               The uprising revealed the British government’s inability to depend on the loyalty of Indian armed
 Colonial Weakness         forces, highlighting the fragility of colonial rule in India.
 International             The mutiny attracted international media attention, including from The Times, and reinforced the
 Attention                 growing global criticism of colonialism after World War II.
 Hastened Transfer         The scale and intensity of the mutiny forced the British to expedite the process of transferring
 of Power                  power, as it became clear that continued control over India was unsustainable.
 Decline in British        The widespread support for the mutiny and defections within the armed forces severely
 Morale                    undermined British confidence, while simultaneously boosting Indian morale and self-assurance in
                           the struggle for independence.
 Demonstration of          The revolt showcased unprecedented unity among Indians, with Hindu and Muslim sailors rebelling
 Indian Unity              together, weakening colonial divide-and-rule tactics and strengthening the collective fight against
                           British rule.
5. Limitations of the Royal Indian Navy (RIN) Mutiny
          Aspect                                                            Description
 Lack of Political Support       Major political parties, including the Indian National Congress and the Muslim League united
                                 in their opposition to the mutiny rather than supporting it. They jointly pressured the striking
                                 sailors to surrender, fearing that an armed military uprising would derail ongoing constitutional
                                 negotiations for independence
 Absence of Leadership           The sailors acted without centralised or recognised leadership, making coordinated action difficult.
 Gandhi’s Condemnation           Mahatma Gandhi criticised the mutiny for lacking direction from an organised revolutionary
                                 party and established leaders.
 Muslim League’s                 The Muslim League opposed the uprising, arguing that street protests were not the right way
 Disapproval                     to address grievances.
 Focus on Negotiations           National leaders prioritised constitutional dialogue with the British over mass agitation or revolt.
 Communist Party’s               Only the Communist Party of India supported the sailors, mobilizing workers in their favour
 Stance                          and advocating for revolutionary change.
The Royal Indian Navy Mutiny of 1946 exposed growing discontent within India's armed forces, undermined British
confidence, and hastened the transfer of power. As historian Sumit Sarkar observed, it marked the culmination of the
wartime radicalisation of Indian servicemen.
