chapters.t: Constituent Assembly and Indian Independence Act (1947)
chapters.text:
23                 Constituent Assembly and
                        Indian Independence Act (1947)
The Constituent Assembly, established in December 1946 under the Cabinet Mission Plan, was tasked with framing a Constitution
that would embody India’s aspirations for self-governance and social justice. The Indian Independence Act of 1947, passed by the
British Parliament, made the Assembly a sovereign legislative body and marked the formal transfer of power, paving the way for
India’s emergence as a sovereign, democratic republic.

Evolution of the Constituent Assembly Idea in India


1. Composition of the Constituent Assembly
       Aspect                                                       Description
 Method of                Members were partly elected and partly nominated. Elected members were chosen indirectly by
 Selection                provincial legislative assemblies, which themselves were elected on a limited franchise.
 Total                    The original strength was 389 members: 296 from British Indian provinces and 93 from princely
 Membership               states.
 Changes in               After members of the Muslim League from areas that became Pakistan withdrew, the British Indian
 Membership               seats decreased from 296 to 229, and princely state seats reduced from 93 to 70.
 Representation           292 members represented the 11 governor’s provinces, and 4 members came from the four Chief
 from Provinces           Commissioners’ provinces (one from each).
 Community-Based          Seats in each province were distributed among Muslims, Sikhs, and the General category in
 Allocation               proportion to their population.
 Election Process         Representatives of each community were elected by their respective community members in the
                          provincial legislative assemblies, using proportional representation through the Single
                          Transferable Vote system.
 Princely State           Members from princely states were nominated by the rulers of those states.
 Representation


2. Criticism of the Constituent Assembly
        Criticism                                                      Description
 Not a Representative        Members were not directly elected by universal adult franchise, raising questions about the
 Body                        Assembly’s representativeness.
 Not a Sovereign             The Assembly was established under British proposals and required the British Government’s
 Body                        permission to convene its sessions.
 Time-Consuming              The Constitution took almost three years to draft, whereas the American Constitution was
 Process                     completed in just four months.
 Congress                    Granville Austin noted the Assembly functioned as a one-party body, with the Congress party
 Dominance                   holding overwhelming influence.
 Lawyer-Politician           The Assembly was largely composed of lawyers and politicians, limiting representation from
 Domination                  other social and professional groups.
 Hindu Majority              Winston Churchill criticised the Assembly for mainly representing the Hindu community, rather
                             than reflecting the diversity of Indian society.
3. Mountbatten Plan
The Mountbatten Plan, announced on June 3, 1947, was a landmark proposal by Viceroy Lord Louis Mountbatten that paved the
way for the partition of British India into the independent dominions of India and Pakistan. Designed to address escalating
communal tensions and the urgent demand for self-rule, the plan enabled a swift and orderly transfer of power, culminating in
independence on August 15, 1947.
a. Key Provisions of the Mountbatten Plan
      Provision                                                       Description
 Partition of India       Proposed the division of British India into two independent dominions, India and Pakistan. The
                          proposal for an independent united Bengal was not accepted, and Lord Mountbatten and the
                          Indian leadership firmly rejected Hyderabad's demand for complete independence or dominion
                          status within the British Commonwealth.
 Autonomy and             Both dominions were to be granted full autonomy and sovereignty, empowering them to draft their
 Sovereignty              own constitutions.
 Princely                 Princely states were free to accede to either India or Pakistan after the lapse of British paramountcy,
 States                   with geographical contiguity serving as the principal practical consideration.
 Separate Constituent     Separate constituent assemblies were to be set up for India and Pakistan to frame their respective
 Assemblies               constitutions.
 Boundary                 It specifically outlined that a Boundary Commission would be established to demarcate the exact
 Commissions              international borders of the newly divided Punjab and Bengal regions.
 Punjab and Bengal        Legislative assemblies of Punjab and Bengal would vote in separate communal groups (Hindus
 Assemblies               and Muslims) to decide on partition, with simple majority as the deciding factor.
 Sindh Decision           The Sindh Legislative Assembly would determine its own future through a legislative vote.
 Referendums              Referendums were to be held in the North-West Frontier Province (NWFP) and the Sylhet district
                          to decide their future affiliation.
 Date of Independence     The date for granting independence to both dominions was set as August 15, 1947.


4. Indian Independence Act 1947
The Indian Independence Act 1947, enacted by the British Parliament, formally divided British India into the independent
dominions of India and Pakistan on August 15, 1947. Based on the Mountbatten Plan, this Act marked the culmination of decades
of Indian struggle and paved the way for the end of British colonial rule.

a. Key Features of Indian Independence Act 1947

         Feature                                                        Description
 Partition of British         The Act divided British India into two independent dominions, India and Pakistan, each with
 India                        the right to remain in or leave the British Commonwealth.
 End of British               The Act ended British legal authority in India, transferring all governing powers to the new
 Sovereignty                  dominions and removing the Emperor of India from the British monarch’s titles.
 Abolition of the             The office of Viceroy was abolished and replaced by a Governor-General in each dominion,
 Viceroy’s Office             appointed by the British King on the advice of the respective dominion’s cabinet. The
                              British government no longer held responsibility for the governance of India or Pakistan.
 Empowerment of               The Constituent Assemblies of both India and Pakistan were empowered to draft their own
 Constituent Assemblies       constitutions and repeal or adopt existing British laws as they saw fit. No new British laws
                              would apply unless accepted by the dominion legislatures.
 Boundary Demarcation         The Act provided for Boundary Commissions under Sir Cyril Radcliffe to determine the final
                              boundaries between India and Pakistan.
 Abolition of Secretary       The office of Secretary of State for India was abolished, with its duties transferred to the
 of State for India           Secretary of State for Commonwealth Affairs.
 Status of Princely           British authority over princely states and tribal areas ended on August 15, 1947. Princely states
 States                       were given the option to join India, Pakistan, or remain independent.
 Governance Structure         The Act provided a framework for the new governments, allowing both dominions to use the
                              Government of India Act, 1935 as an interim constitution, with the power to amend it as
                              necessary.
 Legislative Authority        The British monarch’s right to veto or reserve bills was abolished; this power was now held
                              by the respective Governor-General, who could assent to bills in His Majesty’s name.
 Governor-General and         The Governor-General and provincial governors were to act as nominal heads, bound by the
 Provincial Governors         advice of their respective councils of ministers.
 Civil Services               The Act ended the British Secretary of State’s authority to appoint or reserve posts in the Indian
                              Civil Services. Existing civil servants retained their rights and benefits if appointed before
                              August 15, 1947.

b. Significance of the Indian Independence Act 1947

        Aspect                                                         Description
 End of Colonial Rule      Marked the conclusion of nearly two centuries of British rule, resulting in the emergence of India
                           and Pakistan as independent, sovereign nations.
 Resolution of             Partition addressed the Muslim League’s demand for a separate nation, but also triggered
 Communal Issue            large-scale migrations and widespread communal violence.
 Influence on              India’s independence set a precedent, inspiring other colonies in Asia and Africa to pursue their
 Decolonisation            freedom and accelerating the global decolonisation movement.
 Foundation for            Empowered India and Pakistan to create their own Constituent Assemblies, with India adopting its
 Constitutional            Constitution in 1950 and establishing itself as the world’s largest democracy.
 Development
 Shift in Global Power     Britain’s exit marked the decline of its imperial dominance, while India and Pakistan emerged as
 Dynamics                  important players on the world stage, reshaping international relations after World War II.


c. Impacts of the Indian Independence Act 1947

           Aspect                                                            Description
 Partition and Communal            The Act resulted in the division of British India, sparking widespread communal riots and
 Violence                          violence that led to the displacement and deaths of millions.
 Mass Migration                    The partition caused one of the largest migrations in history, with Hindus and Sikhs moving
                                   to India and Muslims to Pakistan, creating a major humanitarian crisis.
 Integration of Princely           The process of integrating princely states into India or Pakistan was challenging, with
 States                            unresolved issues like Kashmir leading to prolonged conflict and tensions.
 Constitutional                    Both India and Pakistan embarked on framing their own constitutions, with India adopting a
 Development                       comprehensive constitution in 1950, laying the foundation for democracy.
 Unresolved Issues                 The Act left several disputes unresolved, including the Kashmir issue and boundary conflicts
                                   with neighboring countries, which continue to impact India-Pakistan relations.
The Constituent Assembly and the Indian Independence Act of 1947 together marked the end of British colonial rule and the
birth of two sovereign nations, India and Pakistan. While the Act fulfilled the long-cherished dream of self-rule, it also left a complex
legacy of partition, mass migration, and unresolved challenges, but ultimately paved the way for India’s democratic constitutional
development and emergence as a modern nation-state.

chapters.t: Role of Various Sections in the Indian National Movement
chapters.text:
24                 Role of Various Sections in the
                        Indian National Movement
24.1 Role of Women in the Freedom Movement
The role of women in India’s freedom movement was transformative, as they actively participated in protests, civil disobedience,
and underground resistance, breaking traditional gender roles and emerging as leaders alongside men. Inspired by Gandhian ideals
of non-violence and self-reliance, women like Sarojini Naidu, Aruna Asaf Ali, and Kasturba Gandhi played crucial roles in
mobilizing masses and sustaining the nationalist struggle.
                                                UPSC Mains PYQs
      1.     Discuss the role of women in the freedom struggle especially during the Gandhian
             phase. (2016)
      2.     The women's questions arose in modern India as a part of the 19th century social
             reform movement. What are the major issues and debates concerning women in that
             period? (2017)
      3.     Defying the barriers of age, gender, and religion, the Indian women became the torch-
             bearer during the struggle for freedom in India. Discuss. (2013)


1. Historical Evolution of Women’s Participation in the Freedom Movement

       Phase                                                 Details with Examples
 Early Resistance       y Women’s resistance began with regional uprisings, challenging colonialism even before organised
 (1817-1857)              nationalism. Their participation was marked by martial courage and local leadership.
                        y Example: Bhima Bai Holkar played a prominent role during the Third Anglo-Maratha War (1817–18).
 First War of           y Women directly participated in leadership and combat, symbolizing defiance against British
 Independence             oppression. They inspired future generations with their uncompromising bravery.
 (1857)                 y Example: Begum Hazrat Mahal fought to defend Awadh, rejecting British terms even in exile.
 Pre-Gandhian Era       y Women began participating in Swadeshi, educational reform, and international advocacy.
 (1905-1919)              Many acted as early nationalists and social reformers.
                        y Example: Madam Bhikaji Cama hoisted the Indian flag in Stuttgart (1907) and co-founded Paris
                          Indian Society.
 Gandhian Era           y Gandhi's emphasis on non-violence and swaraj enabled unprecedented female mass
 (1920-1947)              mobilisation. Women played roles in protests, underground work, foreign cloth picketing,
                          liquor shop picketing and leadership, breaking societal taboos.
                        y Example: Aruna Asaf Ali hoisted the tricolour at Gowalia Tank during the Quit India
                          Movement (1942).
 Underground and        y Several women defied colonial bans and went underground or joined revolutionary parties,
 Revolutionary            participating in militant nationalism.
 Roles                  y Example: Usha Mehta ran the underground “Congress Radio” during Quit India Movement.
 Women in Social-       y Women contributed through formation of Mahila Samitis, promotion of Khadi, social reform (child
 Reform and               marriage restraint, women’s education), and founding of women’s journals and cooperatives.
 Mobilization           y Example: Rameshwari Nehru published Stree Darpan, linking women's issues with national
                          politics.

2. Role of Women in the Indian Freedom Movement

           Role                           Description                                          Example
 Leadership in           Led protests, addressed public gatherings,         Sarojini Naidu led the Salt Satyagraha (1930)
 Mass Movements          mobilized people, and represented women’s          and was the first Indian woman President of the
                         voices in Congress and national platforms.         INC (1925).


Participation in        Actively engaged in picketing, breaking salt           Kamaladevi Chattopadhyay joined Salt
Civil Disobedience      laws, boycott of foreign goods, and mass               Satyagraha; Ramadevi mobilized women in
                        satyagrahas.                                           Odisha.
Revolutionary           Took up armed resistance, participated in              Kalpana Dutt in Chittagong Armoury Raid;
Activities              conspiracies, and attacked colonial symbols.           Pritilata Waddedar attacked British clubs.
Underground             Engaged in secret communications,                      Usha Mehta operated the “Congress Radio”
Resistance &            publication of underground newspapers, and             during the Quit India Movement (1942).
Propaganda              running covert radio operations.
International           Voiced India’s cause abroad, built solidarity          Madam Bhikaji Cama hoisted the Indian flag in
Advocacy                with anti-colonial movements internationally.          Germany (1907) and published Bande Mataram
                                                                               in Paris.
Symbolic & Moral        Became enduring symbols of courage,                    Rani Lakshmibai, Begum Hazrat Mahal, and
Inspiration             nationalism, and defiance against British              Kanaklata Barua inspired regional uprisings.
                        authority, inspiring mass mobilisation.
Social Reform and       Advocated for education, legal reforms, and            Annie Besant founded Home Rule League;
Women’s Rights          upliftment of women as part of nationalist             Kamaladevi promoted women’s cooperatives and
                        awakening.                                             legal reform.
Grassroots              Mobilized rural and urban women, formed                Women in Meerut, Madras, and Assam carried
Mobilisation            Mahila Samitis, promoted khadi, and sustained          forward Quit India Movement during mass
                        movements during leader arrests.                       detentions.
Post-Movement           Took part in post-independence governance              Vijaya Lakshmi Pandit became a cabinet minister
Constitutional          and constitution-making, laying foundations for        and UNGA President; Sucheta Kripalani became
Role                    gender justice in new India.                           CM of U.P.

3. Challenges Faced by Women
 Challenge                        Description                                              Examples
Patriarchal          Women faced societal restrictions            Practices like purdah, sati, and child marriages confined
Norms                limiting public roles, rooted in male        women, yet leaders like Sarojini Naidu broke barriers during
                     dominance.                                   the 1905 Bengal Partition protests.
Repressive           British laws targeted women activists,       Durgabai Deshmukh was jailed for three years for leading Salt
Laws                 leading to arrests and imprisonment.         Satyagraha (1930) at Marina Beach under the Salt Law.
Physical             Women endured torture and violence           Matangini Hazra, ‘Gandhi Buri,’ was shot dead leading a 1942
Violence             during protests, risking personal safety.    Quit India procession in Bengal.
Limited              Women’s contributions were often             Regional leaders like A.V. Kuttimaluamma in Kerala gained
Recognition          overshadowed by male leaders in              less prominence despite active roles in Non-Cooperation.
                     historical narratives.
Balancing            Women juggled domestic duties with           Vijayalakshmi Pandit led Salt Satyagraha processions (1930)
Roles                activism, facing dual pressures.             with her daughters, balancing family and nationalism.

4. Gandhian Influence on Women’s Participation
   Aspect                               Description                                             Examples
Non-Violence         Gandhi’s non-violent methods encouraged                 Sarojini Naidu led the Dharasana Salt Works
Advocacy             women’s participation, emphasizing tolerance            Satyagraha after Gandhi's arrest in 1930.
                     and courage.
Empowerment          Gandhi’s campaigns against caste, child marriage,       Amrit Kaur and Muthulakshmi Reddy became leaders
Initiatives          and for education inspired women to join.               in Non-Cooperation, inspired by Gandhi’s reforms.
Mass                 Gandhi transformed the INC into a mass                  Women’s participation peaked in the 1920
Mobilization         movement, enabling women’s widespread                   Non-Cooperation Movement, with leaders like
                     involvement.                                            Kasturba Gandhi joining protests.


 Symbolic Roles       Gandhi initially encouraged symbolic roles but        While Gandhi tasked Anasuya Sarabhai with
                      later supported leadership positions.                 distributing charitable relief to striking families to
                                                                            sustain their endurance during the 1918
                                                                            Ahmedabad Mill Strike, he also appointed her as
                                                                            the founding president of the Textile Labour
                                                                            Association in 1920.
Women were not mere supporters but crusaders who reshaped India’s political and social landscape through their courage and
sacrifice. As Sarojini Naidu profoundly said, “A country's greatness lies in its undying ideals of love and sacrifice that inspire
the mothers of the race” highlighting the pivotal role women played in inspiring and sustaining the freedom movement.

24.2 Role of Workers and Labour Movements
The workers and labour movements in India emerged as a response to harsh industrial exploitation and colonial oppression, uniting
labourers across sectors to demand fair wages, better working conditions, and social justice. Over time, these movements became
deeply intertwined with the broader national struggle for independence, playing a crucial role in mobilizing mass resistance
against British rule and shaping the trajectory of modern Indian history.

1. Causes of Labour Movements in India

       Cause                                                        Explanation
 Poor Working           y Industrial workers faced long hours, hazardous environments, lack of safety measures, and no
 Conditions               medical or welfare provisions.
                        y Example: The Bombay Mill-Hands Association (1890), founded by Narayan Meghaji Lokhande,
                          was India's first organised labour association that demanded better working hours and conditions for
                          mill workers.
 Low and                y Wages were insufficient, irregular, and did not keep pace with rising prices, especially during and
 Arbitrary Wages          after World War I.
                        y Example: Textile workers in Bombay and Ahmedabad frequently struck work in the 1920s and
                          1930s, demanding fair wages.
 Discrimination         y Indian workers received lower pay and inferior treatment compared to their European
                          counterparts, fostering resentment and unity.
                        y Example: Indian railway workers were paid less than Europeans for the same work, leading to
                          strikes in the Great Indian Peninsular Railways (1899).
 Lack of Basic          y Workers lived in slums with inadequate access to food, water, sanitation, and healthcare,
 Necessities              compelling them to seek collective redress.
                        y Example: Mill workers in Bombay and Calcutta lived in overcrowded chawls and bustees, leading
                          to frequent health crises.
 Economic               y Both British colonial policies and Indian capitalists prioritised profits over worker welfare,
 Exploitation             intensifying grievances.
                        y Example: The Indian indenture system sent over a million Indians to work in harsh conditions in
                          colonies like Mauritius and the Caribbean.
 Political              y Nationalist leaders and the freedom movement inspired workers to link their struggle with the
 Awakening                broader fight for independence.
                        y Example: The All India Trade Union Congress (AITUC) was founded in 1920 with support from
                          national leaders like Lala Lajpat Rai.
 Influence of            The Russian Revolution (1917) and the formation of the International Labour Organization (ILO)
 International           inspired Indian workers to organize and demand rights.
 Events
 Growth of Trade        y The formation of unions provided organisational strength and a platform for collective action.
 Unions                 y Example: The Madras Labour Union (1918) was among the first to organize workers in a structured
                          manner to press for their rights.


Educated             y Educated youth and leaders played a key role in mobilising workers, spreading awareness, and
Leadership             articulating demands.
                     y Example: Sasipada Banerjea started the Working Men's Club and the journal Bharat Shramjeevi
                       (India's first working-class journal).
Legislative          y Early labour laws were either absent or anti-worker, and attempts to suppress strikes often
Neglect                backfired, strengthening the movement.
                     y Example: The Trade Disputes Act (1929) was enacted to curb strikes, but instead led to greater
                       mobilization among workers.

2. Different Phases of the Working Class Movement in India
     Phase                                                         Description
Early/              y Industrialisation began with the railways, jute, cotton, and coal industries. Initial efforts were sporadic
Autonomous            and philanthropic, led by reformers such as Sasipada Banerjea (Working Men's Club, 1870),
Phase:                Sorabjee Shapoorji Bengalee (Labour bill, 1878), and Narayan Meghajee Lokhande
1850s-1918            (Deenbandhu newspaper, Bombay Mill and Millhands Association, 1890).
                    y Strikes like the Great Indian Peninsular Railways strike (1899) occurred, but organization was weak.
                    y Early nationalists focused more on industrial growth than labour rights.
                    y The Swadeshi movement (post-1905) saw workers participate in political protests, but trade unions
                      remained limited.
Emergence of        y Economic distress after World War I (inflation, stagnant wages) and global influences (ILO
Trade Unionism:       formation, 1919) politicized labour.
1918-1926           y Surge in strikes (1918-1922); formation of Madras Labour Union (1918) and All India Trade
                      Union Congress (AITUC, 1920).
                    y Ahmedabad Textile Labour Association (Majoor Mahajan Sabha) established under Gandhian
                      influence.
                    y Other organizations like Central Labour Board (Bombay), Bengal Trade Union Federation, and
                      All India Railwaymen's Federation formed.
                    y First May Day celebrated in India (1923, Madras).
                    y Labour activism influenced by Khilafat and Non-Cooperation movements.
                    y Trade Union Act (1926) legalized and regulated trade unions.
Ideological         y Congress’s inconsistent support led to the rise of leftist and communist influence in unions.
Diversification &   y Major strikes: Girni Kamgar Union’s six-month Bombay textile strike (1928).
Struggles:          y Government repression: Public Safety Ordinance and Trade Disputes Act (1929); Meerut
1927-1937             Conspiracy Case (1929-1933) with mass arrests of labour leaders.
                    y Split in AITUC: Formation of All India Trade Union Federation (1931) and Red Trade Union
                      Congress (1931, remerged 1934).
                    y Workers participated in the Civil Disobedience Movement (1930); the movement weakened after
                      1931 due to internal divisions.
Consolidation       y Congress ministries (1937) and increased civil liberties encouraged union growth; the National
and Mass              Federation of Trade Unions merged with AITUC (agreement signed in 1938 and formally
Mobilization:         merged in 1940).
1937-1947           y Bombay Industrial Disputes Act (1939) aimed to curb labour militancy.
                    y World War II: Anti-war strikes; split in AITUC (formation of Indian Federation of Labour, 1941,
                      under M.N. Roy).
                    y Unions played a role in the Quit India Movement (1942); the post-war period saw renewed activism
                      and eventual unification of major trade union federations.
                    y By independence, the working class was an organised and politically significant force.

3. Features of Workers’ Movements in India
    Feature                                                         Description
Collective           Workers united to oppose oppressive working conditions, low wages, long hours of work, and lack
Struggle Against     of safety and demanded fair wages, better working conditions, reduction of working hours, and the
Exploitation         right to organise.
                     Example: The 1928 Bombay Textile Strike led by the Girni Kamgar Union.


Industrial and         Movements primarily emerged in urban industrial centers (railways, textiles, jute, coal) where modern
Urban Focus            industries and large workforces developed.
Influence of           The movement was shaped by dual exploitation (by colonial rulers and Indian capitalists) making it
Colonialism            distinct from Western labour movements. It became intertwined with the anti-colonial struggle.
Formation of           The creation of unions like the Bombay Mill-Hands Association (1890), Madras Labour Union
Trade Unions           (1918), and AITUC (1920) provided organizational strength and a platform for collective bargaining.
Leadership and         y Leadership came from within the working class as well as from nationalist and socialist leaders,
Organization             helping to articulate demands and mobilise workers.
                       y Example: Leaders like S.A. Dange, B.P. Wadia, and N.M. Joshi championed workers' rights.
Integration            Workers’ movements often aligned with the broader struggle for independence, especially during
with National          the Swadeshi, Non-Cooperation, and Civil Disobedience Movements.
Movement
Adoption of            Methods included strikes, protests, petitions, and negotiations. Over time, the movement evolved
Diverse Strategies     from sporadic protests to organised, large-scale industrial actions.
Ideological            The movement saw the influence of nationalist, socialist, and communist ideologies, especially from
Diversity              the 1920s onward, leading to both moderate and militant phases.
Challenges and         Faced obstacles such as caste and religious divisions, lack of organisation in early phases, state
Limitations            repression, and underrepresentation of rural/agricultural workers.

4. Features of Socialism-Based Workers’ Movements in India

      Aspect                                                        Description
Progressive and          y Advocated for equality, social justice, and the dismantling of economic hierarchies, aiming to
Egalitarian Ideology       uplift the working class and marginalised sections.
                         y Example: The Karachi Session of the Indian National Congress (1931) adopted a resolution on
                           fundamental rights and an economic programme with a clear socialist orientation, drafted by
                           Jawaharlal Nehru.
Mass Mobilization        y Mobilised workers, peasants, and the middle class, making the movement broad-based and
and Multi-Class            inclusive.
Participation            y Example: While founded to advocate for formal industrial workers (in textiles and railways),
                           AITUC leadership drew heavily from middle-class intellectuals, lawyers, and prominent freedom
                           fighters (e.g., Lala Lajpat Rai).
Radical and              Sought comprehensive socio-economic transformation, including redistribution of wealth, land
Transformative           reforms, and workers’ control over production.
Goals
Democratic and           y Emphasised democratic rights, secularism, and unity across religious and ethnic lines.
Secular Outlook          y Example: The Congress Socialist Party (CSP) advocated for a democratic socialism rooted in
                           indigenous realities rather than strict Comintern dictates. It demanded the redistribution of land
                           and systemic workers' rights to push the Indian National Congress toward a socialist programme.
Pragmatic                y Valued nonviolence but were open to militant tactics when necessary.
Approach to              y Example: Born out of the successful 1918 Ahmedabad Mill Strike, the Textile Labour Association
Nonviolence                epitomized non-violent socialism
Emphasis on Social       y Stressed that true independence required both political and socio-economic rights, addressing
and Economic               poverty, unemployment, and exploitation.
Freedom                  y Example: The Congress Socialist Party (founded in 1934) worked within the Congress to push for
                           policies addressing both political and economic rights of workers.
Influence of             Inspired by global socialist movements, especially the Russian Revolution, which shaped strategies
International            and ideology.
Socialism                Example: The Girni Kamgar Union (GKU): Established in 1928 by communist leaders like S.A.
                         Dange, this Bombay-based textile workers' union was heavily influenced by international Marxist
                         labour tactics.
Formation of             Led to the creation of socialist groups and parties that organised workers and peasants.
Socialist                Example: Disillusioned by Gandhian non-violence and inspired by international Marxism, the
Organizations            Hindustan Socialist Republican Association (HSRA) pivoted toward workers' rights.


 Leadership by             Leaders such as Jawaharlal Nehru, S.C. Bose, Jayaprakash Narayan, and Acharya Narendra
 Visionaries               Dev popularised socialist ideals and integrated them into the independence movement.
                           Example: Jayaprakash Narayan’s role in founding the Bihar Socialist Party (1931) and later the
                           Congress Socialist Party (1934).

5. Impact of Workers' Movements in India
                                                        Positive Impacts
 Legal and Policy          Led to the enactment of important labour laws (Minimum Wages Act, Factories Act, and
 Reforms                   Industrial Disputes Act) and compelled the government to focus on workers’ issues through policy
                           committees.
                           Example: The Trade Union Act, 1926, recognised and regulated trade unions in India.
 Improvement in            Secured wage hikes, reduced working hours, introduced shifts, rest days, and leave provisions
 Conditions                in several sectors.
                           Example: Led by Narayan Meghaji Lokhande, Bombay Millhands Association (1890), following a
                           massive rally of 10,000 workers in Bombay successfully secured a weekly holiday on Sundays, a
                           half-hour afternoon recess, and more regular wage payments
 Formation of Trade        Enabled the creation of strong unions, fostering solidarity and collective bargaining among
 Unions                    workers across sectors.
                           Example: Formation of the All India Trade Union Congress (AITUC) in 1920 played a central role
                           in organising labour actions.
 Sector-Specific           Brought significant benefits to workers in organised sectors like railways, textiles, and defence industries,
 Gains                     such as securing legal recognition for Trade Unions, weekly paid holidays, and early safety standards.

                                                       Negative Impacts
 Violence and Unrest       Some movements turned violent, resulting in loss of lives and property damage.
                           Example: The Punnapra-Vayalar uprising (1946) in Kerala led to hundreds of worker deaths due to
                           police firing.
 Neglect of                The focus remained largely on organised-sector workers of urban regions, sidelining the vast
 Unorganised Sector        unorganised workforce of rural areas.

The workers and labour movements played a pivotal role in India’s freedom struggle and nation-building by championing the
rights of the oppressed and mobilising collective action for social and economic justice. As Lala Lajpat Rai aptly stated,
“Imperialism and milita rism are the twin children of capitalism” highlighting how the fight for workers’ rights was inseparably
linked to the broader struggle against colonialism and exploitation.

24.3 Role of Press, Literature & Vernacular Media
The press, literature, and vernacular media during British rule in India emerged as powerful tools for political awakening and
social reform, spreading nationalist ideas and connecting diverse communities across linguistic and regional lines. Despite facing
stringent colonial restrictions like the Vernacular Press Act of 1878, these platforms played a crucial role in mobilising public
opinion against imperial policies and fostering a collective national identity.

1. Historical Evolution of Press and Media
     Phase                                                    Details with Examples
 Early               y India’s press began with the first newspaper in 1780, which criticised British officials and faced
 Beginnings            immediate censorship, setting a precedent for press struggles.
 (1780-1850)         y Early publications catered to the English-speaking elite, focusing on colonial administration and
                       local news.
                     y Repressive laws, like those requiring government scrutiny before printing, restricted growth but laid
                       the foundation for nationalist journalism.
                     y Example: James Augustus Hicky launched Bengal Gazette (also called the Original Calcutta General
                       Advertiser) in 1780 in Calcutta. It was India's and Asia's first newspaper, and it famously fought against
                       East India Company censorship


 Nationalist         y Mid-19th century saw the rise of nationalist newspapers challenging British narratives, advocating
 Press                 self-rule post-1857 revolt.
 (1850-1910)         y Vernacular papers in Marathi and Bengali emerged, expanding reach to rural areas and fostering
                       regional pride.
                     y These publications ignited political consciousness by critiquing colonial policies.
                     y Example: Kesari, Sudharak, Bengalee, etc.
 Early 20th          y The press became a platform for mass mobilisation, reporting on atrocities like the 1919 Jallianwala
 Century               Bagh massacre.
 (1910-1940)         y English and vernacular papers promoted non-violence, constitutional reforms, civil disobedience and
                       social unity.
                     y Gandhi’s publications spread Satyagraha, aligning with movements like Non-Cooperation.
                     y Example: The Hindustan Times (1924) reported nationalist activities and supported constitutional
                       reforms.
 Quit India          y The press was pivotal in 1942, with underground publications circulating anti-colonial messages
 Movement              despite censorship.
 (1940-1947)         y Clandestine pamphlets and radio sustained resistance, rallying support for immediate independence.
                     y These efforts kept the movement alive under severe repression.
                     y Example: 'Twelve-Point Programme' during Quit India.

2. Role of Press, Literature & Vernacular Media during the Freedom Struggle
     Aspect                                   Role                                                 Example
 Dissemination       Informed masses about colonial atrocities, protests,       Example: Kesari, Amrita Bazar Patrika
 of                  and policies; bridged urban-rural gap through              reported the Jallianwala Bagh massacre and
 Information         accessible narratives.                                     civil disobedience events.
 Mobilisation        Stimulated political consciousness; urged                  Example: Young India urged readers to join
 and Mass            participation in movements through editorials and          Gandhian movements like NCM and CDM.
 Awakening           appeals.
 Platform for        Nationalist leaders used newspapers and journals to        Example: Gandhi’s Harijan and Tilak’s Kesari
 Leaders             articulate political ideas and reach diverse               became ideological tools of mass
                     audiences.                                                 communication.
 Vernacular          The vernacular press overcame literacy and class           Example: Swadesamitran (Tamil), Navjivan
 Media’s             barriers, uniting peasants, workers, and regional          (Gujarati), and Bande Mataram (Bengali, edited
 Reach               communities in the national movement.                      by Bipin Chandra Pal) spread nationalist ideas.
 Nationalist         Novels, poems, and essays instilled patriotism and         Example: Bankim Chandra’s Anandamath with
 Literature          criticised imperial rule, evoking emotional                “Vande Mataram” became a national symbol.
                     nationalism and pride in Indian culture.
 Resisting           Despite severe press laws (like Vernacular Press Act,      Example: Usha Mehta’s secret Congress Radio
 Censorship          Press Act 1910), underground literature and coded          (1942) defied wartime censorship during Quit
                     writings kept resistance alive.                            India Movement.
 Fostering           Promoted unity across castes, classes, and regions;        Example: Nationalist press celebrated symbols
 National            constructed a common national narrative that               like Gandhi, Swaraj, Khadi across diverse
 Identity            transcended linguistic and religious lines.                linguistic media.
 Critique of         Exposed British economic exploitation, racial              Example: The Hindu and The Tribune
 Colonial            discrimination, and repressive laws; shaped anti-          editorialised against the Rowlatt Act and Simon
 Policies            colonial public opinion.                                   Commission.
 Support for         Addressed issues like untouchability, child marriage,      Example: Articles in Harijan and Sudharak
 Social Reform       and women’s rights, building the moral and                 supported Gandhi’s anti-untouchability
                     intellectual ground for political reform.                  campaign.

3. Role of Music, Dance, Theatre, and Art in India’s Freedom Struggle
Nationalist songs, theatre, and art played a transformative role in India’s freedom struggle by awakening collective consciousness,
fostering unity, and inspiring resistance against colonial rule. Through revolutionary poetry, stirring songs, impactful plays, and
evocative visual arts, creative expression became a powerful tool to spread nationalist ideas, expose British oppression, and
mobilise people across regions and communities.


  Medium                                                             Description
 Music            y Music unified people, stirred emotions, and spread revolutionary ideas, becoming a powerful tool of
                    protest and patriotism.
                  y Examples: “Vande Mataram” by Bankim Chandra Chattopadhyay became the anthem of the freedom
                    movement; “Jana Gana Mana” by Rabindranath Tagore promoted unity; “Sare Jahan Se Achha” by
                    Muhammad Iqbal inspired pride in Indian heritage.
 Bhakti and       y Devotional songs and folk music in regional languages spread messages of freedom, non-violence, and
 Folk Music         unity, ensuring mass participation.
                  y Example: “Raghupati Raghav Raja Ram” was sung at Gandhi’s prayer meetings, and folk songs were
                    performed during protests and gatherings to sustain morale among freedom fighters.
 Dance            y Classical and folk dances reclaimed cultural identity and resisted colonial suppression, celebrating Indian
                    heritage.
                  y Example: Bharatanatyam was revived by Rukmini Devi Arundale and Uday Shankar used dance to express
                    nationalist themes.
 Folk             y Folk dances incorporated patriotic themes and were performed at gatherings, protests, and festivals to
 Dances             sustain the spirit of resistance.
                  y Example: Tamasha in Maharashtra and Nautanki in North India included social and nationalist themes to
                    mobilize the masses.
 Drama            y Theatre articulated nationalist ideas, exposed colonial exploitation, and inspired collective action.
 and              y Example: Plays like “Kichak Vadh” by Krushnaji Prabhakar Khadilkar and “Andher Nagari” by Bhartendu
 Theatre            Harishchandra criticised British rule.
 Street Play      y Street plays and folk theatre forms reached the common people, especially in rural areas, making anti-
                    colonial messages accessible and engaging.
                  y Example: "Nabanna" (Harvest) is a landmark 1944 Bengali drama written by playwright Bijon
                    Bhattacharya that vividly depicts the horrors of the man-made 1943 Great Bengal Famine.
 Festivals        y Traditional festivals and public gatherings were creatively used to spread nationalist ideas and mobilise
 and Melas          communities.
                  y Example: Bal Gangadhar Tilak popularised Ganapati and Shivaji festivals in Maharashtra as platforms for
                    Swadeshi propaganda and anti-British mobilisation during the early 20th century.
 Art              y Visual and performing arts, along with literature in regional languages, united people across linguistic
                    divides in the freedom movement.
                  y Example: Bharat Mata paintings by Abanindranath Tagore and folk performances conveyed nationalist
                    messages beyond language barriers.
Nationalist literature, songs, theatre, and art left an enduring legacy by awakening the spirit of resistance, uniting diverse communities,
and embedding the ideals of freedom and justice in the national consciousness. As Rabindranath Tagore envisioned in his iconic
lines, “Where the mind is without fear and the head is held high… Into that heaven of freedom, my Father, let my country
awake,” creative expression became both the voice and the soul of India’s quest for independence.

4. Challenges and Repression Faced
   Challenge                      Description                                                 Examples
 Censorship            British laws imposed strict               The Vernacular Press Act (1878) specifically targeted non-English
 Laws                  controls, requiring pre-publication       (vernacular language) newspapers to suppress criticism of British
                       scrutiny and banning dissent.             policies (like the Second Anglo-Afghan War and famine relief efforts).
 Violence and          Journalists faced arrests, beatings,      Bal Gangadhar Tilak was convicted thrice under Section 124A
 Intimidation          and imprisonment for anti-                (sedition) for Kesari’s anti-colonial editorials, facing transportation
                       colonial reporting, creating fear.        and imprisonment, reflecting the climate of fear for journalists.
 Publication           Colonial authorities banned               Newspapers such as the Amrita Bazar Patrika faced censorship
 Bans                  newspapers critical of British            under the Defence of India Rules during WWII.
                       rule, disrupting information flow.


 Resource            Poverty, illiteracy, and limited       Early newspapers struggled with mass illiteracy and restrictive
 Constraints         printing technology slowed the         regulations, such as pre-publication scrutiny, limiting their reach in
                     press’s growth, especially in rural    rural areas despite growing nationalist fervor.
                     areas.
 Underground         Journalists countered censorship by    During QIM, cyclostyled sheets and illegal weekly news pamphlets
 Resistance          publishing clandestine papers          like Biplabi (published in Midnapore) and Harijan (which
                     and pamphlets, risking                 continued via secret presses) circulated to spread protest
                     persecution.                           timetables.

The vernacular press became a powerful instrument of India's freedom struggle by spreading nationalist ideas, mobilising public
opinion, and fostering national unity. As Mahatma Gandhi said, the true function of the press is to understand and express public
sentiment while shaping it.
subs: 24.1 Role of Women in the Freedom Movement
24.2 Role of Workers and Labour Movements
24.3 Role of Press, Literature & Vernacular Media
pyq: Discuss the role of women in the freedom struggle especially during the Gandhian phase. (2016)
The women's questions arose in modern India as a part of the 19th century social reform movement. What are the major issues and debates concerning women in that period? (2017)
Defying the barriers of age, gender, and religion, the Indian women became the torch- bearer during the struggle for freedom in India. Discuss. (2013)

Historical Evolution of Women’s Participation in the Freedom Movement Phase Details with Examples Early Resistance y Women’s resistance began with regional uprisings, challenging colonialism even before organised (1817-1857) nationalism. Their participation was marked by martial courage and local leadership. y Example: Bhima Bai Holkar played a prominent role during the Third Anglo-Maratha War (1817–18). First War of y Women directly participated in leadership and combat, symbolizing defiance against British Independence oppression. They inspired future generations with their uncompromising bravery. (1857) y Example: Begum Hazrat Mahal fought to defend Awadh, rejecting British terms even in exile. Pre-Gandhian Era y Women began participating in Swadeshi, educational reform, and international advocacy. (1905-1919) Many acted as early nationalists and social reformers. y Example: Madam Bhikaji Cama hoisted the Indian flag in Stuttgart (1907)
Role of Women in the Indian Freedom Movement Role Description Example Leadership in Led protests, addressed public gatherings, Sarojini Naidu led the Salt Satyagraha (1930)

chapters.text:
25                   Nationalists and Freedom
                        Fighters
                                                 UPSC Mains PYQs
    1.     Several foreigners made India their homeland and participated in various movements.
           Analyze their role in the Indian struggle for freedom. (2013)
    2.     Discuss the contribution of Maulana Abul Kalam Azad to pre-and post- independent
           India. (2013)

1. Jawaharlal Nehru (1889-1964)
     Aspect                                                          Contribution
Freedom                  A prominent figure in the Non-Cooperation, Civil Disobedience, and Quit India movements, led
Movement                 the Indian National Congress as President during the 1929 Lahore Session, where the demand for
                         complete independence, or ‘Purna Swaraj’ was boldly declared.
Imprisonment             Endured multiple imprisonments, especially during the 1930s and the 1942 Quit India Movement;
and Writings             wrote influential works like The Discovery of India and An Autobiography while in jail, reflecting on
                         India’s history and his vision for its future.
Nation Building as       Served as India’s first Prime Minister (1947-64); launched the Five-Year Plans and set up the
PM                       Planning Commission to drive economic growth; promoted a mixed economy and self-reliance
                         through import substitution policies.
Foreign Policy           Shaped India’s global stance by co-founding the Non-Aligned Movement and advocating the
Vision                   Panchsheel principles of peaceful coexistence; positioned India as a voice for newly independent
                         nations on the world stage.

2. Subhash Chandra Bose (1897-1945)
  Aspect                                                          Contribution
Congress           Elected Congress President twice (1938 and 1939); stepped down as 1939 INC president due to
Leadership         differences with moderates and founded the Forward Bloc to rally radical nationalists for a more
                   aggressive push toward independence.
Radical            Called for bold, militant action against British rule; established the Rani of Jhansi Regiment, a pioneering
Activism           women’s unit in the Indian National Army (INA), empowering women in the freedom struggle.
Indian             Revived and led the INA (Azad Hind Fauj) in 1943 with Japanese support; spearheaded military
National           campaigns toward Imphal and Kohima; used Azad Hind Radio to inspire resistance, hastening British
Army (INA)         withdrawal.
International      Forged alliances with Axis powers to globalize India’s independence struggle, seeking international
Diplomacy          support to weaken British colonial rule.
3. Bhagat Singh (1907-1931)
   Aspect                                                          Contribution
Revolutionary        Co-founded the Naujawan Bharat Sabha (1926) to mobilise youth and played the foundational role in
Organisation         the formation of the Hindustan Socialist Republican Association (HSRA) in 1928 to plan revolutionary
                     actions against British oppression.
Militant Protest     Avenged Lala Lajpat Rai’s death by assassinating British officer Saunders (1928); threw non-lethal
Actions              bombs in the Central Legislative Assembly (1929) with Batukeshwar Dutt to protest oppressive laws,
                     aiming to awaken the nation.
Hunger Strike        Staged a prolonged hunger strike in jail with other nationalists to demand humane treatment for political
& Trial              prisoners; used his trial as a platform to denounce colonial injustices, inspiring widespread support.
Ideology and         Embraced socialism, secularism, and Marxist ideals; wrote ‘Why I Am an Atheist?’ to articulate his
Legacy               beliefs; popularized the slogan ‘Inquilab Zindabad’ (Long Live Revolution); his execution at 23 ignited
                     youth activism.


4. Bal Gangadhar Tilak (1856-1920)
      Aspect                                                      Contribution
Advocacy for           Boldly advocated Swaraj (self-government) and popularised militant nationalism with his iconic
Swaraj                 slogan, “Swaraj is my birthright and I shall have it", laying the foundation for radical nationalism.
Mass Mobilisation      Transformed Ganesh festivals and Shivaji celebrations into vibrant platforms for uniting people
via Culture            and spreading nationalist ideas across communities.
Education &            Co-founded New English School (1880), Deccan Education Society, and Fergusson College to
Nationalist Press      promote modern education; launched Kesari (Marathi) and Mahratta (English) newspapers to stir
                       patriotic awareness.
Religious-Political    Wrote Shrimadh Bhagavad Gita Rahasya (1915) in Mandalay Jail, interpreting the Gita as a call to
Thought                selfless action (Karmayoga) to inspire political resistance.
Home Rule              Established his own Indian Home Rule League (1916), other than Annie Besant’s, demanding
Movement               self-governance and promoting education in local languages.
Triumvirate            As part of the “Lal-Bal-Pal” trio, drove Swadeshi and boycott movements; helped broker the 1916
Leadership             Lucknow Pact to foster Hindu-Muslim unity for the freedom struggle.

5. Lala Lajpat Rai (1865-1928)
     Aspect                                                       Contribution
Extremist              A key member of the “Lal-Bal-Pal” trio; led Swadeshi campaigns and fiercely opposed the Simon
Leadership             Commission in 1928, rallying people against British policies.
Mass Mobilisation      Declared during the Simon Commission protest, “Every blow aimed at me is a nail in the coffin of
and Principle          British rule” inspiring nationwide resistance against colonial rule.
Social Reform and      Backed Arya Samaj to fight untouchability and casteism; founded National College (Lahore),
Institution            Punjab National Bank and Lakshmi Insurance Company. Founded the Servants of the People
Building               Society in 1921 to enlist and train national missionaries dedicated to the social, educational, and
                       economic upliftment of the country.
Journalism and         Edited The Tribune, Vande Mataram, and Arya Gazette; wrote influential books like Young India
Literature             and Unhappy India to expose colonial exploitation.
Patriotic              Died from injuries sustained in a brutal lathi-charge during a 1928 Lahore protest; his death fueled
Martyrdom              revolutionary zeal, inspiring figures like Bhagat Singh.
Educational and        Established Gulab Devi Chest Hospital (1928) for women and set up educational institutions and
Medical Legacy         scholarships to advance social progress.

6. Bipin Chandra Pal (1858-1932)
     Aspect                                                       Contribution
Swadeshi and          Passionately advocated for complete independence and promoted Swadeshi (use of Indian goods)
Swaraj                through powerful speeches that galvanized public support.
Journalism and        Edited The Democrat & the Independent and founded Bande Mataram (English-language daily
Commentary            newspaper). He used his writings to ignite nationalist fervour and critique British rule.
Social Reform         Fought against caste discrimination, poverty, and inequality; pushed for national education to
                      empower Indians and foster social progress.

7. Sardar Vallabhbhai Patel (1875-1950)
      Aspect                                                       Contribution
Leadership in            Led the Kheda and Bardoli Satyagrahas, showcasing nonviolent resistance; rose as a towering
Freedom Struggle         leader within the Indian National Congress.
Integration of           As India’s first Deputy PM and Home Minister, he skilfully united over 560 princely states
Princely States          into the Indian Union, ensuring national cohesion.
Nation-Building          Managed refugee rehabilitation after Partition; strengthened democratic institutions and
Administration           improved governance in tribal and rural areas.


8. Sarojini Naidu (1879-1949)
     Aspect                                                       Contribution
Political              Became the first Indian woman President of the Indian National Congress (1925); led nonviolent
Leadership             protests, inspiring widespread participation in the freedom movement.
Women's Rights         Fought for women’s voting rights; co-founded the Women’s India Association to advance gender
Advocate               equality within the nationalist cause.
Literary and           Renowned poet, with works like The Golden Threshold, became India’s first female Governor
Governor Role          (Uttar Pradesh), breaking gender barriers in leadership.

9. Maulana Abul Kalam Azad (1888-1958)
      Aspect                                                      Contribution
Freedom Movement         Played a key role in the Khilafat and Non-Cooperation movements; promoted Hindu-Muslim

India's freedom struggle was a confluence of constitutionalism, revolutionary nationalism, socio-religious reform, and mass
mobilisation. Leaders like Ambedkar, Phule, and Periyar linked freedom with social justice, while Bose, Bhagat Singh, and
Azad broadened the nationalist movement. Nehru, Patel, and Azad carried this plural legacy into nation-building, supported
by foreign sympathisers like Besant, Nivedita, and Andrews who gave the cause global moral resonance.
pyq: Several foreigners made India their homeland and participated in various movements. Analyze their role in the Indian struggle for freedom. (2013)
Discuss the contribution of Maulana Abul Kalam Azad to pre-and post- independent India. (2013)
